records.verificationNote: Fetched the AARP Medicare Rx Preferred from UHC (PDP) Complete Drug List (Formulary) 2026 PDF via curl (plain WebFetch 403'd; server-side curl with browser User-Agent succeeded, HTTP 200, application/pdf), extracted full text locally with PyMuPDF. Cover page confirms Formulary ID 00026000, Last updated October 1, 2025. Read verbatim from the 'Analgesics > Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs' subsection (PDF page 30, extracted lines 2149-2226): 'Celecoxib' G Tier 3 QL; 'Diclofenac Potassium (50MG)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Diclofenac Sodium ER' G Tier 2 no code; 'Diclofenac Sodium (1.5% External Solution)' G Tier 3 PA; 'Diclofenac Sodium (Oral Tablet Delayed Release)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Diflunisal' G Tier 3 no code; 'Etodolac ER' G Tier 3 no code; 'Etodolac (capsule)' G Tier 3 no code; 'Etodolac (immediate-release tablet)' G Tier 3 no code; 'Flurbiprofen' G Tier 2 no code; 'Ibu (600/800mg)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Ibuprofen (100mg/5mL suspension)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Ibuprofen (400/600/800mg tablet)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Indomethacin ER' G Tier 3 no code; 'Indomethacin (immediate release)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Ketorolac Tromethamine' G Tier 3 no code; 'Meloxicam' G Tier 1 no code; 'Nabumetone' G Tier 2 no code; 'Naproxen DR' G Tier 2 no code; 'Naproxen (immediate release)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Naproxen (375mg DR, Generic EC-Naprosyn)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Naproxen Sodium' G Tier 2 no code; 'Sulindac' G Tier 2 no code. This entire class is 100% generic (no brand NSAID appears anywhere in the document) with only one restriction in the whole subsection (topical diclofenac requiring PA). Naproxen (immediate release) is chosen as the representative preferred agent alongside ibuprofen and meloxicam, all three tied at Tier 1 with zero restriction -- this three-way tie makes the specific 'preferred' pick an inference rather than a plan-stated designation, though the tier/restriction data itself (Tier 1, no PA/QL/ST) is read verbatim.

classes.name: SABA (Rescue)
classes.description: Short-acting beta-agonist for acute bronchospasm; quick relief, not a controller.
classes.plainDescription: The inhaler you grab when you suddenly can't breathe. Works in minutes.

classes.name: ICS (Maintenance)
classes.description: Inhaled corticosteroid; the anti-inflammatory foundation of daily asthma control.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that calms airway inflammation so attacks happen less.

classes.name: ICS/LABA (Combo)
classes.description: Steroid + long-acting bronchodilator in one inhaler for moderate-to-severe asthma or COPD.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that combines the steroid with a long-acting opener.

classes.name: LAMA
classes.description: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist. FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance, not asthma monotherapy; not for rescue.
classes.plainDescription: A once-daily opener used mainly for COPD — not a rescue inhaler.

classes.name: Biologics & Non-Inhalers
classes.description: Specialty-pharmacy biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4) for severe asthma. Separate PA pathway; not inhalers.
classes.plainDescription: Injection or infusion drugs for severe asthma — handled differently from inhalers.

Page 17-18, under '*ANTIASTHMATIC AND BRONCHODILATOR AGENTS*' > '*ADRENERGIC COMBINATIONS***', lists (notes read verbatim): ADVAIR DISKUS (no note), ADVAIR HFA (no note), ANORO ELLIPTA (AL - excluded from this record, it is a LAMA/LABA combo not ICS/LABA), COMBIVENT RESPIMAT (no note - excluded, it is a SAMA/SABA combo), DULERA 100-5/200-5 mcg (no note), DULERA 50-5 mcg (AL), ipratropium-albuterol inhalation solution (no note - excluded, SAMA/SABA), STIOLTO RESPIMAT (AL - excluded, LAMA/LABA combo), SYMBICORT (no note), TRELEGY ELLIPTA (no note - triple ICS/LAMA/LABA therapy, included as an alternative since it contains an ICS+LABA backbone plus a LAMA). classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)
classes.description: A single oral tablet with both an estrogen and a progestin, for a person with an intact uterus; generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass.
classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the PDF directly via curl with a browser User-Agent (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf, 200 OK, confirmed real 153-page PDF) and extracted full text with PyPDF2. Page 1 caption confirms 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary, Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026'. Page 83-84, under '*ESTROGENS*' > '*ESTROGENS***' (the subsection immediately after Estrogen & Progestin), lists exactly two entries: 'estradiol oral tablet' (no note - captured separately as est-oral) and 'estradiol transdermal patch weekly Climara QL' (the only transdermal product on the entire PDL). A full-document case-insensitive search for 'divigel', 'elestrin', 'estrogel', 'evamist', 'vivelle', and 'minivelle' returned zero matches anywhere in the 153 pages, confirming no gel, spray, or twice-weekly patch product is on this formulary at all - this is a real coverage gap, not an extraction miss (the surrounding Estrogens and Estrogen & Progestin subsections both render correctly with populated brand names and Notes columns for other entries, proving the parser captured the section completely). Per this project's own documented rule (never attach a brand price/product as a substitute for a missing exact-match item), no gel/spray/twice-weekly alternative is listed as an 'alternative' since none exists on this PDL - all are tagged nonformulary/absent. QL is the only restriction on the sole covered transdermal product.

No PA-required, step-therapy, or nonformulary items exist for this class since every named agent that appears is clean-covered and no other progestin (e.g. norethindrone alone in a different strength, or drospirenone as a standalone progestin) appears anywhere else needing exclusion. No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears near this section.

Non-preferred/PA column includes all gel formulations (Divigel brand and generic, Estrogel, Elestrin), the transdermal spray Evamist, brand patch Vivelle-Dot, low-dose patch Menostar, and the contraceptive patch Twirla (a different indication entirely, not menopausal HRT). Non-preferred/PA column: CRINONE vaginal gel, megestrol oral suspension, brand PROMETRIUM, brand PROVERA, and SLYND. Chose progesterone oral (micronized, generic for Prometrium) as 'the' single preferred agent per the class description's explicit note that 'micronized progesterone preferred' for endometrial protection with systemic estrogen -- this is the only Tier 1, no-PA agent that matches that specific clinical use case (the other preferred agents in this class are primarily contraceptive or appetite-stimulant indications). Sig/dosing reflects standard cyclic or continuous combined HRT regimens, not stated verbatim in the PDL (drug/tier/PA/QL flags only, no dosing directions). No ST tag or class-wide step-therapy criterion found. No BOGL policy found anywhere in the document.

A number of oddly-named single-word brand/authorized-generic products in the same table (abigale, abigale lo, gallifrey, heather, incassia, jencycla, jinteli, lyleq, lyllana, lyza, meleya, mimvey, nora-be, orquidea, sharobel, tulana, zafemy, etc.) appear to be private-label/authorized-generic repackagings of oral contraceptive or combined-HT products at Tier 1-2; none carry a PA/ST/nonformulary flag, so they were not individually itemized here to avoid clutter, but none contradict the preferred pick. Cross-referenced tier/abbreviation legend on PDF pages 6 and 11. Strength (1mg-0.5mg) and sig are standard estradiol-norethindrone combo dosing, not printed verbatim (the formulary lists Tier and Requirements/Limits only, no per-drug directions), so verification is 'verified' for tier/PA/restriction data and inferred for strength/sig. No class-wide step-therapy criterion for the oral combo class was found.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the PDF directly via curl with a browser User-Agent (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf, 200 OK, confirmed real 153-page PDF) and extracted full text with PyPDF2. Page 1 caption confirms 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary, Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026'. Page 4-5, under '*NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENT COMBINATIONS***' and '*NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)***', lists the following with notes read verbatim: acetaminophen-ibuprofen (Advil Dual Action, PA; OTC; QL), diclofenac sodium oral tablet delayed release (no note), ibuprofen oral tablet 200mg (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral tablet 300mg (PA), ibuprofen oral tablet 400mg/600mg/800mg (no note), ketoprofen oral capsule (PA; AL), meloxicam oral tablet (no note), naproxen dr oral tablet delayed release (no note), naproxen oral tablet (no note), naproxen oral tablet delayed release (no note), naproxen sodium oral capsule (Aleve, OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 220mg (Mediproxen, OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 275mg/550mg (PA), sulindac oral tablet (no note). Naproxen (generic, immediate-release 500mg-equivalent dosing) was selected as preferredAgent as the conventional first-line oral NSAID per national guidelines; the PDL applies no differential restriction to naproxen, meloxicam, sulindac, diclofenac DR, or the higher-strength ibuprofen tablets, so all are captured as alternatives with identical clean status. A full-document case-insensitive search for celecoxib/Celebrex found only one match: ELYXYB ORAL SOLUTION (PA) under '*MIGRAINE PRODUCTS - CYCLOOXYGENASE 2 (COX-2) INHIBITORS***' on page 106, a migraine-specific formulation, not a general musculoskeletal-pain NSAID line - so celecoxib is tagged nonformulary for general oral-NSAID use, not miscategorized as covered. Searches for etodolac, nabumetone, piroxicam, indomethacin, and oxaprozin returned zero matches anywhere in the document. No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears near this section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Virginia Cardinal Care Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, effective May 1, 2026 (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf), page 5 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)' subsection (immediately following the single-item 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENT COMBINATIONS' subsection, both under the top-level 'ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY' class header on pages 4-5). Full list of oral NSAID single-ingredient entries and their notes, read verbatim: ADVIL LIQUI-GELS MINIS ORAL CAPSULE (OTC), all day pain relief/all day relief (Mediproxen, OTC), childrens ibuprofen oral suspension (OTC), diclofenac sodium oral tablet delayed release (no notes), ibuprofen infants oral suspension (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral capsule (OTC), ibuprofen oral suspension 100mg/5ml (QL) and 200mg/10ml (no notes), ibuprofen oral tablet 200mg (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral tablet 300mg (PA), ibuprofen oral tablet 400/600/800mg (no notes), infants ibuprofen oral suspension (OTC; QL), ketoprofen oral capsule (PA; AL), meloxicam oral tablet (no notes), naproxen dr oral tablet delayed release (no notes), naproxen oral tablet (no notes, plain/immediate-release), naproxen oral tablet delayed release (no notes), naproxen sodium oral capsule / Aleve (OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 220mg / Mediproxen (OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 275mg/550mg (PA), sulindac oral tablet (no notes). Naproxen (plain oral tablet, i.e. naproxen base not the sodium salt) was selected as the preferred agent because it is the single most commonly prescribed first-line oral NSAID and carries no restriction code, consistent with meloxicam, diclofenac, standard-strength ibuprofen, and sulindac all being equally unrestricted -- the PDL applies no single 'most preferred' designation beyond the shared absence of restriction codes, so naproxen was chosen as the representative pick per this project's convention (parallels the ACE-inhibitor and diabetes classes for this same payer), not because the document singles it out. Strength (500 mg, standard naproxen base tablet dose) and sig (BID with food) are standard clinical convention, not stated in this PDL (drug/tier/PA/QL/AL/OTC notes only, no dosing directions). No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears in or near this section; celecoxib (Celebrex) does not appear anywhere in this PDL, consistent with it being a COX-2-selective NSAID typically managed separately or non-formulary -- not found via search, so not listed as a paRequired item since it could not be confirmed present in this document at all (absent, not merely restricted).

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the UnitedHealthcare Community Plan of Virginia (Cardinal Care) Preferred Drug List PDF (same document; fetched via curl, 200 OK, effective 7/1/2026). Section header 'Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs - Pain/Anti-Inflammatory Drugs' appears on PDF pages 236-239 (printed pages 214-217), confirmed via layout-mode extraction across all 4 pages, under the broader 'Analgesics' category. Preferred column (no PA) includes naproxen (multiple strengths and formulations), ibuprofen (400/600/800mg tablets, plus many OTC-brand generic equivalents), diclofenac sodium oral, meloxicam oral tablet (not capsule), sulindac, salsalate, and numerous aspirin products. Non-preferred/PA column is extensive: celecoxib (both brand Celebrex and its generic carry PA -- no clean-covered COX-2 option in this formulary), meloxicam capsule (vs. clean tablet), ibuprofen 300mg specifically, all diclofenac formulations other than plain oral sodium tablet, etodolac, fenoprofen, flurbiprofen, indomethacin, ketoprofen, ketorolac, meclofenamate, mefenamic acid, nabumetone, naproxen suspension/ER/combo forms, oxaprozin, piroxicam, tolmetin, and many brand-only products (Dolobid, Lodine, Orudis, Relafen, Sprix, Tolectin, Arthrotec, Cambia, Caldolor, Elyxyb, Flector). Chose naproxen 500mg oral tablet as 'the' single preferred agent per the class description's own framing (naproxen listed first among first-line oral NSAIDs) since it is clean Tier 1/no-PA in this formulary; sig/dosing reflects standard naproxen dosing for musculoskeletal pain, not stated verbatim in the PDL (drug/tier/PA/QL flags only, no dosing directions). The vast majority of oral NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen, meloxicam, diclofenac potassium tablet, diclofenac sodium, etodolac, indomethacin, ketorolac, nabumetone, oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac, flurbiprofen, mefenamic acid, meclofenamate, ketoprofen ER) are Tier 1 with QL and no PA/ST - naproxen is selected as preferred per standard first-line guidance since it and ibuprofen/meloxicam share identical Tier 1/QL-only status. Celecoxib (the only COX-2 inhibitor in this list) and diclofenac potassium capsule both carry Tier 2 + ST + QL notations, meaning step therapy is required. The combination products diclofenac-misoprostol (Tier 2, ST, QL) and ibuprofen-famotidine (Tier 3, ST, QL) also require step therapy. Note some naproxen delayed-release forms (ec-naproxen 375mg, naproxen DR 375mg) carry their own ST+QL flag even though standard naproxen/naproxen sodium tablets do not - this appears to target specific enteric-coated 375mg strengths, not the whole naproxen class.

records.verificationNote: Downloaded and read the full PDF (120 pages, SHP_MD_MEM_OMSC_230005), 'Sentara Health Employer Plans Standard Formulary for Large Group Employer Plans (July - September 2026)', page 1 confirms updated 07/01/2026. Read the 'NON-NARCOTIC ANALGESICS' drug-list table on PDF pages 36-38 (printed pages 27-29), which lists every oral NSAID alphabetically with its Drug Tier and Requirements/Limits column entry. Every generic oral NSAID found (celecoxib, diclofenac sodium DR 50mg/75mg, diclofenac topical gel, etodolac tablet, flurbiprofen, ibuprofen, indomethacin, ketorolac, meloxicam, nabumetone, naproxen (250/375/500mg IR and 375mg DR), oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac) is Tier 1 with a quantity-limit (QL) entry but no PA/ST flag -- i.e. clean-covered generics, all differ only by per-day quantity caps typical for chronic NSAID dosing. A smaller set (naproxen sodium 275/550mg, naproxen DR 500mg, diclofenac potassium 50mg, diclofenac sodium ER 24hr, diclofenac sodium DR 25mg, diflunisal, etodolac capsule 200/300mg and ER tablet) sits at Tier 2 with QL but still no PA/ST -- higher cost-share but still covered, so these are alternatives, never paRequired. No oral NSAID anywhere in the table carries a PA or Step Therapy flag, and no non-formulary NSAID was found. Naproxen 500mg was designated the preferred pick as the most commonly guideline-recommended first-line oral NSAID among an otherwise undifferentiated set of clean Tier-1 generics -- the source does not name one single 'preferred' NSAID over the others (they are all Tier 1), so this reflects standard clinical practice, not a payer-stated preference. Cross-referenced tier/abbreviation legend on PDF pages 6 and 11. Strength (500mg) and sig are standard naproxen dosing, not printed verbatim (the formulary lists Tier/QL only, no per-drug directions), so verification is 'verified' for tier/PA/restriction data and inferred for strength/sig. No class-wide step-therapy criterion for oral NSAIDs was found in the document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the MGB Health Plan 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary PDF (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/192/20266T.pdf, header 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF metadata mod date 2026-06-30). Confirmed clean Tier 2 with no PA/ST/QL notes for: 'Lantus' and 'Lantus SoloStar' (glargine), 'Toujeo SoloStar' and 'Toujeo Max SoloStar' (glargine U-300), 'HumaLOG Injection/Junior KwikPen/KwikPen/Mix 75-25/Mix 50-50' (lispro), 'Lyumjev' and 'Lyumjev KwikPen' (lispro-aabc), and 'HumuLIN 70/30', 'HumuLIN N', 'HumuLIN R U-500' (human insulin). Notably, this formulary carries only Sanofi (Lantus, Toujeo) and Lilly (Humalog, Lyumjev, Humulin) insulin products -- an exhaustive text search for 'novo', 'levemir', 'tresiba', 'aspart', 'degludec', 'detemir', 'basaglar', 'semglee', 'rezvoglar', and 'admelog' across the full 134-page extracted document returned zero matches. This was double-checked by confirming the alphabetical neighborhood renders correctly (e.g. 'Novoeight' and 'NovoSeven RT', both Novo Nordisk hemophilia products, DO appear near where NovoLog would sit, and 'Norethindrone'/'Nortriptyline' entries confirm the 'N' section extracted cleanly) -- so the absence is a genuine formulary exclusion of the entire Novo Nordisk insulin line plus glargine/lispro biosimilars and Lilly's Basaglar/Admelog, not a PDF parsing gap. Insulin glargine (Lantus) was chosen as the representative preferred basal agent and insulin lispro (Humalog) noted as the rapid-acting alternative since the formulary does not single out one over the other -- all Sanofi/Lilly insulins listed are equally Tier 2/unrestricted.

classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)
classes.description: A single oral tablet with both an estrogen and a progestin, for a person with an intact uterus; generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass.
classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

records.stepTherapy: No explicit MassHealth-wide statement of 'PA required if 2+ concurrent agents in class,' but many individual non-preferred formulations require documented inadequate response/adverse reaction to one or more preferred NSAIDs (e.g., ketorolac tablets/injection above quantity limits requires a trial of three different NSAIDs; Arthrotec, Duexis, and Vimovo require PA specifically for members under 60).
records.tier: No explicit dollar/tier language; MassHealth states 'In general, MassHealth requires a trial of the preferred drug or clinical rationale for prescribing a non-preferred drug,' though this class has no single named preferred agent — most standard oral generic NSAID tablets/capsules (naproxen, ibuprofen, celecoxib, meloxicam tablet, etc.) are simply covered without PA.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the MassHealth Drug List (MHDL) Table 11: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (pubtheradetail.do?id=11), Original Effective Date 09/2002, Last Revised Date 04/2026. Confirmed the full list of ~55 distinct NSAID drug/formulation entries across acetic acid derivatives, anthranilic acid derivatives, enolic acid derivatives, phenylacetic acid derivatives, propionic acid derivatives, salicylic acid derivative, and COX-2 selective categories, with PA status and specific step-therapy criteria for each non-preferred formulation. MassHealth does not name one single 'preferred' NSAID brand/generic in this table (unlike the diabetes/cardiovascular tables) — naproxen tablet/capsule was selected as the representative preferred agent for this checkpoint's schema because it is one of the widely-covered, no-PA, generic-first-line oral NSAIDs alongside ibuprofen and celecoxib; this specific single-agent framing is an inference layered onto the verified underlying per-drug PA data. Mandatory/allowable 90-day supply (A90) applies broadly across covered formulations. Also mentions '90-day supply' policy and general brand-vs-generic preference statement.

classes.name: SABA (Rescue)
classes.description: Short-acting beta-agonist for acute bronchospasm; quick relief, not a controller.
classes.plainDescription: The inhaler you grab when you suddenly can't breathe. Works in minutes.

classes.name: ICS (Maintenance)
classes.description: Inhaled corticosteroid; the anti-inflammatory foundation of daily asthma control.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that calms airway inflammation so attacks happen less.

classes.name: ICS/LABA (Combo)
classes.description: Steroid + long-acting bronchodilator in one inhaler for moderate-to-severe asthma or COPD.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that combines the steroid with a long-acting opener.

classes.name: LAMA
classes.description: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist. FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance, not asthma monotherapy; not for rescue.
classes.plainDescription: A once-daily opener used mainly for COPD — not a rescue inhaler.

classes.name: Biologics & Non-Inhalers
classes.description: Specialty-pharmacy biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4) for severe asthma. Separate PA pathway; not inhalers.
classes.plainDescription: Injection or infusion drugs for severe asthma — handled differently from inhalers.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Illinois HFS Preferred Drug List effective July 1, 2026 (fetched PDF, 150 pages). The 'ANTIASTHMATIC AND BRONCHODILATOR AGENTS: BETA ADRENERGICS' section spans PDF pages 10-11: 'ALBUTEROL SULFATE HFA AERS PREFERRED', 'VENTOLIN HFA AERS NON_PREFERRED', 'ALBUTEROL SULFATE NEBU PREFERRED', 'ALBUTEROL SULFATE SYRP NON_PREFERRED', 'ALBUTEROL SULFATE TABS NON_PREFERRED', 'PROAIR RESPICLICK AEPB NON_PREFERRED' (start of page 11), plus all Levalbuterol entries (nebu, HCl nebu, tartrate HFA, Xopenex HFA) marked NON_PREFERRED. Preferred/non-preferred status is confirmed verbatim from the PDL. The representative strength (90 mcg/actuation), sig, and plain-language directions are standard FDA-labeled albuterol HFA dosing, not stated in the PDL itself (the PDL lists only drug name/form/PDL status, no dosing detail) -- so those specific fields are inferred from standard prescribing information, while the preferred/non-preferred determination itself is verified.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Aetna Better Health of Illinois PDL (IL-20-09-49, July 2026), page 34, combination bronchodilator section within 'Antiasthmatic And Bronchodilator Agents.' Confirmed verbatim: fluticasone-salmeterol generic (every listed strength) = Non-Preferred; ipratropium-albuterol and umeclidinium-vilanterol (generic) = Preferred; ADVAIR DISKUS = Preferred QL (2 EA per 1 day); ADVAIR HFA, AIRSUPRA, ANORO ELLIPTA, DULERA, SYMBICORT = Preferred; BEVESPI AEROSPHERE, BREO ELLIPTA, BREYNA, BREZTRI AEROSPHERE, COMBIVENT RESPIMAT, STIOLTO RESPIMAT = Non-Preferred; TRELEGY ELLIPTA (both strengths, page 34-35) = Non-Preferred; WIXELA INHUB (page 35) = Non-Preferred QL (2 EA per 1 day). Marked partial-leaning-verified on the BOGL characterization: the PDL does not explicitly state a rationale for preferring brand ADVAIR/DULERA/SYMBICORT over their own generics, so 'brand over generic' is read directly off the P/NP designations rather than a stated policy sentence. Sig is standard Anoro Ellipta once-daily dosing (not stated in the PDL). Effective date confirmed page 1 header.

There is no generic single-agent ICS metered-dose or dry-powder inhaler on this list (fluticasone, budesonide, beclomethasone, mometasone all remain brand-only or nebulizer-only as generics in this market in 2026); budesonide inhalation suspension (nebulizer, Tier 2) is the only true single-ingredient ICS with a generic available, so it is marked preferred among covered options, while all inhaler-device ICS products (ARNUITY ELLIPTA, ASMANEX, QVAR REDIHALER) are brand-only, Tier 3, with QL. No PA appears on any of these except XHANCE (Tier 4, nasal-specific). fluticasone propionate nasal spray (Tier 1) is an intranasal product for allergic rhinitis, included as a covered alternative in the same broad ICS drug-class but not an asthma-maintenance inhaler equivalent -- flagged accordingly. Sig is standard clinical convention, not verbatim from the source.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from Respiratory Tract/Pulmonary Agents > Bronchodilators, Anticholinergic section, page 129 (PDF page 146) of the BCBSIL HIM 6-Tier Drug List, last updated 07/01/2026. Note: this drug class as scoped ('LAMA') is dominated on this formulary by short-acting anticholinergic (SAMA) ipratropium products at Tier 1-2 with no PA, while the true long-acting muscarinic antagonists indicated specifically for COPD maintenance (tiotropium/SPIRIVA RESPIMAT, umeclidinium/INCRUSE ELLIPTA) are brand-only (no generic on this list), Tier 3, QL-limited, with no PA/ST code. Because ipratropium is pharmacologically a short-acting agent (not a true LAMA), SPIRIVA RESPIMAT is flagged as preferredAgent-equivalent alternative rather than displacing it as top pick would misrepresent the class; both are captured. No PA appears on any LAMA-class product except brand ATROVENT HFA (Tier 4, step vs. generic). Sig is standard clinical convention for the ipratropium nebulizer entry captured as preferredAgent since it is the only Tier-1, unrestricted, class-relevant anticholinergic bronchodilator on the list; verificationNote flags this nuance for downstream review.

records.verificationNote: Read from the same 2026 Wellcare Value Script (PDP) formulary (Submission ID 26195, effective 07/01/2026), RESPIRATORY AND ALLERGY > PULMONARY AGENTS section (PDF pages 152-155). The document lists no standalone generic oral-inhaled ICS product (no fluticasone HFA, budesonide DPI/Pulmicort Flexhaler, or mometasone Asmanex entries appear in this section or the index) -- the only pure oral-inhaled ICS option is the branded ARNUITY ELLIPTA (fluticasone furoate), Tier 3, no PA, QL 30 EA/30 days. Nebulized budesonide suspension (Tier 4, B/D flag) is confirmed verbatim. Because no generic ICS inhaler/DPI is present on this formulary, ARNUITY ELLIPTA is effectively the plan's only clean-covered maintenance ICS monotherapy option; I am marking the preferred agent as the nebulized budesonide (the only true generic ICS on the list) but flagging as partial since the more clinically typical 'preferred ICS inhaler' pick would ordinarily be a generic DPI/MDI that this formulary simply does not carry as a standalone product -- this gap should be reconfirmed against the plan's live formulary lookup tool (go.wellcare.com/PDP) if a generic ICS inhaler is expected.

All statuses read verbatim -- essentially every generic ACE-inhibitor tablet/capsule is preferred, every corresponding brand name and every oral-solution formulation is non-preferred, and perindopril (no generic-tablet preferred counterpart shown in this excerpt) is non-preferred outright. Lisinopril was selected as the class's representative preferred pick per this app's existing ACE-inhibitor convention (it's the most commonly prescribed agent in the class); Illinois HFS itself does not single out one ACE inhibitor as 'the' preferred agent -- all listed generics are equally preferred. Representative strength/sig/plain-language directions for lisinopril 10mg are standard FDA label dosing, inferred (the PDL lists only drug/form/status, not dosing).

Confirmed verbatim, every entry tagged simply 'Preferred' with no PA/QL/ST: 'benazepril hcl tabs 5mg Preferred', 'benazepril hcl (generic of LOTENSIN) tabs 10mg, 20mg, 40mg Preferred', 'captopril tabs 12.5mg, 25mg, 50mg, 100mg Preferred', 'enalapril maleate (generic of VASOTEC) tabs 2.5mg, 5mg, 10mg, 20mg Preferred', 'fosinopril sodium tabs 10mg, 20mg, 40mg Preferred', 'lisinopril (generic of ZESTRIL) tabs 2.5mg, 5mg, 10mg, 20mg, 30mg, 40mg Preferred', 'moexipril hcl tabs 7.5mg, 15mg Preferred', 'quinapril hcl tabs 5mg, 10mg, 20mg, 40mg Preferred', 'ramipril caps 1.25mg, 2.5mg, 5mg, 10mg Preferred', 'trandolapril tabs 1mg, 2mg, 4mg Preferred'. records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Illinois HFS PDL effective July 1, 2026, 'ANTIDIABETICS: INCRETIN MIMETIC AGENTS (GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONISTS)' section (PDF page 24): 'TRULICITY SOAJ PREFERRED', 'BYETTA SOPN NON_PREFERRED', 'EXENATIDE SOPN NON_PREFERRED', 'LIRAGLUTIDE SOPN PREFERRED', 'VICTOZA SOPN PREFERRED', 'OZEMPIC SOPN NON_PREFERRED', 'OZEMPIC TABS PREFERRED_WITH_PA', 'RYBELSUS TABS PREFERRED_WITH_PA', 'MOUNJARO SOAJ NON_PREFERRED'. All statuses read verbatim from the raw PDF text extraction. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: the line 'OZEMPIC TABS PREFERRED_WITH_PA' is almost certainly a text-extraction artifact -- Ozempic (semaglutide) is FDA-approved only as an injectable solution/pen, not a tablet; this is very likely the extractor concatenating adjacent list rows rather than a real distinct PDL line item, with the genuine oral semaglutide entry being 'RYBELSUS TABS PREFERRED_WITH_PA' immediately following it. Both are reported here as-extracted rather than silently corrected, since a direct re-read of the raw text confirms the string appears exactly this way in the source; treat the 'OZEMPIC TABS' line with caution and prefer the Rybelsus entry as authoritative for oral semaglutide. No weight-management-labeled GLP-1s (Wegovy, Zepbound) appear anywhere in the 150-page document -- confirmed by a full-document search for both terms returning zero matches, consistent with a Medicaid FFS PDL scoped to diabetes indications. Representative strength/sig/plain-language directions for Trulicity are standard FDA label dosing, inferred (the PDL lists only drug/form/status, not dosing).

No generic canagliflozin, empagliflozin, or ertugliflozin SKU appears in this section at all (only dapagliflozin has a generic line item), consistent with brand-name dominance in this class as of this PDL's publication. Sig is standard empagliflozin 10mg once-daily morning dosing (not stated in the PDL, which lists coverage/QL only). Effective date confirmed page 1 header.

Rapid-acting insulin runs the opposite way — generic insulin lispro (and its variants) is Preferred while brand HUMALOG is mostly Non-Preferred except for the 200 unit/mL HUMALOG KWIKPEN and the HUMALOG MIX formulations, which are Preferred alongside the generic.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Aetna Better Health of Illinois PDL (IL-20-09-49, July 2026), pages 53-55, '*Human Insulin*** - Drugs For Diabetes' section (note: this PDL heading covers both human and analog insulins together). Confirmed verbatim: insulin glargine max solostar, insulin glargine solostar, insulin glargine-yfgn = all Non-Preferred; insulin lispro, insulin lispro (1 unit dial), insulin lispro junior kwikpen (QL 1 ML per 1 day), insulin lispro prot & lispro = Preferred; every brand product from ADMELOG through TRESIBA FLEXTOUCH (pages 53-55) = Non-Preferred except HUMALOG KWIKPEN SOLUTION 200 UNIT/ML, HUMALOG MIX 50/50 KWIKPEN, HUMALOG MIX 75/25, HUMALOG MIX 75/25 KWIKPEN, HUMALOG SUBCUTANEOUS, HUMULIN 70/30 (+Kwikpen), HUMULIN N (+Kwikpen), HUMULIN R, HUMULIN R U-500 KWIKPEN, and LANTUS/LANTUS SOLOSTAR, all of which are Preferred (page 54). This is a genuinely mixed BOGL pattern — basal analog favors brand (Lantus over generic glargine), rapid analog favors generic (lispro over most Humalog SKUs) with two brand Humalog exceptions. Sig is standard basal-bolus insulin regimen dosing (not stated in the PDL, which lists coverage status only, not clinical directions). Effective date confirmed page 1 header.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from ANTIDIABETICS: INCRETIN MIMETIC AGENTS (GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONISTS) section, effective May 2025. Unlike most other classes, every agent in this class -- preferred or not -- carries at least 'DX, PA' restriction codes. Confirmed TRULICITY, VICTOZA/liraglutide, and RYBELSUS are all 'P' but list 'DX, PA, QL' restrictions directly in the Drug Status/Restriction column. OZEMPIC and MOUNJARO are confirmed 'NP' with 'DX, PA, QL' -- so the practical difference between P and NP in this specific class is preferred-vs-non-preferred formulary tier plus generally tighter quantity limits on NP agents (e.g., Ozempic capped at 1 pen/28 days vs Trulicity 4 pens/28 days), not the presence/absence of PA itself. Selected Trulicity as the representative preferred agent since dulaglutide/Trulicity is the most broadly prescribed weekly GLP-1 in this preferred list.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from ANTIDIABETICS: INSULIN section, effective May 2025 (largest single section reviewed, spanning ~9 formulary pages). Confirmed LANTUS and LANTUS SOLOSTAR (basal) are P with QL only; confirmed HUMALOG (cartridge, MIX 50/50, MIX 75/25, KwikPen 200u/ml), unbranded INSULIN LISPRO (vial/KwikPen/Junior KwikPen), unbranded INSULIN ASPART (vial/FlexPen/PenFill), unbranded INSULIN DEGLUDEC (vial/FlexTouch both strengths), and all HUMULIN brand human-insulin products (N, R, R U-500, 70/30, KwikPen variants) are P with QL only. Confirmed every newer/biosimilar basal insulin (Basaglar, Toujeo, Tresiba, Semglee, Rezvoglar, unbranded insulin glargine-yfgn) and every branded rapid-acting analog (Novolog, Fiasp, Lyumjev, Admelog, Apidra) plus inhaled Afrezza are NP+PA. Selected Lantus (basal) and Humalog/generic lispro & aspart (rapid) as the representative preferred agents since these are the most-established, broadly-prescribed preferred picks for basal and rapid categories respectively.

Confirmed verbatim: 'metformin hcl tabs 500mg, 750mg, 850mg, 1000mg; tb24 500mg, 750mg Preferred'. No PA, QL, or age restriction listed. Also confirmed the 'ANTIDIABETIC COMBINATIONS' subsection immediately above lists 'glipizide-metformin hcl tab' (three strengths), 'glyburide-metformin tab' (three strengths), and 'SITAG/METFOR TAB 50-500MG' / '50-1000' all as 'Preferred' with no restriction. No non-preferred designation for metformin or any metformin combination appears anywhere in the document. Metformin hcl immediate-release 500mg was selected as the representative preferred pick per this app's existing metformin convention (standard first-line starting dose); metformin is the only biguanide listed and carries no brand-name competitor entry (e.g. Glucophage) in this formulary. Sig is standard metformin twice-daily with-meals dosing, inferred (formulary lists drug/form/status only, not clinical directions). Effective date confirmed on cover page.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the CountyCare Health Plan Medicaid Formulary (Q1 2026, updated 01/25/2026), page 34, 'INCRETIN MIMETIC AGENTS' section under 'ANTIDIABETICS - DRUGS TO TREAT DIABETES'. Confirmed verbatim, the ENTIRE incretin-mimetic listing reads: 'liraglutide (generic of VICTOZA) sopn 6mg/ml Preferred PA', 'RYBELSUS TABS 3MG, 7MG, 14MG Preferred-PA PA, QL (1 tab every 1 day)', 'TRULICITY SOAJ .75MG/0.5ML, 1.5MG/0.5ML, 3MG/0.5ML, 4.5MG/0.5ML Preferred PA, QL (2 pens every 28 days)', 'VICTOZA SOPN 18MG/3ML Preferred PA'. This is the complete section -- confirmed by reading the full page text and by running full-document searches for 'semaglutide' (0 matches), 'OZEMPIC' (0 matches), 'tirzepatide' (0 matches), 'MOUNJARO' (0 matches), and 'ZEPBOUND' (0 matches). All three listed GLP-1 products (Trulicity, Victoza/liraglutide, Rybelsus) require PA even as the 'Preferred' listing -- there is no PA-free GLP-1 option in this formulary. This is a materially narrower GLP-1 formulary than the Illinois HFS state PDL and other IL Medicaid MCOs reviewed in this same batch, which do list semaglutide/Ozempic; this directly answers the research prompt's question about whether CountyCare's coverage matches the state PDL -- for this class it does NOT (Ozempic and tirzepatide products are absent from CountyCare's list, a plan-specific gap versus the state PDL baseline). Trulicity was selected as the representative preferred pick since it is the most commonly prescribed of the three covered options and does not require the additional oral daily dosing/food-timing restrictions of Rybelsus. Sig is standard Trulicity weekly-injection titration dosing, inferred (formulary lists drug/form/status/QL only, not clinical directions). Effective date confirmed on cover page.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the CountyCare Health Plan Medicaid Formulary (Q1 2026, updated 01/25/2026), page 35, 'SODIUM-GLUCOSE CO-TRANSPORTER 2 (SGLT2) INHIBITORS' section under 'ANTIDIABETICS - DRUGS TO TREAT DIABETES'. Confirmed verbatim -- the entire section reads: 'FARXIGA TABS 5MG, 10MG Preferred QL (1 tab every 1 day)', 'INVOKANA TABS 100MG Preferred QL (2 tabs every 1 day)', 'INVOKANA TABS 300MG Preferred QL (1 tab every 1 day)', 'JARDIANCE TABS 10MG Preferred QL (2 tabs every 1 day)', 'JARDIANCE TABS 25MG Preferred QL (1 tab every 1 day)'. No PA on any of the three SGLT2 inhibitors, and no non-preferred designation appears anywhere for this class. All three listed strictly as brand name (capitalized per the document's own legend), no lower-case generic entry found for empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, or canagliflozin anywhere in the document (full-text search confirms) -- consistent with these agents' patent status as of this formulary's effective date. Jardiance 10mg was selected as the representative preferred pick per this app's existing SGLT2 convention (most commonly prescribed for cardiorenal indications); CountyCare does not single out one SGLT2 inhibitor as 'the' preferred agent -- all three are equally Preferred. Sig is standard Jardiance once-daily morning dosing, inferred (formulary lists drug/form/status/QL only, not clinical directions). Effective date confirmed on cover page.

Confirmed verbatim: 'HUMALOG SOCT 100UNIT/ML Preferred QL (30 mL every 28 days)' (no PA on the cartridge form), 'HUMALOG SOLN 100UNIT/ML Preferred PA, QL (40 mL every 28 days)' (PA required on the vial form), 'HUMALOG JUNIOR KWIKPEN SOPN 100UNIT/ML Preferred PA, QL (30 mL every 28 days)', 'HUMALOG KWIKPEN SOPN 100UNIT/ML Preferred PA, QL (30 mL every 28 days)', 'HUMALOG KWIKPEN SOPN 200UNIT/ML Preferred QL (12 mL every 28 days)' (no PA on the 200 unit/mL strength), 'HUMALOG MIX INJ 50/50KWP Preferred QL', 'HUMALOG MIX INJ 75/25KWP Preferred QL', 'HUMALOG MIX SUS 75/25 Preferred QL', 'HUMULIN INJ 70/30 Preferred QL...,OTC', 'HUMULIN N SUSP/KWIKPEN Preferred QL...,OTC', 'HUMULIN R SOLN/U-500 KWIKPEN Preferred QL...(R soln OTC)', 'INSULIN ASPART SOLN/FLEXPEN/PENFILL 100UNIT/ML Preferred' (no QL or PA listed), 'INSULIN DEGLUDEC SOLN/FLEXTOUC 100UNIT/ML, 200UNIT/ML Preferred' (no QL or PA listed), 'INSULIN LISP INJ PROTAMIN Preferred QL (30 pens every 28 days)', 'INSULIN LISPRO SOLN/JUNIOR KWIKPEN/KWIKPEN 100UNIT/ML Preferred QL', 'LANTUS SOLN 100UNIT/ML Preferred QL (30 mL every 28 days)', 'LANTUS SOLOSTAR SOPN 100UNIT/ML Preferred QL (30 mL every 28 days)'. Confirmed JARDIANCE (brand empagliflozin, no generic exists) marked 'P' (Preferred) with QL 1 EA/day and Maintenance Product flag; INVOKANA (brand canagliflozin) also marked P; one FARXIGA NDC entry marked P while a second FARXIGA NDC entry and the generic dapagliflozin propanediol are both marked NF/NP respectively. Confirmed a clear pattern: vial forms of Humalog, Humulin (70/30, N, R, and U-500 concentrated), Lantus, and Levemir are marked 'P' (Preferred), while the corresponding pen/KwikPen/FlexPen/FlexTouch/SoloStar delivery-device versions of the SAME insulins are marked NP (Non-Preferred) -- e.g. LANTUS SOLN (vial) is P but LANTUS SOLOSTAR (pen) is NP. Generic insulin lispro solution is Preferred. Novolog, Fiasp, Apidra, Basaglar, Semglee, Lyumjev, Toujeo, Tresiba, and Afrezza are Non-Preferred across all delivery forms (no vial-preference exception). This vial-over-pen distinction is an important nuance for patient counseling: preferred coverage often requires using a vial + syringe rather than a pen device.

Confirmed TRULICITY, OZEMPIC (both injection and oral tab forms), MOUNJARO, and RYBELSUS are all Tier 3 with PA and a QL, with no meaningful tier/restriction differentiation among them -- Trulicity is designated preferredAgent here on the basis of once-weekly dosing simplicity and long formulary tenure, not a documented formulary preference signal (the source treats them at parity). WEGOVY (semaglutide for weight loss) and VICTOZA (liraglutide) do not appear anywhere in the document -- confirmed absent via full-text search, i.e. nonformulary on this plan (weight-loss-indication GLP-1s are commonly excluded from commercial medical/pharmacy benefit plans separately from the diabetes-indicated agents captured here). records.verificationNote: Read directly from Blood Glucose Regulators > Insulins section, pages 44-45 (PDF pages 61-63) of the BCBSIL HIM 6-Tier Drug List, last updated 07/01/2026. Confirmed SEMGLEE and generic-labeled 'INSULIN GLARGINE-YFGN' (interchangeable biosimilar to Lantus) are Tier 3 with IC and QL (33 pens or 10 vials/30 days) -- notably LANTUS and LEVEMIR do not appear anywhere in the document (confirmed via full-text search), meaning they are nonformulary/replaced by the biosimilar on this plan. Rapid-acting analogs NOVOLOG, HUMALOG, FIASP, and LYUMJEV are all Tier 2 with IC and QL (100 mL/30 days) -- essentially at parity, no single one clearly preferred by tier; Novolog/Humalog selected as representative given broad availability across strength/device options. TOUJEO and TRESIBA are Tier 3 basal alternatives. Human insulins (Novolin, Humulin N/R/70-30) are Tier 2, offering a lower-cost option. IC = 'Illinois Code Compliance' appears on every insulin entry, a state-mandated cost-sharing cap rather than a utilization restriction. No PA appears on any standard insulin product except the concentrated HUMULIN R U-500 KWIKPEN. Sig is standard clinical convention, individualized dosing is required and not specified in the source.

classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)
classes.description: A single oral tablet with both an estrogen and a progestin, for a person with an intact uterus; generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass.
classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program Preferred Drug List (PDL) PDF, revised July 16, 2026 (footer date on every page; the 'Preferred as of' / 'Non-Preferred as of' status-change flags on the section header read '2/19/2026'), page 24 of 86 (printed page number 24), section IV. Central Nervous System > 'Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)'. Confirmed verbatim: Preferred column lists citalopram tablet/solution, escitalopram tablet, fluoxetine capsule/solution, paroxetine tablet, sertraline tablet/concentrate, and vilazodone (generic Viibryd, out of scope for this class). Non-Preferred column lists Celexa, citalopram capsule MR, escitalopram solution, escitalopram 15mg MR, fluoxetine tablet, fluoxetine DR weekly, fluvoxamine (IR and ER, flagged Clinical Criteria), Lexapro (Dose Optimization), paroxetine capsule/CR/suspension, Paxil, Paxil CR, Prozac, sertraline capsule MR, Trintellix (Dose Optimization, out of scope), Viibryd (Dose Optimization, out of scope), Zoloft. Coverage Parameters column states the exact fluvoxamine carve-out verbatim: 'Clinical editing will allow patients currently stabilized on fluvoxamine or fluvoxamine ER to continue to receive that agent without PA' and 'Clinical editing to allow patients with a diagnosis of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) to receive fluvoxamine and fluvoxamine ER without PA.' Preferred agent chosen as sertraline (most commonly prescribed first-line SSRI per standard clinical guidelines); citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, and paroxetine are equally preferred per the source and listed as alternatives. No PA flag/footnote appears next to any of the five preferred generics, so none of them (including sertraline) carries a preferred-agent restriction. Dose/sig (sertraline 50mg once daily) is standard first-line dosing convention, not itself stated in the PDL (which lists drug names and coverage status only). NYRx does not publish per-agent dollar copay tiers in this PDL -- 'tier' reflects PDL preferred/non-preferred status only, consistent with every other ny-medicaid cell in this dataset.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (curl with a browser User-Agent; a plain WebFetch call to fm.formularynavigator.com 403s, consistent with prior scar tissue in this repo) and confirmed on page 1 it is the '2026 Individual & Family Metal Plans Base, Simply Blue Plus and College Blue Plans Formulary Guide (2981)' from ExcellusBCBS.com, with the drug-list pages headed 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026' (page 5), matching the effective date already on file for this payer's other guides. Read the restriction-code legend on page 4 (PA=prior authorization, PV=preventive drug list [a cost-share benefit tag, not a barrier], QL=quantity limit, S/MS=specialty, ST=step therapy) and the 3-tier definitions on page 3 (Tier 1=most cost-effective generics, Tier 2=preferred brands, Tier 3=all other brand/generic). Read the exact 'ANTIDEPRESSANT - SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS (SSRIS)' section verbatim on page 35: citalopram (oral solution and tablet, Tier 1, PV), escitalopram oxalate tablet (Tier 1, PV), fluoxetine (capsule/solution Tier 1 PV; DR capsule Tier 3 QL/PV; tablet Tier 3 PV), fluvoxamine (tablet Tier 1 PV; ER capsule Tier 3 QL/PV), paroxetine HCl (tablet Tier 1 PV; ER tablet Tier 3 QL/PV), sertraline (concentrate and tablet, both Tier 1, PV). Every named agent in the class is a generic, all differences between them are tier/cost-sharing and quantity-limit only, and none carry a PA or step-therapy flag in the source, so paRequired is genuinely empty rather than an extraction gap. Sertraline oral tablet was selected as the preferred agent (clinically standard first-line SSRI, Tier 1, no restriction beyond the PV benefit tag) among several equally clean Tier-1 options; this single-agent selection among ties is my inference, not something the source itself ranks. No brand-name SSRI (Zoloft, Prozac, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Luvox) appears anywhere in the document — confirmed, not fabricated as non-formulary since the source is silent on them.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the PDF fetched at the cited URL (200 OK via curl with a browser User-Agent; a plain WebFetch call was not attempted given the known uhcprovider.com pattern documented in this repo's CLAUDE.md). Page 1 confirms the document is UnitedHealthcare's '2026 Prescription Drug List,' Effective January 1, 2026, publisher 'UnitedHealthcare & affiliated companies.' The 'Antidepressants - Drugs for Depression' section spans pages 12-13, and the page footer on 12-14 reads 'CT, NJ and NY,' confirming this edition applies to New York commercial plans. Page 5 supplies the symbol legend: 'E' = 'May be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York'; PA = prior authorization required; QL = quantity limit; ST = step therapy. Confirmed verbatim from the table: citalopram hydrobromide oral tablet (Tier 1), escitalopram oxalate oral tablet (Tier 1) / oral solution (Tier 2), fluoxetine hcl oral capsule/solution (Tier 1) vs. oral tablet 10/20/60mg (Tier 3, QL on the 10mg), fluvoxamine maleate (Tier 1) vs. ER (Tier 3, QL), paroxetine hcl oral tablet (Tier 1) vs. ER (Tier 3, QL), sertraline hcl oral tablet and oral concentrate (Tier 1) vs. oral capsule (marked E, QL), and all five brand names (CELEXA, LEXAPRO, PROZAC, PAXIL, PAXIL CR, ZOLOFT) marked 'E'. Sertraline tablet was selected as the single class-level preferred agent because it is the only SSRI in this list with zero flags of any kind (no PA/QL/E) on its plain oral tablet -- a judgment call inferred from the tier/flag pattern rather than an explicit 'preferred agent' statement in the document (the PDL lists all six SSRI generics at Tier 1 with no ranking among them), so this single-agent selection is marked 'partial' in spirit but the underlying tier/PA/QL data for every named agent is verbatim-verified. The representative 50mg strength and directions are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the PDL (which lists drug names/tiers/flags only, no dosing guidance).

Page 19 of the PDF (page index 18) contains the '*SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS (SSRIS)***' section heading with a table listing all 6 core oral SSRI generics -- citalopram hydrobromide (solution + tablet), escitalopram oxalate (solution + tablet), fluoxetine hcl (capsule, delayed-release capsule, solution, tablet), fluvoxamine maleate (tablet), paroxetine hcl (tablet and extended-release tablet), sertraline hcl (concentrate + tablet) -- every single row assigned 'Tier 1' with a blank Notes column (no PA/ST/QL/DO/NF/BE codes), confirmed against the document's own legend on the same page ('PA=Prior Authorization QL=Quantity Limit ST=Step Therapy DO=Dose Optimization'). No brand-name SSRI (Zoloft, Lexapro, Celexa, Prozac, Paxil, Luvox) appears anywhere in this section or the document's brand-name index, consistent with all six molecules being long-generic with no branded product carried on this list -- so there is nothing to flag as PA/step/non-formulary for this class. Confirmed: the Tier-1-for-all-six status and the absence of any restriction code is read verbatim off the table. Inferred (not stated verbatim in the source, which does not label any single SSRI as a plan 'preferred' pick since all six sit at the identical Tier 1 with identical notes): sertraline was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the most commonly first-line-prescribed generic SSRI in US clinical practice and carries the lowest typical cash/generic price among the six -- the other five are listed as equally-covered Tier 1 alternatives, not because the source ranks sertraline above them.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off pages 15-16 (PDF pages 25-26) of the 2026 Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 'Formulary (List of Covered Drugs)' for Medicare BlueActive (PPO) and 9 sibling Medicare Advantage plans, effective/updated 7/01/2026 (date visible on the cover page and repeated in the footer 'B-8970Y26 I 7/01/2026'). The document lists a single combined 'Ssris/Snris' section with a flat DRUG NAME / DRUG TIER / REQUIREMENTS-LIMITS table (no separate 'preferred' flag column). Confirmed verbatim: citalopram oral tablet = Tier 1 no restrictions; escitalopram oxalate oral tablet = Tier 1 no restrictions; fluoxetine oral capsule = Tier 1 no restrictions; paroxetine hcl oral tablet = Tier 2 no restrictions; fluvoxamine oral tablet = Tier 3 no restrictions; the corresponding oral solution/suspension/concentrate liquid forms of all six agents sit at Tier 4 with no PA/QL codes either. No brand-name SSRI (Lexapro, Zoloft, Prozac, Paxil, Celexa, Luvox) appears anywhere in the 108-page document -- confirmed via full-text search across every page -- so none are captured as PA/step/nonformulary because the source simply omits them (generics are the only SSRI products on this formulary; there is nothing brand-name to restrict). No class-wide step-therapy criterion is stated for the SSRI/SNRI section (unlike the B/D PA seen elsewhere in the document for unrelated antiemetic classes). What is INFERRED rather than read verbatim: the source does not itself designate any single agent as 'the' preferred SSRI -- four agents (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, sertraline) sit at the same Tier 1 with identical zero-restriction status. Sertraline was selected as the representative preferred pick because it is the most broadly-recommended first-line SSRI in clinical practice guidelines (favorable interaction/side-effect profile across age groups), not because the formulary singles it out -- the other three Tier-1 agents are clinically interchangeable substitutes at the identical tier and restriction level.

records.verificationNote: The URL supplied for this task (mhdl.pharmacy.services.conduent.com/MHDL/pubtheradetail.do?id=23, MassHealth's own MHDL 'Table 23') and its state-domain mirror (masshealthdruglist.ehs.state.ma.us) were both completely unreachable: a plain curl with a browser User-Agent hung to a 45s timeout with zero bytes, WebFetch timed out at 60s on three different paths on that host (id=23, id=17, and the /MHDL index), and two independent browser-automation tools (a Claude_Browser preview session and the claude-in-chrome extension) either hung indefinitely on navigate or silently reset the tab to about:blank/new-tab immediately after a reported-successful navigation -- confirmed on this exact domain across roughly a dozen attempts with different URLs, so this is a genuine, confirmed access block in this environment, not a one-off fluke. Per the task's fallback instruction, switched to WebSearch: found and read (via a real Chrome tab, pages 1-3) 'Antidepressants_PA_MH_MBRx_06.05.23.pdf' hosted on Mass General Brigham Health Plan's own resources domain, whose page-1 header explicitly states 'Plan: MassHealth UPPL' and 'Effective 12/04/2023' -- i.e. this document reproduces MassHealth's own uniform antidepressant PA criteria, not a generic MCO policy. A separate WebSearch snippet (from a different MGB Health Plan medical policy on IV ketamine) independently confirmed that MGB's own documents cite 'MHDL Table 17: Antidepressants' as their MassHealth source -- meaning Table 17, not Table 23, is the correct MHDL antidepressant class id; the id given in this task (23) does not appear to be the antidepressant table, which independently explains part of why that specific URL never surfaced a matching page even before the domain-access failure. Confirmed directly from the fetched PDF: every standard-dose oral SSRI formulation (citalopram tablet/solution, escitalopram tablet, fluoxetine 10/20/40 mg capsule/solution, paroxetine hydrochloride immediate-release tablet, sertraline oral concentrate/tablet, fluvoxamine immediate-release) is listed in the 'No PA' column, both generic and its brand name together (Celexa, Lexapro, Prozac, Paxil, Zoloft); PA is required only for atypical formulations/doses of the same molecules (fluoxetine 60 mg tablet and 90 mg weekly capsule, fluvoxamine ER, Paxil CR, Pexeva, sertraline capsule). Inferred rather than read verbatim: which single agent counts as 'the preferred agent' for this checkpoint's schema -- this document's own structure is binary (No PA vs Require PA) with no formal preferred/non-preferred PDL tier language for standard oral SSRIs, so no single molecule is payer-named as 'preferred.' Sertraline was picked as the representative top-line pick for schema consistency, matching this project's existing ny-medicaid and mgb (same state, different payer) SSRI records, not because the source itself ranks it above citalopram/escitalopram/fluoxetine/paroxetine/fluvoxamine IR -- all six are equally open (no PA) per this document. Because the primary MHDL Table 17 page itself could not be reached to confirm this is still current in 2026 (over 2.5 years after the document's 12/04/2023 effective date) and because the source is a health-plan's PA policy applying MassHealth's criteria rather than the state PBM's own MHDL page, this is marked partial rather than verified.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off "Standard Control with Advanced Control Specialty Formulary® – Focused" (CVS Caremark, per PDF metadata author field and document title "BCBSMA SC ACSF Focused 0126"), cover page states "Effective 01/01/2026", pages 39-40 of 177, section "ANTIDEPRESSANTS - DRUGS TO TREAT DEPRESSION" > "SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS (SSRIS)". Confirmed verbatim: generic citalopram hydrobromide (soln, tabs), generic escitalopram oxalate (soln, tabs), generic fluoxetine hcl (caps, cpdr, soln, and standard tabs 10mg/20mg except generics for SARAFEM), generic fluvoxamine maleate (cp24, tabs), generic paroxetine hcl (susp, tabs, and tb24 except NDC 60505367503), and generic sertraline hcl (caps, conc, tabs) are all Tier 1 with no PA/QL/ST flag in the "Requirements/Limits" column. Brand CELEXA (tabs 10mg/20mg/40mg) is listed at Tier 3 with no restriction flag, i.e. covered but non-preferred cost-sharing, not a PA/step/nonformulary barrier. No brand line items for Zoloft, Prozac, Paxil, Paxil CR, or Luvox appear anywhere in the document's antidepressant section (pages 39-40) or elsewhere via a full-text search of all 177 pages for those strings, so no claim is made about their status — only agents actually named in the source are reported here. Preferred agent was chosen as sertraline (most commonly prescribed first-line SSRI per standard clinical convention) since the source lists all five oral SSRI molecules as equally Tier 1 with no differentiation; the specific 50mg strength and once-daily sig are standard first-line dosing convention, not themselves stated in the formulary (which lists drug names/tiers/requirements only, not sig). The document's PDF metadata (author='CVS Caremark', creationDate 2025-10-24) and the URL host (home.bluecrossma.com, BCBSMA's own collateral-asset domain) both corroborate this is the correct, current, payer-specific source referenced by the task's known-source link (bluecrossma.org/medication/standard-control-formulary, which itself 403'd a direct fetch and links out to this PDF via BCBSMA's document library).

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (WebFetch retrieved the raw binary; text was then extracted page-by-page with a dedicated PDF-reading tool since a plain text pass over the object stream was unreadable, consistent with prior scar tissue in this repo re: large government/payer PDLs). Page 1 confirms this is Tufts Health Plan's 'Prescription drug list, Direct 3-Tier,' 'Effective Jan. 1, 2026,' created October 1, 2025, distributed via welcome.optumrx.com/valuedirecty2 (OptumRx member portal) -- matching the requested payer and PBM exactly. Every content page carries a rolling footer 'Last Update: 07/15/26 / Next Update: 08/15/26,' indicating this specific capture reflects the July 15, 2026 monthly refresh of the same Jan-1-2026 plan-year document. Page 4 confirms the tier/PA/ST/QL legend (PA = Prior Authorization; a bare 'PA' with no further text means full PA for all ages, distinct from the explicit 'PA: Prior Authorization required for members 12 and younger' pediatric-only notation used elsewhere in the same list). Pages 18-19 (Antidepressants section, confirmed via text search on 'sertraline') were read in full and directly confirm: sertraline (Tier 1, no restriction note), citalopram (Tier 1, no restriction), escitalopram (Tier 1, no restriction), fluoxetine -- all 4 listed forms (Tier 1, no restriction), fluvoxamine -- both forms (Tier 1, no restriction), paroxetine IR and ER tablets (Tier 1, PA for members 12 and younger only), and paroxetine oral suspension (Tier 2, bare 'PA' = full PA for any age). The absence of all five common SSRI brand names (Zoloft, Prozac, Paxil, Celexa, Lexapro) was confirmed by a direct text search returning zero matches across the full 147-page document; their nonformulary status is inferred from that absence plus the document's demonstrated practice of listing other covered brands in ALL CAPS, not from an explicit statement naming each brand non-formulary -- flagged as an inference in the paRequired reason above.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited OptumRx-hosted PDF (WebFetch's built-in PDF text extraction failed on this 3.3MB/160-page file and returned only binary/metadata, consistent with this repo's known large-PDF extraction gap -- re-extracted the saved binary locally with pypdf instead). Page 1 confirms the document title 'Select 3-Tier / 2026 Prescription Drug List,' publisher 'Harvard Pilgrim Health Care' (also naming Harvard Pilgrim Health Care of New England and HPHC Insurance Company), created October 1, 2025, with mail-order administered via 'Optum Home Delivery' (page 2), confirming the OptumRx PBM relationship stated in the task. Every content page carries a footer 'Last Update: 07/15/26 / Next Update: 08/15/26,' which is the document's live revision date used here as effectiveDate. The Tier legend (page 2) and restriction-code glossary (page 3: PA=Prior Authorization, ST=Step Therapy, HSA=HSA Preventive Drug benefit tag, QL=Quantity Limit) were read directly. The 'Antidepressants' table spans PDF pages 18-19 (footer page numbers 18-19) and lists all six oral SSRI molecules verbatim: citalopram (tablet + solution, Tier 1, HSA, no PA), escitalopram (tablet + solution, Tier 1, HSA, no PA), fluoxetine (capsule/DR-capsule/solution/tablet, all Tier 1, HSA, no PA), fluvoxamine (tablet Tier 1 HSA no PA; ER capsule Tier 2 HSA no PA), paroxetine (tablet + ER-tablet, Tier 1, HSA, 'PA: Prior Authorization required for members 12 and younger' -- an age-limited PA, not a general adult barrier; oral suspension, Tier 2, HSA, plain 'PA' with no age qualifier -- a genuine adult-applicable PA), and sertraline (concentrate + tablet, both Tier 1, HSA, no PA). Confirmed by full-text search across all 160 pages that no brand-name SSRI (Zoloft, Prozac, Paxil, Celexa, Lexapro, Luvox) appears anywhere in the document -- the false-positive substring hit for 'luvox' inside 'fluvoxamine' was manually checked and is not a real brand mention -- so there is nothing to list as non-formulary brand product; every SSRI product on this formulary is already generic. Sertraline tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the most broadly first-line-prescribed generic SSRI in clinical practice, not because the source itself ranks one SSRI above the other four Tier-1, no-PA agents (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, and paroxetine's PA is pediatric-only) -- this single-agent selection among clinically-tied options is an inference, consistent with the same convention used across every other payer in this dataset's ssri-oral class. The 50 mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the drug list itself (which lists drug names, tiers, and restriction codes only, no dosing guidance). No class-wide step-therapy criterion appears anywhere in this section.

All six oral SSRI molecules are generic-only entries at Tier 1 (the plan's lowest/preferred tier) with quantity limits but no PA, step therapy, or non-formulary flags anywhere in this class -- confirmed directly from the document text, not inferred. Sertraline chosen as the representative preferred pick per this app's cross-guide convention (matches the NY guides); the underlying data shows the plan treats all five SSRI molecules as equally preferred (all Tier 1), so the 'preferred vs alternative' split here is a convention choice, not a payer-stated ranking -- flagging this since the source itself does not name one single top agent within the class.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (WebFetch's built-in extraction returned only binary/metadata on this 236-page/2.6MB file, consistent with this repo's known large-PDF extraction gap, so the saved binary was re-read locally via the PDF-tools MCP's page-range and search functions instead). Page 1 confirms the document title 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026' with 'Last updated 07/01/26' as a footer/header stamp -- used as effectiveDate. Page 2 confirms publisher CareFirst BlueCross BlueShield and the 5-tier legend (Tier 0 $0, Tier 1 Generic, Tier 2 Preferred Brand, Tier 3 Non-preferred Brand, Tier 4/5 Specialty); page 3 defines the PA/PA**/QL/ST/M/OTC restriction codes. This is the Exchange/individual-market formulary named in the task (explicitly scoped to individual/family plans, small-group <51 employees, and student health plans on page 1) -- matches the payer and PBM (CVS Caremark) named in the task. The Antidepressants section (alphabetical drug list, PDF pages 63-69, printed page numbers 61-67) was read via targeted keyword search plus full page reads rather than reading the whole 236-page document. All six oral SSRI molecules were confirmed verbatim, every strength/form line item Tier 1 with no PA/ST/QL code present: citalopram hydrobromide (oral solution 10mg/5ml; tabs 10/20/40mg, page 65), escitalopram oxalate (oral solution 5mg/5ml; tabs 5/10/20mg, page 67), fluoxetine HCl (caps 10/20/40mg, DR cap 90mg, solution 20mg/5ml, all Tier 1 no restriction; tabs 10mg/20mg also Tier 1 but carry the explicit annotation '(generic Sarafem not covered)' -- confirmed by direct text search for 'Sarafem', page 67), paroxetine HCl (tabs 10/20/30/40mg; ER tabs 12.5/25/37.5mg, page 69), fluvoxamine maleate (tabs 25/50/100mg; ER caps 100/150mg, page 63), and sertraline HCl (oral concentrate 20mg/ml; tabs 25/50/100mg, page 69). A full-text search across the document for every brand SSRI name (PROZAC, ZOLOFT, PAXIL, CELEXA, LEXAPRO) returned zero matches -- none appear anywhere in the formulary as distinct brand line items, so there is no brand-name entry to cite as a separate non-formulary product beyond the one explicit Sarafem annotation found. Sertraline tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the most broadly first-line-prescribed generic SSRI in clinical practice, not because the source ranks one SSRI above the other four Tier-1, no-restriction agents -- this single-agent selection among clinically-tied, equally-covered options is an inference, consistent with the convention used across every other payer in this dataset's ssri-oral class. The 50 mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the drug list itself (which lists drug name, tier, and restriction codes only, no dosing guidance). No class-wide step-therapy or PA criterion appears anywhere in the antidepressants section for any of the six SSRI molecules.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the payer's own cited source first: 'Priority Partners (690)' formulary PDF (PPMCO Formulary 690), effective 07/01/2026, 184 pages, via a dedicated PDF-reading tool (a plain WebFetch on this URL returns only corrupted/binary stream text, consistent with this repo's known large-PDF caveat). Confirmed by exhaustive search of the full document that sertraline, fluoxetine, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine, fluvoxamine, and their brand names (Zoloft, Prozac, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Luvox) appear ZERO times anywhere in the 184 pages — this MCO's own formulary carries no SSRI/antidepressant listing at all. Page 16 of that PDF states why, verbatim: 'Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is responsible for formulary management of most drugs used for behavioral health purposes which are covered under the Medicaid Mental Health Formulary... Drugs in these classes are carved out of the MCO pharmacy benefit and are payable as fee-for-service through Maryland Medical Assistance.' Page 17 explicitly lists 'Antidepressants (AHFS Class No. 281604)' among the carved-out AHFS classes. This is a real, verified structural fact about Priority Partners, not a gap: SSRIs are not managed by this MCO/PBM (Johns Hopkins HealthCare) at all — coverage for a Priority Partners member is instead determined entirely by the statewide Maryland Medicaid Pharmacy Program PDL under fee-for-service. Followed that carve-out to its actual source (already used elsewhere in this repo as 'md-pdl-2026'): the Maryland Department of Health, Office of Pharmacy Services 'Preferred Drug List', Effective Date 1/1/2026, Updated 3/26/2026 (health.maryland.gov). Page 16, section 'CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM' > 'Antidepressants, Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)', read verbatim: Preferred column lists citalopram tablets/solution (Celexa, ql), escitalopram solution/tablets (Lexapro), fluoxetine capsules/solution/tablets at all strengths except 60mg and weekly (Prozac), fluvoxamine (Luvox), paroxetine (Paxil), and sertraline tablets/concentrated solution (Zoloft) — all six class molecules, standard formulations, no PA. The Non-Preferred/Requires Prior Authorization column lists only atypical dosage forms of those same six molecules (citalopram capsules; fluoxetine 60mg and Prozac Weekly; fluvoxamine ER/Luvox CR; paroxetine CR/Paxil CR; paroxetine mesylate 7.5mg capsules/Brisdelle with cc+ql flags; paroxetine suspension; sertraline capsules), not any additional agent outside the six named molecules. Sertraline (50mg tablet once daily) selected as the representative preferred agent per this repo's existing ssri-oral convention (see the ny-ssris guide). Strength/sig (50mg, once daily) is standard first-line dosing convention, not itself printed in the PDL (which lists drug/formulation names and PA status only, no doses or sigs) — marked inferred; the preferred/non-preferred/PA determination itself is read verbatim from the cited document with its effective date visible. This is a binary Preferred/Non-Preferred Medicaid PDL, not a tiered commercial formulary, so no dollar-tier/copay-level statement exists to cite.

The Antidepressants -- Drugs for Depression section (PDF pages 12-13) was located via a direct text search for 'Antidepressants' rather than reading the whole 80-page document, then read in full. All six oral SSRI molecules were confirmed verbatim: citalopram hydrobromide oral tablet (Tier 1, no code), CELEXA brand (marked E), escitalopram oxalate oral tablet (Tier 1) and oral solution 5mg/5ml (Tier 2), LEXAPRO brand (marked E), fluoxetine HCl oral capsule and oral solution (Tier 1, no code), fluoxetine HCl oral tablet 10mg (Tier 3, QL) and 20mg/60mg (Tier 3), PROZAC brand (marked E), fluvoxamine maleate immediate-release (Tier 1) and ER (Tier 3, QL), paroxetine HCl oral tablet (Tier 1) and ER (Tier 3, QL), PAXIL and PAXIL CR brands (marked E, PAXIL CR also QL), sertraline HCl oral tablet and oral concentrate (Tier 1, no code) but a separately-listed 'sertraline hcl oral capsule' line marked E and QL, and ZOLOFT brand (marked E). Sertraline tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the most broadly first-line-prescribed generic SSRI in clinical practice and reads cleanest (Tier 1, no code) among the six Tier-1 no-restriction options -- this single-agent selection among clinically-tied, equally-covered options is an inference, consistent with the convention used across every other payer in this dataset's ssri-oral class. The 50 mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the PDL itself (which lists drug name, tier, and restriction codes only, no dosing guidance). No class-wide step-therapy or PA criterion appears anywhere in the Antidepressants section that applies across all SSRI molecules as a group (the ST codes present in this section attach only to non-SSRI agents: AUVELITY, FETZIMA, TRINTELLIX, VIIBRYD). Read verbatim off page 41 (the 'SSRI/SNRI' subsection of the 'Antidepressants' category, itself continuing from page 40): every one of the six target generics (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine, fluvoxamine, sertraline) is listed with its own tier number and any coverage-rule code (PA/ST/QL/DL) taken from the abbreviation legend on pages 6-7 of the same document. records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly with a dedicated PDF-reading tool (a plain WebFetch on this URL returned only raw PDF object/binary stream text, consistent with this repo's known large-PDF extraction gap). Page 1 confirms the document title 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary — Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026' and the restriction-code legend (AL, DO, GR, OTC, PA, QL, SP, ST). The 'SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS (SSRIS)' section (PDF page 23, under 'ANTIDEPRESSANTS') was located via full-text search for 'SSRI' and read verbatim: citalopram hydrobromide oral solution (no code) and oral tablet (reference CeleXA, no code); escitalopram oxalate oral tablet (reference Lexapro, QL); fluoxetine hcl oral capsule and oral solution (no code); fluvoxamine maleate oral tablet (no code); paroxetine hcl oral tablet (reference Paxil, no code); sertraline hcl oral concentrate (reference Zoloft, PA) and oral tablet (reference Zoloft, no code). All six SSRI molecules in standard oral tablet/capsule/solution form are on this Preferred Drug List with no PA/step therapy (only escitalopram tablet carries a QL, and only the sertraline oral-concentrate liquid form carries PA) -- consistent with the convention used across every other payer already in this dataset's ssri-oral class. Sertraline tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the most broadly first-line-prescribed generic SSRI in clinical practice, not because the source ranks one SSRI above the other four equally-listed, no-restriction agents -- this single-agent selection among clinically-tied options is an inference, matching this repo's cross-payer ssri-oral convention. The 50 mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the PDL itself (which lists drug/formulation names and restriction codes only, no dosing guidance). No class-wide step-therapy criterion appears anywhere in the Antidepressants section for the SSRI molecules. This is a binary PDL (listed = covered, with inline restriction codes), not a tiered commercial formulary, so 'tier' reflects PDL/copay-flat status rather than a numbered cost tier.
