records.tier: PDL preferred generic · no service authorization (Medicaid has no tier system)
records.verificationNote: Read directly from Virginia's Medicaid Preferred Drug List (PDL)/Common Core Formulary, effective 4/1/2026 (Version 04/01/2026 v1), pages 29-30 of the 73-page PDF, 'Antidepressants > SSRI' section. Confirmed verbatim: Preferred Agents column lists citalopram soln/tab, escitalopram tab, fluoxetine cap/soln, fluvoxamine tab, paroxetine tab, and sertraline conc/soln/tab/cap (sertraline capsule appears split across the page break onto p.30 as a Non-Preferred entry -- see note below). Non-Preferred Agents column lists Brisdelle, Celexa tab, citalopram HBR 30mg, escitalopram soln, fluoxetine DR cap/tab, fluvoxamine ER, Lexapro tab, paroxetine CR, paroxetine susp, Paxil tab/susp/CR, Prozac cap/weekly, sertraline cap (on p.30), and Zoloft conc/tab. The SA Criteria column for this SSRI section is blank (no PA-criteria narrative), unlike the adjacent 'Antidepressants Other' and CGRP sections, which have explicit narrative service-authorization criteria -- confirming SSRI non-preferred items follow only the routine PDL prior-authorization edit (no clinical criteria beyond preferred-first). VA Medicaid MCOs including Anthem HealthKeepers Plus follow this statewide PDL (confirmed via Anthem's pharmacy page linking the same Cardinal Care PDL, and the payer's own Cardinal Care formulary printout, effective 5/1/2026, tracking the same PDL). Preferred agent chosen as sertraline per this dataset's cross-payer convention (most commonly prescribed first-line SSRI); citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, and paroxetine are equally preferred per the PDL and listed as alternatives. Dose/sig (sertraline 50mg once daily) is standard first-line dosing convention, not itself stated in the PDL (which lists drug names and preferred/non-preferred status only, not strengths/sigs beyond dosage form). Virginia Medicaid PDLs do not publish per-agent dollar copay tiers -- 'tier' reflects PDL preferred/non-preferred status only, consistent with every other anthem-healthkeepers-plus cell in this dataset (see the metformin-oral cell for the identical tier phrasing and pa/nonformulary convention).

records.tier: Generic (formulary lists a single 'Generic' tier for these six agents; no numbered cost-share tiers used in this Medicaid drug list)
records.verificationNote: Fetched the PDF directly from the URL discovered on the Sentara Health Plans Medicaid prescription-drug-lists page (https://www.sentarahealthplans.com/en/members/medicaid/prescription-drug-lists), which links to '2026 Sentara Community Plan Prescription Drug Formulary (Effective 7/1/2026)'; a plain WebFetch on the PDF URL itself returned only binary/compressed stream data (consistent with prior scar tissue on large PDFs in this repo), so extracted text with pdfplumber instead. Page 1 confirms title 'SENTARA COMMUNITY PLAN PRESCRIPTION DRUG FORMULARY (July-September 2026)', 'This formulary was updated on 07/01/2026', doc code SHP_MD_MEM_OMSC_230005. Page 2 confirms this is the Virginia Medicaid P&T-committee-approved preferred drug list, administered per DMAS PDL rules, with PBM Express Scripts named explicitly on page 6. Pages 34, 36, and 38 (within the psychiatric/CNS section) list all six oral SSRIs verbatim: citalopram oral tablet/solution, escitalopram oxalate oral tablet, fluoxetine oral capsule/solution, fluvoxamine oral tablet, paroxetine hcl oral tablet, sertraline oral tablet/concentrate -- every single row assigned 'Generic' tier with only a Quantity Limit (QL) restriction column value, no PA and no age restriction (unlike many nearby antipsychotic/stimulant rows on the same pages, which DO carry PA-for-age flags). Confirmed via full-text search across all 160 pages that no brand-name SSRI (Zoloft, Celexa, Lexapro, Prozac, Paxil, Luvox) appears anywhere in the document (the one 'luvox' text match was a substring false-positive inside the word 'fluvoxamine'). Page 6's 'Mandated Generic Substitution' policy statement (multi-source drugs must be filled generically unless brand is PDL-preferred or prescriber requests brand-medically-necessary) is read verbatim and applied to explain why the never-listed brand names are 'nonformulary' rather than fabricating a specific PA/ST code for them -- this inference (brand status) is the one non-verbatim step; every generic agent's tier and restriction is read directly off the page. No class-wide step-therapy criterion is stated anywhere near the SSRI rows. Choice of sertraline as the single 'preferred agent' for this app's schema is this session's convention (matching sibling ssri-oral records for other Medicaid/commercial payers in this repo), not a distinction the source itself draws -- the source treats all six generics as equally covered with no ranking beyond identical Generic tier and QL-only limits.

records.tier: Tier 1 - preferred generic; subject to a quantity limit (QL)
records.verificationNote: Fetched the UHC Community Plan of Virginia (Cardinal Care) Preferred Drug List PDF directly (curl with a browser User-Agent; a plain WebFetch was not attempted first given this repo's known uhcprovider.com/formulary-PDF-host block pattern). Cover page (PDF page 1) reads 'Preferred Drug List (PDL), Virginia Cardinal Care, Effective Date: 7/1/2026, (c) 2024 United HealthCare Services, Inc.' -- confirming publisher and effective date. Read the 'SSRIs/SNRIs (Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors/Serotonin and Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor) - Antidepressants' section verbatim, PDF pages 282-283 (printed page 260-261 per footer). Confirmed verbatim, Preferred Agents column (Tier 1, no PA): citalopram hydrobromide oral solution 10mg/5ml and oral tablet, escitalopram oxalate oral tablet, fluoxetine hcl oral capsule (10mg/40mg and 20mg) and oral solution, fluvoxamine maleate, paroxetine hcl oral tablet, sertraline hcl oral concentrate and oral tablet (also present in the same preferred column but out of scope for this SSRI-only class: desvenlafaxine ER, trazodone, venlafaxine/venlafaxine ER, vilazodone). Confirmed verbatim, Non-Preferred Agents column (mix of Tier 1-with-PA dosage forms and Tier 2 PA brands): Celexa, citalopram capsule, escitalopram capsule, escitalopram solution, Lexapro, fluoxetine (PMDD product), fluoxetine DR/weekly capsule, fluoxetine tablet, fluvoxamine ER, paroxetine ER, paroxetine suspension, paroxetine mesylate, Paxil, Paxil CR, sertraline capsule, Zoloft. The legend footer on both pages defines Tier 1 = Generic, Tier 2 = Brand, PA = Prior Auth Required, QL = Quantity Limit, AL = age-limited, ST = Step Therapy -- no ST codes appear anywhere in this SSRI/SNRI section, so no class-wide step-therapy criterion is stated. Preferred agent chosen as sertraline (standard first-line convention, consistent with other guides in this dataset); citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine, and fluvoxamine (in their Tier-1-no-PA forms) are equally preferred per the source and listed as alternatives. Dose/sig (sertraline 50mg once daily) is standard first-line dosing convention -- inferred, not itself stated in the PDL, which lists drug/dosage-form names and coverage codes only, hence 'partial' would apply narrowly to the sig fields alone while the coverage/PA facts are fully verified verbatim.

records.tier: Preferred (state PDL) · no PA/step flag on the preferred forms
records.verificationNote: Fetched Aetna Better Health of Virginia's own FormularyNavigator FBO export directly (https://fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/111/Aetna_Better_Health_of_Virginia.json, a plain WebFetch 403s the host but a browser-UA curl succeeds per this project's known FormularyNavigator quirk) -- confirmed the page's own Profile_Information caption: public_name 'Aetna_Better_Health_of_Virginia', state VIRGINIA, formulary_type 'State Medicaid', formulary_name 'ABH VA V2', formulary_id 22791, formulary_version 33, formulary_effective_date 07/01/2026. Searched all 102,529 NDC rows in Drug_Information for every SSRI generic/brand name (sertraline/Zoloft, citalopram/Celexa, escitalopram/Lexapro, fluoxetine/Prozac/Sarafem, paroxetine/Paxil/Brisdelle, fluvoxamine/Luvox) and grouped by ndc_label_name. Confirmed verbatim per-drug drug_tier values: citalopram tablet/solution, escitalopram tablet/solution, fluoxetine capsule/solution, fluvoxamine tablet, paroxetine tablet, and sertraline tablet/concentrate are all tier='Preferred'; every brand-name entry (Zoloft, Celexa, Lexapro, Prozac, Paxil/Paxil CR, Brisdelle) and every non-standard form (sertraline capsule, fluoxetine tablet/PMDD tablet/DR weekly capsule, paroxetine ER/suspension, fluvoxamine ER, escitalopram capsule, citalopram capsule) is tier='State PDL Non-Preferred'. The prior_authorization and step_therapy booleans are false on every SSRI row in this export (both preferred and non-preferred) -- the 'State PDL Non-Preferred' tier label itself, not a separate boolean flag, is how this MCO's export encodes the state PDL's routine PA edit, matching the pattern already confirmed for the sibling anthem-healthkeepers-plus VA Medicaid MCO cell (whose direct DMAS PDL PDF read shows no clinical PA-criteria narrative for SSRI non-preferred items, only the routine preferred-first PDL edit). One discrepancy versus that April 2026 DMAS PDL PDF: this July 2026 Aetna export shows escitalopram oral solution as Preferred with a quantity limit (QL=true), where the 4/1/2026 statewide PDL PDF listed escitalopram solution as non-preferred (tablet only preferred) -- plausibly reflects the newer 7/1/2026 quarterly PDL update captured live in Aetna's claims export versus the older PDF revision; the escitalopram tablet forms are Preferred in both sources regardless, so the class's preferred-agent pick (sertraline) and overall structure are unaffected. All VA Medicaid MCOs including Aetna Better Health follow the same statewide Cardinal Care PDL by contract (confirmed for this payer specifically via its own live FBO export, per this project's convention of preferring the payer's own FormularyNavigator export over a shared-PDL mirror). Dose/sig (sertraline 50mg once daily) is standard first-line dosing convention, not itself stated in the export (which carries NDC/tier/PA/QL flags, not sig text). No numbered dollar-tier language appears in this Medicaid export -- 'tier' reflects PDL preferred/non-preferred status only.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (curl with a browser User-Agent, 200 OK, 1,284,168 bytes, 83 pages; confirmed byte-identical via the PDF Tools MCP fetch as well). Page 1 title reads 'Select Drug List — Drug list — Four Tier Drug Plan'; page 4 and page 81 footer text confirm publisher 'Anthem Blue Cross and Blue Shield is the trade name of Anthem Health Plans of Virginia' / 'Anthem Health Plans, Inc., Independent licensee of the Blue Cross and Blue Shield Association', matching payer anthem-commercial. Page footer reads 'Effective 07012026' (2026-07-01) on every content page including page 19, the exact page holding the '*SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS (SSRIS)***' section. Directly read off page 19: citalopram (solution + tablet), escitalopram (solution + tablet), fluoxetine (capsule, delayed-release capsule, solution, tablet), fluvoxamine (tablet), paroxetine (tablet + ER 24-hour tablet), and sertraline (concentrate + tablet) are ALL listed as Tier 1 with a blank Notes column (no PA/ST/QL/DO code) -- confirmed against the tier/code legend on page 18/page 4 (PA=Prior Authorization, ST=Step Therapy, QL=Quantity Limit, DO=Dose Optimization). A full-document text search for ZOLOFT, LEXAPRO, PROZAC, CELEXA, and PAXIL returned zero matches across all 83 pages; page 2's own FAQ states 'this is a complete listing of all covered drugs,' so the consistent absence of every brand name is read as non-formulary status rather than an extraction gap. What is inferred rather than read verbatim: the source's tier list does not itself designate a single 'most preferred' agent among the six equally-tiered, equally-unrestricted SSRIs -- sertraline was chosen as the single preferred pick for consistency with this app's convention in other state guides (ny-ssris, ma-ssris, md-ssris), not because the Anthem document ranks it above the other five. The sig/strength/plain-language fields are standard clinical convention, not sourced from this formulary (which does not include dosing instructions).

records.verificationNote: Fetched the exact PDF cited by the WebFetch lookup of the given source page (curl with a browser User-Agent, 200 OK, 2,195,790 bytes, 120 pages). Page 1 title reads 'SENTARA HEALTH EMPLOYER PLANS STANDARD FORMULARY (FOR LARGE GROUP EMPLOYER PLANS) PRESCRIPTION DRUG FORMULARY (July - September 2026)', 'This formulary was updated on 07/01/2026', doc code SHP_MD_MEM_OMSC_230005 -- confirming publisher Sentara Health Plans and matching the payer's commercial/employer-group segment (this is the general 'Standard Formulary - Large Group (51 or more employees)' variant, not the org-specific copy). Pages 9-10 name Express Scripts explicitly as the PBM whose systems the guidebook data was collected from. Page 6's Tier Definition table and page 11's abbreviation legend were read verbatim: Tier 1 = Preferred Generic, Tier 2 = Preferred Brand & Other Generic (higher-cost generics/standard-therapy brands), Tier 3 = Non-Preferred Brand, Tier 4 = Specialty, Tier 9 = $0 ACA preventive; PA/QL/ST defined explicitly. Page 7 states brand products are printed in CAPITALS and generics in lowercase italics throughout the drug table. Directly read off pages 39-41 and 43 (within the 'AUTONOMIC & CNS DRUGS, NEUROLOGY & PSYCH' section, TOC page 19): sertraline oral tablet 25/50mg and 100mg -- Tier 1, QL only (1.5-2/day); sertraline oral concentrate -- Tier 2, QL(10/day); citalopram oral tablet 10/20mg and 40mg -- Tier 1, QL only; citalopram oral solution -- Tier 2, QL(20/day); escitalopram oxalate oral tablet 5/10/20mg -- Tier 1, QL only; escitalopram oral solution -- Tier 2, QL(20/day); fluoxetine oral capsule -- Tier 1, QL(2/day); fluoxetine oral solution and oral tablet 10/20mg -- Tier 2, QL only; paroxetine hcl oral tablet 10/20/30/40mg -- Tier 1, QL only; fluvoxamine oral tablet 25/50/100mg -- Tier 2, QL only. None of the six generic SSRI line items carry a PA or ST flag (in contrast to nearby antipsychotic/CNS rows on the same pages, e.g. chlorpromazine, clozapine, paliperidone, which do carry explicit 'PA for age 17 and younger' flags), confirming no restriction on any SSRI generic itself. A full-document text search for ZOLOFT, LEXAPRO, PROZAC, CELEXA, and PAXIL returned zero matches across all 120 pages; the one LUVOX-adjacent match is a substring false-positive inside 'fluvoxamine'. What is inferred rather than read verbatim: the document does not itself rank one SSRI above the other five as a single 'preferred agent' -- sertraline was chosen as the preferred pick for consistency with this app's convention for every other ssri-oral record in this repo (sentara-community, anthem-commercial, etc.), not because the source distinguishes it. The classification of unlisted brand names as 'nonformulary' rather than a different PA/ST code is inferred by combining (a) their total absence from the capitalized-brand-name convention and (b) the verbatim 'Mandated Generic Substitution' policy on page 8, which describes a Non-Preferred Brand Copay + Brand Additional Charge rather than an outright block -- this is the same convention already applied to the sibling anthem-commercial and sentara-community VA records in this repo. Sig/strength/plain-language fields are standard clinical convention, not sourced from this formulary (which lists tiers and quantity limits, not dosing instructions).

records.verificationNote: Fetched the Wellcare Value Script (PDP) 2026 Comprehensive Formulary PDF directly from fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/67/2_6T_Enhanced_PDP_Comp_Form_26195.pdf (curl with a browser User-Agent; a plain WebFetch to this host returned only the wellcare.com landing page, consistent with this repo's known FormularyNavigator WebFetch-blocking pattern) -- reached via Wellcare's own site (wellcare.com/en/virginia/members/prescription-drug-plans-2026/wellcare-value-script/pharmacy/drug-list-formulary), confirming this exact PDF is the one Wellcare links for the Virginia Value Script (PDP) plan (contract/plan S4802-142). Page 1 confirms the document title 'Formulary (List of Covered Drugs or Drug List) -- Wellcare Value Script (PDP)', publisher Wellcare, HPMS Approved Formulary File Submission ID 26195, 'This formulary was updated on 07/01/2026' (also stamped in the running footer on every content page). Page 13-14 give the tier legend verbatim: Tier 1 = Preferred Generic ($0 preferred/$15 standard copay), Tier 2 = Generic ($3/$20), Tier 4 = Non-Preferred Drug (40%/50% coinsurance). Read the alphabetical drug table directly: citalopram oral tablet 10/20/40mg and oral solution = Tier 1, no requirement/limit codes (page 73); escitalopram oxalate oral tablet 5/10/20mg and oral solution = Tier 4, no codes (page 76); fluoxetine oral capsule 10mg/20mg = Tier 1 no codes, capsule 40mg and oral solution = Tier 2 no codes (pages 76-77); fluvoxamine oral tablet 100/25/50mg = Tier 2, no codes (page 77); paroxetine hcl oral tablet 10/20/40mg = Tier 2 with QL (30 EA per 30 days), oral tablet 30mg = Tier 2 QL (60 EA per 30 days), oral suspension = Tier 4 QL (page 81); sertraline oral tablet 100/25/50mg = Tier 1 no codes, oral concentrate = Tier 4 no codes (page 82). Confirmed via full-document text search that no brand-name SSRI (Prozac, Zoloft, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Luvox) appears anywhere in the 190-page document -- every SSRI product on this Part D formulary is generic-only, so paRequired is genuinely empty (nothing to flag as PA/step/non-formulary) rather than an extraction gap. Sertraline tablet was selected as the single preferred agent (Tier 1, zero restriction codes, standard first-line SSRI) among the two other equally-clean Tier 1 options (citalopram, fluoxetine 10/20mg) -- this single-agent ranking among Tier-1 ties is my inference (the source does not itself label one specific SSRI 'preferred' over the others at the same tier), consistent with the same judgment call made for every other payer's ssri-oral cell in this dataset. No class-wide step-therapy criterion appears in the SSRI section of this document.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (a plain WebFetch call returned only a low-confidence summary saying the text wasn't legible for this class -- consistent with the repo's existing large-government-PDL scar tissue -- so the dedicated PDF tool was used instead to download and read it locally). Page 1 confirms the document is the 'Illinois Medicaid Preferred Drug List, Effective July 1, 2026' (Illinois Dept. of Healthcare and Family Services). The SSRI section is explicitly headed 'ANTIDEPRESSANTS : SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITOR (SSRIs)' (this exact heading string appears in the extracted text at the bottom of page 22, labeling the drug list immediately above it, which is this PDF's consistent per-page layout convention); the six SSRI molecules (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine, sertraline) and every named dosage form's PREFERRED/NON_PREFERRED status were read directly and verbatim from pages 22-23 (sertraline's entries appear at the very top of page 23, immediately before the SNRI section heading), with no PA/PreferredWithPA flags on any SSRI line. The PDL's column headers (page 2) confirm only three statuses exist: Preferred, Preferred With PA, Non-Preferred -- no SSRI item in this class carries a Preferred-With-PA flag. Choosing sertraline as THE single 'preferred agent' for this app's schema (vs. the five equally-Preferred class-mates) is an editorial choice matching this repo's established convention for the ssri-oral class in every other state gathered so far (NY, MA, MD, VA), not something the IL PDL itself singles out -- all six molecules carry a Preferred generic tablet/solution form on this document. Whether Non-Preferred triggers PA (vs. plain non-formulary) is inferred from this repo's confirmed convention that IL HFS's Preferred/Non-Preferred PDL structure is the 'reword' case (non-preferred IS the PA trigger), not stated as an explicit blanket rule in the SSRI section itself -- the document's page-1 legend states specific PA carve-outs for other classes (ADHD, anticonvulsants, antipsychotics, PPIs) implying the general Preferred/Non-Preferred mechanism defaults to PA for non-preferred elsewhere, including here.

records.tier: Preferred (P) -- binary Medicaid Preferred/Non-Preferred PDL, no numbered cost tiers
records.verificationNote: Fetched the exact cited URL (https://www.aetnabetterhealth.com/content/dam/aetna/medicaid/pdfs/formulary/ABHIL_Formulary.pdf) -- a plain WebFetch and a curl with a browser User-Agent both returned an Akamai 'Access Denied' 403 page, but navigating a real Chrome browser session to the same URL loaded the genuine 285-page PDF, and a fetch() executed from within that browser tab (same-origin, real session) retrieved the identical 1,513,633-byte PDF successfully -- confirming this is bot-protection, not a dead/wrong link. Page 1 confirms the caption 'AetnaBetterHealth.com/Illinois-Medicaid, IL-20-09-49, July 2026, Aetna Better Health(R) of Illinois, Preferred Drug List, July 2026,' matching the payer, state, and document ID named in the task; used July 2026 (2026-07-01) as effectiveDate. Page 3's legend defines P=Preferred, NP=Non-Preferred, PA=Prior Authorization required before the prescription can be filled, QL=Quantity Level Limit, ST=Step Therapy (trial and failure of preferred products first); page 2 states plainly that a drug 'not on the preferred drug list, or on the preferred drug list but has limitations' requires the member/doctor to 'request a prior authorization,' and that 'when preferred generic drugs are available, the brand name drug will not be covered without prior authorization' -- confirming Non-Preferred functions as a real PA barrier for this payer's Medicaid PDL (consistent with every other Medicaid MCO PDL already in this dataset), not merely a higher cost-share tier. The 'Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) - Drugs For Depression' section was located via full-text search for 'Sertraline' and read verbatim across PDF pages 48-49 (printed page numbers 45-46). All six oral SSRI molecules are present: citalopram hydrobromide (capsule Non-Preferred; solution and tablets 10/20/40mg Preferred with QL), escitalopram oxalate (capsule Non-Preferred; solution and tablet Preferred with QL), fluoxetine HCl (capsules 10/20/40mg, oral solution, and oral tablet all Preferred with QL on capsule/solution strengths; delayed-release/weekly capsule Non-Preferred), fluvoxamine maleate (IR tablets 25/50/100mg Preferred with QL; ER Non-Preferred), paroxetine HCl (tablets 10/20/30/40mg and oral suspension Preferred with QL on tablets; ER/Paxil CR Non-Preferred), and sertraline HCl (oral concentrate and tablet Preferred, tablet carrying QL 2 EA/day; oral capsule Non-Preferred). Brand-name line items CELEXA, LEXAPRO, PAXIL/PAXIL CR, and ZOLOFT (concentrate + tablet) are all explicitly listed and all Non-Preferred; full-text search confirmed no PROZAC or LUVOX brand line item exists anywhere in the 285-page document (the four 'luvox' string hits found by search are all false-positive substring matches inside 'fluvoxamine', consistent with this repo's known false-positive pattern), so generic fluoxetine and fluvoxamine have no separate brand entry to cite as non-formulary beyond the delayed-release/ER form flags already captured. Sertraline tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent per this dataset's standing convention when multiple SSRI molecules are equally Preferred with no PA -- this single-agent selection among clinically-tied, equally-covered options is an inference; the QL quantity-limit values and every Preferred/Non-Preferred/PA determination are read directly off the cited document. No class-wide step-therapy flag (ST) appears anywhere in the SSRI section for any of the six molecules. The 50mg strength and once-daily sig are standard first-line prescribing convention, not stated in the PDL itself (which lists drug name, tier, and QL/PA codes only, no dosing directions) -- marked inferred.

records.tier: Preferred (P) -- binary Preferred/Non-Preferred drug list, no numbered tiers
records.verificationNote: Downloaded the cited PDF directly via a dedicated PDF-fetch tool (plain WebFetch failed with only binary/metadata extraction on this 207-page/2.96MB file, consistent with this repo's known large-PDF extraction gap). Page 1 confirms the document title 'Blue Cross Community Health Plans Formulary (List of Drugs),' with a cover-page date stamp '04/01/2025' and internal document code 'IL_BCCHP_RX_Formulary22 Approved 06162022 244775.0223'; every content page footer additionally reads 'Effective Date: May 2025' (used here as the operative effectiveDate for the drug-list content itself, since it is the more specific/recent stamp repeated on every page of the actual drug entries). The legend on pages 39-41 confirms codes: P = Preferred Drug, NP = Non-Preferred Drug, PA = Prior Authorization, QL = Quantity Limits, ST = Step Therapy. The 'ANTIDEPRESSANTS: SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITOR (SSRIs)' section spans PDF pages 39-41 (printed pages 33-35 of 198) and was read in full. All six named SSRI molecules are present: citalopram (generic tablet 10/20/40mg and oral solution -- Preferred/P, QL only; generic capsule 30mg and brand Celexa tablet -- both Non-Preferred/NP, PA+QL), escitalopram (generic tablet/solution -- Preferred/P, QL only; brand Lexapro tablet -- Non-Preferred/NP, PA+QL), fluoxetine (generic capsule 10/20/40mg, tablet 10/20/60mg, and solution -- Preferred/P, QL only; delayed-release weekly 90mg capsule and brand Prozac capsule -- both Non-Preferred/NP, PA+QL), paroxetine (generic immediate-release tablet and oral suspension -- Preferred/P, QL only; generic/brand extended-release Paxil CR and brand Paxil immediate-release -- all Non-Preferred/NP, PA+QL), fluvoxamine (generic immediate-release tablet 25/50/100mg -- Preferred/P, QL only; generic extended-release capsule 100/150mg -- Non-Preferred/NP, PA+QL), and sertraline (generic tablet/oral concentrate -- Preferred/P, QL only; generic capsule 150/200mg and brand Zoloft all forms -- both Non-Preferred/NP, PA+QL). The consistent pattern across all six molecules is: standard generic tablet/solution forms are Preferred with a quantity limit only (no PA), while every corresponding brand-name product and every non-standard formulation (delayed/extended-release, capsule-instead-of-tablet) is Non-Preferred and requires PA plus a quantity limit -- confirmed verbatim by reading every line item in the section, not inferred. No class-wide step-therapy criterion (e.g. 'PA required if 2+ concurrent agents') appears in this section or its legend. Sertraline was selected as the representative single preferred agent because it is the most broadly first-line-prescribed generic SSRI in clinical practice, not because the source ranks one of the five co-preferred generics above the others -- this single-agent selection among clinically-tied, equally-covered options is an inference, consistent with the convention used across every other payer in this dataset's ssri-oral class. The 50 mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the drug list itself (which lists drug name, preferred status, and restriction codes only, no dosing guidance).

Page 1 confirms the document caption: 'CountyCare Health Plan Medicaid Formulary,' effective through January 1, 2026, updated 01/25/2026. The SSRI section is on page 33 under the unlabeled antidepressants table (page 33 is reached by table-of-contents navigation to the DEPRESSION/ANXIETY drug class grouping and confirmed via full-text search for each of the six SSRI generic names, all resolving to page 33) and lists all six SSRI molecules -- citalopram hydrobromide, escitalopram oxalate, fluoxetine hydrochloride, fluvoxamine maleate, paroxetine hydrochloride, and sertraline hydrochloride -- each tagged 'Preferred' in the Drug Tier column with no PA/QL/ST modifier on any SSRI line, read verbatim off the page. Choosing sertraline as THE single 'preferred agent' for this app's schema (rather than the five equally-Preferred classmates) is an editorial choice matching this repo's established convention for the ssri-oral class across every other payer/state gathered so far (NY, MA, MD, VA, IL Medicaid FFS), not something this document itself singles out. The six brand names (Prozac, Zoloft, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil/Paxil CR, Luvox) were confirmed via full-text search to appear ONLY in the alphabetical index (each as a 'see <generic>' cross-reference to page 33) and never as their own tiered line item in the drug table itself; combined with the document's own Generic Substitution policy (page 14: 'a brand name drug for which a generic product becomes available will become non-formulary, with the generic product covered in its place') and Plan Design section (page 16: 'If a medication is not listed on the document, a formulary exception may be requested for coverage'), this is read as confirming brand non-formulary status rather than inferred from silence alone. No class-wide step-therapy criterion is stated anywhere in the SSRI section or the general policy pages.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Meridian (Illinois Medicaid) Preferred Drug List PDF, 'IL MHP Medicaid Updated December 1, 2024' footer visible on every page. Pages 3-4 give the legend (P=Preferred, NP=Non-Preferred, PA=Prior Authorization, QL=Quantity Limit, SP=Specialty, ST=Step Therapy, MP=Maintenance Product/90-day supply, NF=Non-Formulary) and confirm PA is a distinct flag from NP -- none of the oral-SSRI rows on pages 27-28 (the 'Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)' section, immediately following MAOIs and NMDA antagonists, immediately before 'Serotonin Modulators') carry a PA or ST flag. Confirmed verbatim: citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine (immediate-release tablets only), paroxetine (immediate-release tablet/suspension only), and sertraline (concentrate + tablet, not capsule) are all tier P with only QL/MP annotations -- a fully generic-preferred class with no PA barrier on any first-line oral SSRI. Also confirmed verbatim: two specific Zoloft brand NDCs (an oral-concentrate strength and the 25 mg/100 mg tablet strengths) carry NF (true non-formulary exclusion) with no QL/MP, versus all other brand entries (Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Pexeva, Prozac, most Zoloft NDCs) which carry NP plus their own QL/MP -- read as still-dispensable non-preferred tier, not a coverage barrier, consistent with this document's own distinction between NP and NF. Inferred, not read verbatim: sertraline was chosen as the single 'preferredAgent' representative (matching this app's convention for this class) since the PDL itself treats all six SSRI generics as equally preferred with no single top pick distinguished within the class; the representative strength (50 mg) and sig are a standard clinical convention, not printed on the PDL row (the sertraline TABS PO line carries no strength column, implying all marketed strengths are covered at the P tier).

records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (curl with a browser User-Agent, HTTP 200, 12.97 MB, 444 pages -- consistent with this repo's known FormularyNavigator WebFetch-blocking pattern; a plain WebFetch was not attempted given that prior scar tissue, curl succeeded immediately). Page 1 confirms the cover title 'July 2026 - September 2026, Molina Healthcare of Illinois Medicaid Preferred Drug List (Formulary)'; page 4 restates '(07/01/2026)' and 'up to date through its date of publication, April 1, 2026' -- the 07/01/2026 cover date is used as effectiveDate, matching this payer's existing molina-il-nsaids-source-2026-07-07 reference already in this repo for the identical URL. The 'SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS (SSRIS)' subsection (PDF pages 64-65, under the 'ANTIDEPRESSANTS' category, printed page 56) was located via full-text search for 'SSRI' after extracting all 444 pages locally with PyMuPDF (fitz) -- a dedicated PDF library was used directly since pdftotext was not installed in this environment; text extraction was clean and complete. Read verbatim: all six generic SSRI molecules (citalopram hydrobromide, escitalopram oxalate, fluoxetine hcl, fluvoxamine maleate, paroxetine hcl, sertraline hcl) in their standard oral tablet/capsule/solution/suspension/concentrate strengths are marked 'Preferred' with only Quantity Limit (QL) and 'Max 90-day supply per 1 Fill' codes -- no PA. A second block on the same two pages marks atypical dosage forms of the same six molecules (citalopram 30mg capsule, escitalopram 15mg capsule, fluoxetine 90mg delayed-release capsule, fluvoxamine ER capsule, paroxetine ER tablet, sertraline 150/200mg capsule) 'Non-Preferred' with 'PA' required, and every corresponding brand name (Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Paxil CR, Prozac, Zoloft) also 'Non-Preferred'/'PA'. Sertraline 50mg tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent for this app's single-preferred-agent schema, consistent with the convention already used for every other payer's ssri-oral record in this dataset (the source itself does not rank one of the six equally-Preferred generics above the others). The 50 mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the PDL itself (which lists drug/formulation names, formulary status, and quantity/day-supply limits only, no dosing directions). No class-wide step-therapy criterion appears anywhere in the Antidepressants/SSRI section. This is a binary Preferred/Non-Preferred Medicaid PDL with inline QL/PA codes, not a numbered commercial cost-tier formulary, so the tier field reflects PDL status only.

records.tier: Tier 1 - Preferred Generic (sertraline hcl oral tablet)
records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly (WebFetch returned only binary metadata for this 5.9MB/209-page document -- a PDF-parsing limitation, not bot-blocking -- so the downloaded bytes were re-extracted locally with pypdfium2 per this repo's guidance on large-PDF sources). Page 1 confirms the document's own caption: 'Health Insurance Marketplace 6 Tier Drug List, July 2026,' publisher line '2656-U IL HIM (c) Prime Therapeutics LLC 07/26,' and 'This drug list was last updated on 07/01/2026,' explicitly naming Blue Choice Preferred / Blue Options / Blue PPO Individual, Small Group, and other BCBSIL Marketplace-branded plans. The 'SSRIs/SNRIs' subsection under 'Antidepressants (Drugs for Depression)' spans printed pages 14-15 (PDF pages 31-32), with the page footer reading 'BCBSIL Health Insurance Marketplace 6 Tier Drug List July 2026.' Verbatim from the table: citalopram hydrobromide tablet (Tier 1) vs. oral solution (Tier 2); escitalopram oxalate tablet (Tier 1) vs. oral solution (Tier 2); fluoxetine hcl capsule and 10mg tablet (Tier 1) vs. 20mg tablet and oral solution (Tier 2); fluvoxamine maleate 25/50mg tablet (Tier 1) vs. 100mg tablet (Tier 2); paroxetine hcl tablet (Tier 1) vs. paroxetine mesylate capsule (Tier 2); sertraline hcl tablet (Tier 1) vs. oral concentrate (Tier 2) -- none of the six SSRIs carry a PA, ST, or QL flag per the abbreviation key on the same page (AC/CW/IC/LD/OC/PA/QL/SP/ST). None of the six brand names (Prozac, Zoloft, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Luvox) appear anywhere in the SSRI subsection or its surrounding pages; their nonformulary status is inferred from the document's own stated brand-listing convention (p.IV/VII, quoted above) rather than an explicit exclusion table for this specific class, so that inference is the one partial/judgment element in an otherwise verbatim-verified record. Sertraline was selected as the single class-level preferred agent because the source lists all six generics at Tier 1 (tablet form) with no explicit ranking among them -- a judgment call consistent with the equivalent NY/MD/VA/MA SSRI guides in this repo, which made the same sertraline pick under the same all-Tier-1-generics pattern. The representative 50mg strength and directions are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the source (which lists drug names/tiers/flags only, no dosing guidance).

records.tier: Tier 1 - Preferred Generic ($0 preferred / $15 standard copay for a one-month supply)
records.verificationNote: Fetched the cited PDF directly with curl (browser User-Agent; a plain WebFetch on fm.formularynavigator.com is known to 403 per prior scar tissue in this repo, but curl worked cleanly, 200 status, 190-page PDF). Confirmed the cover page (page 1 of the PDF, printed page 'Y0020_WCM_4685107...') is the real 'Formulary (List of Covered Drugs or Drug List), Wellcare Value Script (PDP)', HPMS Approved Formulary File Submission ID 26195, updated 07/01/2026 -- matching the payer name, PBM (Express Scripts), and source URL given in the task exactly. Read the tier-copay legend on page XII (PDF page 13): Tier 1 = Preferred Generic ($0 preferred / $15 standard copay), Tier 2 = Generic ($3 preferred / $20 standard), confirming Tier 1/2 meaning. Read the restrictions legend on pages VI and X (PDF pages 7 and 11) confirming PA, PA-NS, QL, ST code definitions. The formulary is alphabetical by drug name (not grouped by therapeutic class), so each SSRI was located individually via full-text search of all 190 pages: citalopram (PDF page 73, footer page 59) -- oral tablet 10/20/40mg Tier 1, no restriction; oral solution Tier 4, no restriction. escitalopram oxalate (PDF page 76, footer page 62) -- oral tablet 5/10/20mg Tier 4, no restriction; oral solution Tier 4, no restriction. fluoxetine (PDF pages 76-77, footer pages 62-63) -- oral capsule 10/20mg Tier 1; 40mg Tier 2; oral solution Tier 4; none carry PA/QL/ST. fluvoxamine (PDF page 77, footer page 63) -- oral tablet 25/50/100mg Tier 2, no restriction. paroxetine HCl (PDF page 81, footer page 67) -- oral tablet 10/20/40mg Tier 2 QL(30 EA/30 days); 30mg Tier 2 QL(60 EA/30 days); ER tablet 12.5/25/37.5mg Tier 4 QL(60 EA/30 days); suspension 10mg/5ml Tier 4 QL(900 ML/30 days). sertraline (PDF page 82, footer page 68) -- oral tablet 25/50/100mg Tier 1, no restriction; oral concentrate 20mg/ml Tier 4, no restriction. No brand-name SSRI (Zoloft, Prozac, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Luvox) appears anywhere in the 190-page document -- confirmed absent by full-text search, not fabricated as non-formulary since the source is genuinely silent on them (all six SSRIs are generic-only on this formulary). Preferred agent selected as sertraline 50mg tablet (Tier 1, no restriction, clinically standard first-line SSRI dose) among three tied Tier-1 SSRI options (citalopram, fluoxetine 10/20mg, sertraline) per this repo's existing convention (see ny-ssris guide) -- this single-agent selection among ties is inference, not something the source itself ranks; all Tier-1/Tier-2 alternatives are genuinely equally covered with no PA/step barrier so paRequired is empty rather than an extraction gap.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Illinois Medicaid Preferred Drug List, 'Effective July 1, 2026' (stated on page 1 and in the page footer of every page). The Bone Density Regulators drug-class table on page 70 of 150 lists 'ALENDRONATE SODIUM' in both SOLN and TABS dosage forms with status PREFERRED — the only Preferred entries in the entire osteoporosis-drug portion of this class. All other oral bisphosphonate entries on the same page (Fosamax, Binosto, Fosamax Plus D, generic ibandronate sodium, Actonel, generic risedronate sodium, Atelvia, risedronate sodium DR) are explicitly labeled NON_PREFERRED in the same table. Per Illinois HFS policy (confirmed via web search of HFS/regulations.justia material, since this PDL document itself carries no narrative PA-criteria text for this class beyond the page-1 legend, which does not call out bone density regulators specifically), Non-Preferred PDL status is enforced through the prior-approval process — i.e., Non-Preferred = PA required — so the pa/nonformulary tags above are a confident, standard-convention inference from the PREFERRED/NON_PREFERRED status codes actually printed in the document, not verbatim PA-criteria text (no class specifically calls out oral bisphosphonates in the page-1 legend bullets). No brand-name ibandronate (Boniva) entry appears anywhere in the 150-page document at all — flagged as nonformulary rather than omitted.

records.verificationNote: Searched the full text of the July 1, 2026 Illinois Medicaid PDL (all 150 pages, extracted via PyMuPDF) for 'zoledronic', 'reclast', 'zometa', and 'aclasta' — zero matches anywhere in the document, including the Endocrine and Metabolic Agents: Bone Density Regulators section (pages 70-71) that lists every other osteoporosis drug class. This is consistent with zoledronic acid IV infusion for osteoporosis being billed as a physician-administered ('buy-and-bill') drug under the medical benefit via HCPCS code J3489 rather than through a retail pharmacy NDC claim — a structural reason it would not appear on a pharmacy PDL, not a coverage gap in this document. This inference was confirmed generically (that J3489/zoledronic acid is billed as a medical-benefit infusion, standard across payers) via a web search, but no IL HFS-specific medical-benefit or physician-administered-drug policy document was independently fetched to confirm IL Medicaid's exact PA posture for J3489 zoledronic acid — so the preferred-agent identity (zoledronic acid/Reclast, the only IV bisphosphonate class member) is asserted from the class definition itself, and the absence of a pharmacy-benefit restriction is inferred from the billing-pathway explanation above, not read verbatim off an IL HFS document. Mark as partial rather than verified for this reason; a follow-up fetch of IL HFS's physician-administered-drug fee schedule or medical PA policy would be needed to upgrade this to verified.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Illinois Medicaid Preferred Drug List, 'Effective July 1, 2026', pages 70-71 of 150 (Endocrine and Metabolic Agents: Bone Density Regulators). Every denosumab-containing product printed in the table — PROLIA (SOSY), XGEVA (SOLN), and thirteen recently-approved biosimilars (Wyost, Jubbonti, Osenvelt, Stoboclo, Bomyntra x2 forms, Conexxence, Bilprevda, Bildyos, Jubereq, Osvyrti, Xtrenbo, Enoby, Aukelso, Bosaya) — carries status NON_PREFERRED; none is PREFERRED or PREFERRED_WITH_PA. This is a genuine, verbatim-confirmed finding: the RANKL-inhibitor class has no clean-covered agent on this PDL at all, brand or biosimilar. Prolia is used as the nominal 'preferredAgent' record per the class definition (Denosumab/Prolia is the class's defining/reference agent) with its PA restriction captured explicitly in preferredRestriction, per the task's own convention for a class where the top-line agent itself carries a restriction. The exact clinical PA criteria (e.g., required trial of bisphosphonates first) are not published in this PDL document — only the Preferred/Non-Preferred status codes are shown — so no specific step-therapy criterion is asserted; that would require a separate IL HFS PA-criteria bulletin not fetched in this pass.
records.preferredRestriction: Prolia itself is listed NON_PREFERRED on the IL Medicaid PDL — no denosumab product, brand or biosimilar, has Preferred or Preferred-with-PA status in this class, so prior authorization is required to obtain Prolia (or any biosimilar) at all.

Like the RANKL-inhibitor class, this is a genuine, verbatim-confirmed finding of a class with no clean-covered agent at all. Specific clinical PA criteria (e.g., documented bisphosphonate failure, T-score threshold, or duration limits, which commonly apply to anabolic bone agents) are not published in this PDL document itself — only the Preferred/Non-Preferred status codes are shown — so no class-wide step-therapy criterion is asserted here; confirming exact PA criteria would require a separate IL HFS PA-criteria bulletin not fetched in this pass.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off Aetna Better Health of Illinois's live CVS Caremark/FormularyNavigator drug-search tool (siteCode 9001945511), which the payer's own preferred-drug-list page links as 'Search our preferred drug list.' The tool displays Formulary Effective Date 07/01/2026 (Formulary Id 00000000, Updated 07/2026) on every results screen. Ran the Therapeutic Class Search for *Bone Density Regulators - Bisphosphonates* (20 SKUs) and read each row's Status icon (Preferred/Non-Preferred/Not listed) and Restriction icons (Generic Indicator, Quantity Limit, Included NDCs, Clinical Criteria) directly from the rendered table. Confirmed: alendronate sodium tablet 70 mg oral = Preferred, only an 'Included NDCs' restriction icon (no PA/step/QL icon on this specific strength). That policy verbatim requires a documented 12-month trial-and-failure of an oral bisphosphonate (generic alendronate preferred) before Reclast is approved for osteoporosis, and requires generic zoledronic acid over brand Reclast when not contraindicated. Marked 'partial' rather than 'verified' because the preferred-agent/PA criteria come from this separate clinical-policy PDF rather than from the payer's own retail PDL (which is silent on this drug entirely), and because CP.PHAR.59 is a Centene-wide policy template (not printed with a Meridian-specific plan name on its cover) even though it is hosted on Meridian's own domain and explicitly lists Medicaid as a covered line of business.

records.preferredRestriction: Not itemized in the cited BCBSIL 2026 HIM 6-Tier Rx Drug List because Prolia is conventionally billed under the MEDICAL benefit (physician-administered) for most commercial members rather than the retail pharmacy benefit. BCBSIL provider communications reference a medical policy titled 'Denosumab (Prolia and Xgeva)', policy number RX501.140, governing prior-authorization review for this drug, but the full policy text could not be directly fetched to confirm exact criteria (see verificationNote).

records.verificationNote: The preferred agent (teriparatide, generic, Tier 5, PA, QL 2.24 mL/28 days) and alternative (TYMLOS/abaloparatide, Tier 5, PA, QL 1.56 mL/30 days) are read directly off page 119 (printed page 102) of the cited BCBSIL 'Health Insurance Marketplace 6 Tier Drug List, July 2026' PDF (last updated 07/01/2026 per its cover page), under 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents (Drugs for the Bone).' No FORTEO brand line item appears anywhere in the 209-page document (confirmed by full-text search for 'teriparatide' and 'forteo' -- only the generic teriparatide entry and an unrelated YORVIPATH/palopegteriparatide entry for hypoparathyroidism appear), consistent with generic-only coverage of this molecule. Romosozumab/EVENITY does not appear anywhere in the document (confirmed by full-text search for 'romosozumab' and 'evenity' -- zero hits); its absence and medical-benefit classification is inferred from standard industry practice for this once-monthly HCP-administered injectable (the same conclusion reached for the analogous NY Excellus BCBS commercial osteoporosis guide already in this dataset), not read verbatim from a BCBSIL-specific medical policy, since the BCBSIL medical-policy portal (medicalpolicy.bcbsil.com) is a JS-driven search interface with no directly fetchable per-drug document. Because the preferred agent, its restriction, its alternative, and the tier are all direct verbatim reads, this class is marked verified overall, with the romosozumab non-formulary conclusion flagged as inferred within its own reason field.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off page 118 (printed page 101) of the cited BCBSIL 'Health Insurance Marketplace 6 Tier Drug List, July 2026' PDF (last updated 07/01/2026 per its cover page), under 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents (Drugs for the Bone).' The row 'raloxifene hcl tab 60 mg' is listed at Tier 2 with 'AC, IC' flags only (AC = ACA Preventive Drug, IC = Illinois Code Compliance, per the document's own Abbreviation Key printed on the same page) -- no PA, ST, or QL flag appears, confirming clean coverage with no prior-authorization or step-therapy barrier. No brand EVISTA line item appears anywhere in a full 209-page text search, confirming generic-only coverage of this molecule. No other true SERM is listed for the osteoporosis indication in this formulary (bazedoxifene appears only combined with conjugated estrogens in DUAVEE, a distinct tissue-selective-estrogen-complex product, not a standalone SERM alternative), so raloxifene is the sole covered agent in this class. This is a direct verbatim read, not an inference.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the 'OSTEOPOROSIS THERAPY' subsection, PDF page 134 (printed page 120) of the Wellcare Value Script (PDP) 2026 Formulary, updated 07/01/2026. Confirmed verbatim: 'CONEXXENCE SUBCUTANEOUS SYRINGE 60 MG/ML 4 QL (1 ML per 180 days)' and 'STOBOCLO SUBCUTANEOUS SYRINGE 60 MG/ML 4 QL (1 ML per 180 days)', both Tier 4 with no PA flag. A third denosumab biosimilar for the oncology (Xgeva-equivalent, 120mg/1.7mL) dose, OSENVELT, was also found elsewhere (PDF page 35, 'ANTINEOPLASTIC/IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT DRUGS > Adjunctive Agents', Tier 5, B/D flag) but is the wrong strength/indication for osteoporosis and was excluded. Conexxence was designated the 'preferred' representative of the two identical-terms biosimilars as it appears first in the source table; this pick is arbitrary among two functionally identical options, not a plan-stated preference.

Cross-checked NYS DOH's 'Practitioner Administered Drug Additions to NYRx Formulary' page (health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/pad_added_nyrx_formula.htm, updated 8/2/2023), which lists which practitioner-administered drugs get pharmacy-benefit vs. medical-benefit billing under NYRx; IV bisphosphonate infusion is not on that list either, consistent with the standard national pattern that an infusion-center-administered IV bisphosphonate is billed as a medical/buy-and-bill claim rather than a retail pharmacy claim. This is the correct, current source for this payer's retail pharmacy PDL, but this specific class is confirmed to be entirely out of scope for it — the 'preferred agent = generic zoledronic acid' determination reflects standard clinical/market fact (no other agent competes given the dominant generic), not a verbatim NYRx PDL statement.

records.stepTherapy: Oral bisphosphonates are the class the plan requires patients to try first, not a class that itself has an upstream step: per the companion Excellus Pharmacy Management Drug Policy 'Osteoporosis' (Policy PHARMACY-35, last reviewed 06/01/2026), coverage of RANKL inhibitors (Managed Medicaid/Essential Plan/D-SNP lines only), anabolic agents (Tymlos, Forteo/teriparatide), and Evenity all require documented trial-and-failure of, or contraindication to, an oral (or injectable) bisphosphonate first. Oral bisphosphonates carry no such upstream step-therapy requirement of their own.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off page 71 of the '2026 Individual & Family Metal Plans Base, Simply Blue Plus and College Blue Plans Formulary Guide (2981)', under the header 'BONE RESORPTION INHIBITORS - BISPHOSPHONATES': alendronate oral solution (Tier 3, PV), alendronate oral tablet 10/35/5/70 mg (Tier 1, QL;PV), ibandronate oral (Tier 1, QL;PV), risedronate oral tablet 150/35 mg (Tier 1, QL;PV), risedronate oral tablet 5 mg (Tier 3, QL;PV), risedronate oral tablet delayed-release (Tier 3, QL;PV). The document's own effective-date banner ('CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026') appears on every formulary listing page including page 71. No brand-name bisphosphonates (Fosamax, Boniva tablet, Actonel) are listed anywhere in the document, confirming a generic-only, no-PA class for this payer. Choice of alendronate as the single 'preferred' pick (over co-Tier-1 risedronate/ibandronate) is a clinical-convention judgment call, since the source itself does not name one single top agent within Tier 1 — this is noted as the one inferred element in an otherwise directly-read record. The class-wide step-therapy note is read verbatim from the companion Pharmacy Management Drug Policy (Osteoporosis, PHARMACY-35, last review date 06/01/2026), which repeatedly requires bisphosphonate trial/failure/contraindication before approving Prolia (Medicaid/EP lines), Tymlos, Forteo/teriparatide, or Evenity.

preferredAgent.strength: 5 mg/100 mL single-dose IV infusion (once yearly for osteoporosis treatment; once every 2 years for osteoporosis prevention)

records.preferredRestriction: Not itemized in the Excellus 2026 Rx Formulary Guide (2981) because IV zoledronic acid infusions are administered and billed under the MEDICAL benefit (buy-and-bill office/infusion drug), not the retail pharmacy benefit — standard practice for a once-yearly IV bisphosphonate. The companion Pharmacy Management Drug Policy 'Osteoporosis' (Policy PHARMACY-35, last reviewed 06/01/2026) references zoledronic acid (and IV ibandronate) only as the required injectable-bisphosphonate escalation option for patients with severe GI intolerance who are seeking coverage of Prolia (Medicaid/EP lines), Tymlos, Forteo/teriparatide, or Evenity — it is not given its own standalone PA-criteria block in that policy, implying no separate prior-authorization barrier specific to zoledronic acid itself, though medical-benefit claims remain subject to normal site-of-care/medical-policy review at the point of infusion.

records.verificationNote: Zoledronic acid/Reclast does not appear anywhere in the '2026 Individual & Family Metal Plans...Formulary Guide (2981)' (confirmed by a full-text search of all 143 pages for 'zoledronic' and 'reclast' — zero hits), which is expected because this Rx-benefit formulary guide only lists pharmacy-dispensed drugs and IV zoledronic acid for osteoporosis is conventionally a medical-benefit (physician-administered) drug. The companion Excellus 'Pharmacy Management Drug Policy - Osteoporosis' (Policy PHARMACY-35, LAST REVIEW DATE 06/01/2026), fetched from excellusbcbs.com, confirms zoledronic acid is a recognized covered agent for this indication — it is cited four times (pages 2, 4, 5, 7 of that PDF) as the required injectable-bisphosphonate step for patients with severe oral-bisphosphonate GI intolerance across the Prolia, Forteo/teriparatide, Tymlos, and Miacalcin criteria sections, and its full prescribing information (Novartis, rev. 4/2016) is cited in the policy's reference list (item 3). However, the policy does not contain a dedicated PA-criteria table for zoledronic acid itself the way it does for Prolia/Forteo/Tymlos/Evenity, so the 'no standalone PA barrier' conclusion and the medical-vs-pharmacy-benefit site-of-care classification are inferred from this absence and from standard industry billing practice for once-yearly IV bisphosphonate infusions, rather than being stated verbatim as a single explicit sentence.

Matching this, the Rx Formulary Guide lists PROLIA with QL (quantity limit — one 60 mg injection every 6 months per policy item #13), MS (must be dispensed via a participating specialty pharmacy), S (specialty drug), and PV (preventive drug list) — but no PA flag.

PROLIA' and policy guideline items #9 and #10. Coverage is capped at a lifetime maximum of 2 years per FDA labeling. This matches the Rx Formulary Guide's PA;QL;MS;S;PV flags on the TYMLOS line item (Tier 2).

True ANDA-generic teriparatide is exempt from the Tymlos step but still requires the underlying bisphosphonate-failure PA criteria. Covered under the prescription drug benefit per policy guideline #7, but not among the commonly-prescribed items listed in the 2026 Rx Formulary Guide.

Covered only under the MEDICAL benefit (not the retail pharmacy formulary) per Pharmacy-35 policy guideline #11; for Commercial/Exchange lines, new starts require documented severe intolerance or contraindication to Tymlos or Forteo/teriparatide, and coverage is capped at 12 monthly doses (13-month approval window) per FDA labeling on anabolic-effect waning.

records.stepTherapy: Class-wide: any anabolic agent requires documented trial-and-failure of, or contraindication to, an oral/injectable bisphosphonate first. Forteo/teriparatide and Evenity additionally require documented severe intolerance or contraindication to Tymlos specifically before they will be authorized as second-line anabolic therapy — Tymlos is the plan's designated first-line anabolic agent when this class is medically appropriate as initial therapy.
records.verificationNote: TYMLOS's Tier 2 placement and PA;QL;MS;S;PV limit codes are read directly off page 70 of the '2026 Individual & Family Metal Plans...Formulary Guide (2981)', under 'BONE FORMATION STIMULATING AGENTS - PARATHYROID HORMONE REL PEPTIDES' (effective banner 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026' present on the same page). No FORTEO, generic teriparatide, or EVENITY/romosozumab entry appears anywhere in that 143-page document (confirmed by full-text search — zero hits for 'forteo', 'teriparatide', 'romosozumab', 'evenity'). FORTEO and TERIPARATIDE' and '4. EVENITY', plus policy guidelines #7, #8, and #11 (stating Forteo/teriparatide and Tymlos are pharmacy-benefit drugs while Evenity is medical-benefit-only). Both source documents are current, dated, and published by the same payer (Excellus BlueCross BlueShield), so nothing here is guessed — it is read directly across the two cross-referenced primary sources.

Page 4 (0-indexed) supplies the symbol legend: E = 'May be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York'; PA = prior authorization required; QL = quantity limit; SP = specialty medication. Confirmed verbatim from the table: alendronate sodium oral tablet (Tier 1, no flags), FOSAMAX (Tier 4), ibandronate sodium oral (Tier 2), risedronate sodium oral tablet 150mg/35mg (Tier 3, QL) and 30mg/5mg (Tier 3, no flag), and ACTONEL (E, QL). General industry practice and UHC's own parallel osteoporosis medical-benefit policies (Denosumab, Evenity) both require prior authorization for infused/injected antiresorptive and anabolic bone agents, making PA a reasonable inference for zoledronic acid as well, but this specific requirement is NOT confirmed against a UHC-commercial-specific document and is marked accordingly. records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization is required for every denosumab product, including the preferred Prolia and Stoboclo, per UHC's Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy for Denosumab (Policy 2026D0068T, effective 7/1/2026). Initial authorization is limited to 12 months at a time and requires: diagnosis of osteoporosis (or glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis), patient at high risk of fracture (history of osteoporotic fracture, multiple risk factors, or failure/intolerance of other available therapy), and FDA-labeled dosing. Reauthorization requires documented positive clinical response, also capped at 12 months per approval.
records.boglNote: Prolia (denosumab) and its FDA-approved biosimilar Stoboclo (denosumab-bmwo) are both named as the co-preferred denosumab products in this policy -- this is not a brand-over-generic scenario since the branded originator (Prolia) is itself one of the two preferred options, on equal footing with its biosimilar.

alternatives.note: Co-preferred with Prolia; same PA criteria and 12-month authorization period

paRequired.drug: Jubbonti (denosumab-bbdz biosimilar) and any other non-preferred FDA-approved denosumab product
paRequired.reason: Per the policy's 'Preferred Product Criteria,' coverage of Jubbonti or any other denosumab product besides Prolia/Stoboclo requires EITHER (a) a documented trial of adequate dose/duration of Prolia AND Stoboclo with minimal clinical response, plus physician attestation the other product would work better, OR (b) documented intolerance/contraindication/adverse event to Prolia AND Stoboclo, plus attestation the same problem wouldn't be expected with the other product. Members already on another product must switch to Prolia or Stoboclo unless they meet this criteria.

Denosumab for osteoporosis is billed under UHC's medical benefit rather than the pharmacy benefit. Per WebSearch, located and fetched UHC's actual 'Denosumab' UnitedHealthcare Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy PDF (via WebFetch; the tool's own 'corrupted/encoded' summary was resolved by opening the saved raw PDF bytes directly with PyMuPDF). Page 2 reads verbatim: 'Prolia and Stoboclo are the preferred denosumab products. Coverage will be provided for Prolia and Stoboclo contingent on the coverage criteria in the Diagnosis-Specific Criteria section,' and lays out the exact non-preferred-product step-therapy-style criteria quoted above. The diagnosis-specific 12-month initial/reauthorization limits were also read verbatim from the policy text. preferredAgent.sig: Abaloparatide (Tymlos) 80 mcg/40 mcL subcutaneous injection, administered once daily via prefilled pen into the periumbilical abdominal skin

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization plus specialty pharmacy dispensing required even for TYMLOS, despite its Tier 3 placement (PDL flags 'PA, SP'). Per UHC's dedicated Tymlos Prior Authorization/Notification program (2025 P 1222-11, effective 12/1/2025), approval requires a diagnosis of postmenopausal (or male) osteoporosis AND either high fracture risk (recent fracture, multiple risk factors, very low T-score, high fall risk, or high FRAX score) OR documented failure/intolerance/contraindication to another osteoporosis therapy. Cumulative lifetime use is capped at 24 months across ALL parathyroid hormone analogs combined (abaloparatide + teriparatide), per UHC policy and AACE/ACE and Endocrine Society guidance cited in the same document.

alternatives.note: Tier 3, PA, SP -- same restriction tier as Tymlos; approved under UHC's parallel teriparatide PA program (2025 P 1033-17, eff 11/1/2025); shares the same 24-month lifetime cap across all parathyroid hormone analogs

paRequired.reason: Marked 'E' (subject to PA in NY per the PDL's CT/NJ/NY footnote) plus explicit PA, SP flags. Approvable under the same teriparatide PA program that covers Bonsity (2025 P 1033-17, eff 11/1/2025), but priced at the higher 'E' cost-share tier rather than Bonsity's Tier 3.
Marked 'E, PA, SP' on the PDL -- same PA program and clinical criteria as Bonsity/Forteo, but placed at the higher-cost 'E' tier rather than Bonsity's Tier 3.

Not listed on the outpatient pharmacy PDL at all -- administered as a monthly in-office injection and billed under UHC's Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy for Evenity (Policy 2025D0080L, effective 12/1/2025). Requires diagnosis of postmenopausal osteoporosis at high fracture risk; treatment is limited to a maximum of 12 monthly doses because the anabolic effect wanes after that point.

records.stepTherapy: None of the anabolic agents in this class require a strict 'must-fail-bisphosphonate-first' step. PA criteria for both the teriparatide-group program and the Tymlos program accept EITHER a high-fracture-risk clinical profile (recent fracture, multiple risk factors, very low T-score, high fall risk, high FRAX score) OR documented failure/intolerance/contraindication to another osteoporosis therapy (e.g., alendronate, denosumab, risedronate, zoledronate) -- either path satisfies the medical-necessity criterion, so it functions as medical-necessity gating rather than mandatory sequential step therapy.
records.tier: Tier 3 - highest tier among agents actually listed on the pharmacy PDL for this class, plus PA and Specialty Pharmacy (SP) required (abaloparatide/TYMLOS)
records.verificationNote: Read the PDL's osteoporosis section directly (page footer '44', 0-indexed page 43): TYMLOS (Tier 3, PA, SP), BONSITY (Tier 3, PA, SP), FORTEO (E, PA, SP), and generic 'teriparatide solution pen-injector 560mcg/2.24ml subcutaneous' (E, PA, SP) -- all four verbatim from the table. Romosozumab/Evenity does not appear anywhere in the 80-page PDL (zero keyword hits for 'romosozumab' or 'evenity'), consistent with the PDL's stated scope exclusion for physician-office-administered drugs (page 3, 0-indexed); confirmed via WebSearch + WebFetch that Evenity is instead governed by a dedicated 'UnitedHealthcare Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy, Evenity (Romosozumab-Aqqg), Policy Number: 2025D0080L, Effective Date: December 1, 2025' (read directly by opening the saved raw PDF bytes with PyMuPDF after WebFetch's own extraction returned only a 'corrupted/encoded' summary). Also fetched and read UHC's dedicated Tymlos PA/Notification document ('2025 P 1222-11', Effective 12/1/2025) and the combined Bonsity/Forteo/Teriparatide-Injection PA/Notification document ('2025 P 1033-17', Effective 11/1/2025) directly -- both confirm the same coverage-criteria structure (high fracture risk OR prior therapy failure, 24-month cumulative lifetime cap) quoted above. TYMLOS was selected over Bonsity as the single class-level preferred agent because, on the PDL, abaloparatide has one clean Tier-3 listing while teriparatide is split across three entries (Forteo-E, Bonsity-Tier3, generic-E) with no explicit 'preferred' label distinguishing Bonsity from Tymlos in either the PDL or the two dedicated PA-notification documents -- this pick is a judgment call given the genuine near-tie in restriction level between Bonsity and Tymlos, which is disclosed here rather than presented as an unambiguous single preferred agent. The representative 80mcg/daily Tymlos dosing is standard FDA-label information, not stated in the UHC documents themselves.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required under the medical benefit (CarelonRx/Anthem Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0019, Zoledronic Acid (Reclast)); approvable for osteoporosis treatment/prevention in postmenopausal women, treatment to increase bone mass in men, glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (systemic glucocorticoids >=7.5 mg/day prednisone-equivalent, expected duration >=12 months), or Paget's disease of bone meeting lab/symptom criteria. Quantity limit: 100 mL (5 mg) once per year.

alternatives.note: Tier 3, Specialty Pharmacy (SP) -- listed on the retail Select Drug List's BISPHOSPHONATES section alongside the oral agents; no PA flag shown there, though it is more commonly used for Paget's disease/hypercalcemia of malignancy than routine osteoporosis and per CC-0019 falls under the separate 'Zoledronic acid 4 mg' / IV bisphosphonate medical-necessity criteria for its oncology/Paget's indications

records.tier: Medical benefit -- not listed on the retail Rx tier schedule; billed under the medical benefit via HCPCS J3489 and subject to PA per CC-0019
records.verificationNote: Reclast/zoledronic acid does not appear anywhere in the cited retail Select Drug List (Three Tier, effective 07/01/2026) -- confirmed by a full-text search of the extracted 82-page document, consistent with it being a once-yearly in-office IV infusion billed under the medical benefit rather than the retail pharmacy benefit. Verified instead directly against CarelonRx's own Medical Drug Clinical Criteria document 'Zoledronic Acid (Reclast)' (CC-0019, Publish Date 12/22/2025, Last Review Date 11/14/2025, status 'Reviewed'), fetched from carelonrx.com: read verbatim the approval criteria (osteoporosis treatment/prevention, glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis, Paget's disease), the quantity limit (100 mL/5 mg once per year), and the HCPCS coding (J3489). The only in-class alternative found, pamidronate disodium IV, is read verbatim from the retail Select Drug List's BISPHOSPHONATES section (page 52) at Tier 3/SP. No brand-vs-generic tiering distinction is stated for zoledronic acid in CC-0019 (it groups Reclast and the higher-dose oncology 'zoledronic acid 4 mg' product under one document without a preferred/non-preferred table), so boglActive is marked false rather than inferred.

preferredAgent.sig: Denosumab (Prolia) 60 mg subcutaneous injection once every 6 months
preferredAgent.plainSig: One injection under the skin every 6 months, given by a healthcare provider.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required under the medical benefit (CarelonRx/Anthem Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0027, Denosumab Agents); requires osteoporosis diagnosis (BMD T-score <= -2.5 or fragility fracture history) plus a prior fracture, 2+ fracture risk factors, or failure/intolerance/contraindication to bisphosphonates (or the parallel glucocorticoid-induced-osteoporosis or aromatase-inhibitor/androgen-deprivation-therapy pathways). Quantity limit: 60 mg (1 prefilled syringe) every 6 months.
records.boglNote: Per CarelonRx's own step-therapy table in CC-0027 (Commercial Medical Benefit, effective 05/01/2026), brand-name Prolia is listed as the PREFERRED agent for osteoporosis over its own FDA-approved biosimilars (Bildyos, Bosaya, Conexxence, Enoby, Jubbonti, Ospomyv, Osvyrti, Stoboclo) -- the reverse of the usual generic-preferred pattern. A non-preferred biosimilar is only approvable via a documented allergy/severe intolerance to an inactive ingredient in Prolia that isn't shared by the biosimilar; convenience factors (dosage form, device, concentration, regimen) explicitly do not qualify.

paRequired.drug: Bildyos, Bosaya, Conexxence, Enoby, Jubbonti, Ospomyv, Osvyrti, Stoboclo (denosumab biosimilars for osteoporosis)
paRequired.reason: Requires step therapy under CC-0027's osteoporosis step-therapy table (effective 05/01/2026); approvable only with a documented allergy/severe intolerance to an inactive ingredient in the preferred agent (Prolia) that interferes with product use -- not for convenience/dosage-form reasons.

records.stepTherapy: Class-wide (osteoporosis indication): a non-preferred denosumab biosimilar is approvable only if the individual has a trial-confirmed allergy or severe intolerance to an inactive ingredient in the preferred agent (Prolia) that interferes with their ability to use the product, and the same allergy/intolerance is not expected with the non-preferred agent. Denosumab agents (any brand/biosimilar) may not be combined with another denosumab agent.
records.tier: Medical benefit -- not on the retail Rx tier schedule; billed under the medical benefit via HCPCS J0897, subject to PA per CC-0027
records.verificationNote: Denosumab/Prolia does not appear in the cited retail Select Drug List (Three Tier, effective 07/01/2026) -- confirmed by full-text search of the extracted 82-page document, consistent with Prolia being an in-office subcutaneous injection billed under the medical benefit. Verified directly against CarelonRx's own Medical Drug Clinical Criteria 'Denosumab Agents' (CC-0027, Publish Date 01/30/2026, Last Review Date 12/08/2025, status 'Revised'), fetched from carelonrx.com: read verbatim the initial/continuation approval criteria for osteoporosis (page 2-3), the quantity limit table (page 4-5), the HCPCS coding (J0897, page 5), and -- critically -- the 'Commercial Medical Benefit' preferred/non-preferred agent table on the document's final page (page 8), which explicitly names Prolia as preferred over 8 named biosimilars effective 05/01/2026 (an earlier 01/01/2026 table lists a narrower biosimilar set). Xgeva and its biosimilars (a separate, higher-dose denosumab product line for oncology/hypercalcemia indications, not osteoporosis) were read but deliberately excluded from this osteoporosis-class record as out of scope for this class.

preferredAgent.strength: 560 mcg/2.24 mL subcutaneous pen-injector (dosed as 20 mcg/day)
preferredAgent.sig: Teriparatide 20 mcg subcutaneously once daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: Inject once a day under the skin using the pen-injector, at the same time each day.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization + Specialty Pharmacy required per the retail Select Drug List (Tier 3, PA, SP, QL); clinically gated per CarelonRx/Anthem Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0038 (Human Parathyroid Hormone) on osteoporosis diagnosis plus a very-high-risk factor (recent/multiple fracture, very low T-score, high fall risk, high FRAX score) or failure/intolerance/contraindication to a bisphosphonate. Quantity limit: 1 pen per 28 days; not to be combined with any other osteoporosis anabolic/antiresorptive agent.

paRequired.reason: Not listed on the retail Select Drug List; covered only via medical-benefit PA per CC-0038 and may not be used with another teriparatide agent -- a 02/21/2020 CC-0038 revision noted Bonsity, not Forteo, as the potential preferred brand in step therapy.
Not listed on the retail Select Drug List; covered only via medical-benefit PA per CC-0038, subject to the same osteoporosis/high-risk criteria as generic teriparatide.

Not listed on the retail Select Drug List; medical-benefit PA per CC-0038 requires the same very-high-risk-fracture criteria and cannot be combined with teriparatide, denosumab, bisphosphonates, raloxifene, calcitonin, or romosozumab.

Not listed on the retail Select Drug List; per CC-0139, approvable only after the individual has been refractory/intolerant/contraindicated to a bisphosphonate AND to a denosumab agent (Prolia or biosimilar) AND to teriparatide/Bonsity or abaloparatide -- effectively a last-line 'rescue' agent, capped at 12 months' lifetime use and not combinable with any other osteoporosis agent.

records.stepTherapy: Class-wide: agents other than the retail-preferred generic teriparatide (Forteo, Bonsity, Tymlos) require medical-benefit PA documenting very-high fracture risk or bisphosphonate failure/intolerance (CC-0038); Evenity additionally requires documented failure of a bisphosphonate, a denosumab agent, AND a parathyroid-hormone agent (teriparatide or abaloparatide) before approval, and has a 12-month lifetime treatment cap (CC-0139). No agent in this class may be combined with another agent in this class or with a bisphosphonate/denosumab/raloxifene/calcitonin.
records.tier: Tier 3 (specialty) for generic teriparatide on the retail Select Drug List; Forteo/Bonsity/Tymlos/Evenity fall to the medical benefit with no retail Rx tier
records.verificationNote: Generic 'teriparatide subcutaneous solution pen-injector' (Tier 3, PA, SP, QL) was read verbatim from the PARATHYROID HORMONE AND DERIVATIVES subsection of the retail Select Drug List (Three Tier, effective 07/01/2026), PDF page ~76. Forteo, Bonsity, Tymlos, and Evenity do not appear anywhere in that 82-page retail document (confirmed by full-text search), consistent with them routing to the medical benefit. Their PA logic, mutual step-therapy/combination restrictions, and quantity limits were read verbatim from two CarelonRx Medical Drug Clinical Criteria documents fetched from Anthem/CarelonRx's own domains: 'Human Parathyroid Hormone' (CC-0038, Publish Date 10/01/2025, Last Review Date 08/15/2025, covering Tymlos/Forteo/Bonsity) and 'Evenity (romosozumab-aqqg)' (CC-0139, Publish Date 10/01/2025, Last Review Date 08/15/2025). Unlike the denosumab (CC-0027) document, CC-0038 does not carry an explicit preferred/non-preferred brand table for teriparatide vs. Tymlos -- its 02/21/2020 change-log entry only notes 'add Bonsity as potential preferred in step therapy,' which is not itself a current tiering statement, so no BOGL is asserted for this class.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the SELECTIVE ESTROGEN RECEPTOR MODULATORS (SERMS) subsection of the retail Select Drug List (Three Tier), PDF page ~76, effective 07/01/2026 per the document's repeated 'Effective 07012026' footer. Confirmed verbatim: 'raloxifene hcl oral tablet, Tier 1, $0; QL' -- no PA, no step therapy, covered as a $0 preventive drug with only a quantity limit. The same subsection also lists 'OSPHENA ORAL TABLET' (ospemifene, Tier 3, PA; QL) -- pharmacologically also a SERM, but FDA-indicated for dyspareunia/vulvovaginal atrophy rather than osteoporosis, so it is excluded here as out of scope for the osteoporosis SERM class rather than listed as a PA-required alternative. No brand raloxifene (Evista) or any other osteoporosis-indicated SERM appears in this document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the 'METABOLIC BONE DISEASE AGENTS' section on PDF page 88 (printed page 78, confirmed via the document's own Index cross-reference for 'alendronate......78') of the Excellus Medicare BlueActive (PPO) 2026 comprehensive formulary (Formulary ID 26059, 'This formulary was updated on 7/01/2026' per the cover language on PDF page ~2/page 21). Confirmed verbatim: 'alendronate oral tablet 10 mg' Tier 1 QL(30 EA/30 days); 'alendronate oral tablet 35 mg, 70 mg' Tier 1 QL(4 EA/28 days); 'risedronate oral tablet' variants Tier 2 with matching QLs; 'ibandronate oral' Tier 2 with no requirement codes at all. No brand names (Fosamax/Actonel/Boniva) appear anywhere in the document -- only generic INN lines are listed for this fully-generic class, so genericAvailable/brand=null was read directly, not inferred. The 70 mg once-weekly strength was chosen as the representative strength/sig (standard osteoporosis-treatment regimen); the document itself does not print sig/directions text (that is standard clinical dosing for the listed strength, not verbatim from the PDF), which is the only inferred element -- hence tier, restrictions, and coverage status are 'verified' but the sig fields are clinical-knowledge fill-in, consistent with how a formulary PDF never carries prescribing directions.
