records.preferredRestriction: Not listed anywhere in this Part D drug list (confirmed by full-text search of all 108 pages for 'zoledronic' and 'Reclast' -- zero hits). As a physician-administered IV infusion typically billed 'incident to' an office visit, zoledronic acid for osteoporosis is commonly adjudicated under Medicare Part B (medical benefit) rather than carried on the Part D formulary for Medicare Advantage prescription drug (MAPD) plans like this one -- coverage and cost-sharing for this agent would need to be confirmed against the plan's Part B medical benefit, not this Part D document.
records.verificationNote: The absence of zoledronic acid/Reclast is verified directly and exhaustively: the full extracted text of all 108 pages of the Excellus Medicare BlueActive (PPO) 2026 comprehensive formulary (Formulary ID 26059, effective/updated 7/01/2026 per the document's own cover language) was grepped for 'zoledronic', 'reclast', and 'zometa' with zero matches, confirming this is a genuine gap in the Part D document rather than a missed section (contrast with the adjacent 'METABOLIC BONE DISEASE AGENTS' section on PDF page 88/printed page 78, which does list alendronate, risedronate, ibandronate, PROLIA, EVENITY, and teriparatide by name -- zoledronic acid is conspicuously absent from that same list). What is INFERRED rather than read verbatim is the specific reason (Part B medical-benefit carve-out): this document does use an explicit 'B/D PA' code elsewhere for some physician-administered drugs it DOES choose to list (e.g. tobramycin inhalation solution, aprepitant, ondansetron), so zoledronic acid's total absence (no B/D-flagged line at all) is consistent with, but not explicitly confirmed by, this document as a categorical Part-B-only exclusion for annual osteoporosis dosing -- that clinical/regulatory interpretation is standard industry knowledge for MAPD plans, not a sentence printed in this PDF, hence 'partial' rather than 'verified'.

The line reads exactly 'PROLIA 4' with no requirement code at all -- confirmed no PA, QL, or step-therapy flag is attached, so both tier (Tier 4) and clean-coverage status are read verbatim, not inferred. XGEVA also denosumab (same molecule, brand-only, PA-required, Tier 5) appears two lines below in the same section, but XGEVA is dosed and FDA-labeled for oncologic bone-metastasis prevention (120 mg monthly) rather than osteoporosis (60 mg twice-yearly) -- it is a different clinical product/indication under the same INN, not an interchangeable alternative for this class, so it was deliberately excluded from alternatives/paRequired rather than conflating the two denosumab products. No denosumab biosimilar (e.g. Jubbonti, Osenvelt) appears anywhere in the document (full-text searched), so genericAvailable=false reflects this specific formulary, not the broader US market.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the 'METABOLIC BONE DISEASE AGENTS' section on PDF page 88 (printed page 78, cross-referenced against Index entries 'teriparatide.......................................78' and 'EVENITY..........................................78') of the Excellus Medicare BlueActive (PPO) 2026 comprehensive formulary (Formulary ID 26059, effective/updated 7/01/2026). Confirmed verbatim: 'teriparatide 5 PA; QL (3 ML per 28 days)' and 'EVENITY 5 PA; QL (2.4 ML per 28 days)' -- both Tier 5, both PA+QL, no brand name (Forteo) printed for teriparatide (generic-only line, same pattern as the oral bisphosphonate class). Teriparatide was chosen as the 'preferred' pick over Evenity because it is the fully-generic, lower-priced, longer-established option even though both carry identical PA/QL codes in this document -- the document itself does not rank the two against each other, so this preference ordering is a reasonable inference, not a stated payer preference; the coverage/restriction facts for each drug (tier, PA, QL) are read verbatim. abaloparatide (Tymlos) was confirmed absent via full-text search across all 108 extracted pages -- zero mentions of 'abaloparatide' or 'Tymlos' anywhere in the document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the 'Selective Estrogen Receptor Modifying Agents' section on PDF page 80 (printed page 70, cross-referenced against the document's own Index entry 'raloxifene......................................... 70') of the Excellus Medicare BlueActive (PPO) 2026 comprehensive formulary (Formulary ID 26059, effective/updated 7/01/2026). The line reads exactly 'raloxifene 2 QL (30 EA per 30 days)' -- Tier 2, quantity limit only, no PA/step-therapy code, all read verbatim. No brand name (Evista) is printed anywhere in the document -- generic-only line, same pattern as alendronate and teriparatide. The section also lists clomiphene citrate, DUAVEE (bazedoxifene/conjugated estrogens), and MILOPHENE, but these are infertility (clomiphene/Milophene) or menopausal-symptom/osteoporosis-PREVENTION (DUAVEE, a tissue-selective estrogen complex, not indicated for osteoporosis TREATMENT) products with a different clinical use than raloxifene's osteoporosis-treatment SERM role -- deliberately excluded from alternatives/paRequired to avoid conflating differently-indicated drugs in the same PDL section.

records.verificationNote: The task's supplied URL (id=23) resolved to 'Table 23: Respiratory Agents - Inhaled' (last revised 07/2026), not osteoporosis -- confirmed directly by WebFetch. A follow-up WebSearch ('MassHealth Drug List MHDL osteoporosis table pubtheradetail.do') surfaced the correct page at id=49, 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents,' last revised 05/2026 (day-of-month not stated by the source; recorded as 2026-05-01). Read directly from the 'Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents - Bisphosphonates' subsection: alendronate tablet (Fosamax) No PA, alendronate oral solution PA Required, alendronate effervescent (Binosto) PA Required, alendronate/cholecalciferol (Fosamax Plus D) PA Required, ibandronate tablet No PA, ibandronate injection PA Required, risedronate (Actonel) PA Required, risedronate delayed-release (Atelvia) PA Required, pamidronate No PA, zoledronic acid 4mg/5mg No PA. Also read verbatim the PA-criteria footnotes: alendronate solution/Binosto require documented tube-feeding/swallowing-disorder/age<13 medical necessity; risedronate requires documented inadequate response to or adverse reaction to an oral bisphosphonate; M90 (mandatory 90-day supply) applies to the standard tablet forms. Choosing alendronate over co-equal no-PA ibandronate as the single 'preferred' pick is an inference from clinical-guideline convention (matches this project's existing NY/VA/MD osteoporosis records), since the source itself does not rank within the no-PA set -- everything else in this record (PA outcomes, footnote criteria, M90 designation) is read verbatim from the cited table.

MassHealth does not pay for this drug to be dispensed through the retail pharmacy.' Ibandronate injection (distinct from the ibandronate tablet, which is No PA under the oral-bisphosphonate class) is listed 'PA Required.' Note: zoledronic acid 5 mg (Reclast) also appears as one of three required 'trial/failure/contraindication' comparator agents (alongside a menopausal hormonal agent and raloxifene) in the PA criteria for Duavee's osteoporosis-prevention indication -- read verbatim from the source but out of scope for this record's own class. Selection of the 5 mg/yearly dose as the 'preferred' representative strength (over the 4 mg dose used for other indications) reflects standard osteoporosis dosing convention, not a payer-stated ranking -- everything else in this record is read verbatim.

records.preferredRestriction: PA required for all denosumab osteoporosis products (Prolia and biosimilars Wyost, Osenvelt, Stoboclo, Bomyntra, Conexxence, Bildyos, Bilprevda, Xtrenbo, Enoby). Documentation required: appropriate diagnosis; AND one of -- inadequate response to an adequate trial of, or adverse reaction to, an oral bisphosphonate; OR member at very high risk for fracture (per specified fracture-history/T-score/fall-risk criteria); OR contraindication to all oral bisphosphonates. For at least one of the listed products, the criteria additionally require clinical rationale for using that agent instead of Enoby, suggesting Enoby is MassHealth's cost-preferred denosumab product among the biosimilar options -- flagged as a detail worth re-verifying at prescribing time rather than a fully parsed rule.

paRequired.reason: Xgeva (denosumab 120 mg, monthly) is dosed and indicated for cancer-related bone disease (bone metastases, giant cell tumor of bone), not standard postmenopausal/male osteoporosis dosing; requires its own PA under different criteria than the 60 mg/6-month osteoporosis product.

records.stepTherapy: PA for denosumab in osteoporosis requires documented inadequate response/adverse reaction to an oral bisphosphonate, OR very-high-fracture-risk criteria, OR contraindication to all oral bisphosphonates -- i.e., an oral bisphosphonate trial/failure/contraindication is the class-wide step ahead of denosumab.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Not Otherwise Classified' subsection: 'Denosumab agents (Prolia, Wyost, Osenvelt, Stoboclo, Bomyntra, Conexxence, Bildyos, Bilprevda, Xtrenbo, Enoby) - PA Required' and 'Denosumab-Xgeva - PA Required' as two separate line items. The PA-criteria text block for denosumab was read verbatim, including a clause requiring 'clinical rationale for use of the requested agent instead of Enoby' for certain denosumab products -- this implies a preferred-biosimilar structure within the denosumab class itself, but the exact scope of which products trigger that clause (all non-Enoby products, or only some) was not fully disaggregated from the source in this pass, so it is captured as a flagged detail in preferredRestriction rather than a fully resolved rule. genericAvailable is set to true because MassHealth's own table lists multiple denosumab biosimilars alongside Prolia (a materially different fact from this project's earlier NY-medicaid record, which predates current biosimilar availability). Choosing Prolia as the headline brand (over Enoby, the seemingly cost-preferred biosimilar) follows this project's convention of using the best-known reference brand name for display, not a payer ranking statement.

records.preferredRestriction: PA required for all anabolic bone-building agents (teriparatide/Forteo/Bonsity, abaloparatide/Tymlos, romosozumab/Evenity). Documentation required: appropriate diagnosis; bone mineral density indicating osteoporosis; AND one of -- inadequate response to an adequate trial of, or adverse reaction to, an oral bisphosphonate; OR member at very high risk for fracture (specified fracture-history/T-score/fall-risk criteria); OR contraindication to all oral bisphosphonates; AND additionally one of -- diagnosis of severe osteoporosis (per specified criteria); OR inadequate response, adverse reaction, or contraindication to all of: denosumab, ibandronate injection, and zoledronic acid 5 mg. This is a two-tier step: bisphosphonate failure/contraindication first, then either severe-osteoporosis criteria or denosumab/ibandronate-injection/zoledronic-acid failure on top.

alternatives.note: Same two-tier PA criteria as teriparatide; equally covered once the documentation requirements are met.

records.stepTherapy: Class-wide: all three anabolic agents require a documented trial/failure/contraindication of an oral bisphosphonate first, PLUS either meeting defined 'severe osteoporosis' criteria or documented failure/contraindication to denosumab, ibandronate injection, and zoledronic acid 5 mg (Reclast) on top -- effectively a second-line-after-second-line step above the RANKL-inhibitor class's own bisphosphonate-first step.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Not Otherwise Classified' subsection: 'Abaloparatide (Tymlos) - PA Required', 'Forteo (teriparatide) - PA Required', 'Bonsity (teriparatide) - PA Required', 'Evenity (romosozumab) - PA Required'. PA-criteria text for teriparatide and for abaloparatide/romosozumab was read verbatim and confirmed near-identical in structure (bisphosphonate-failure-or-very-high-risk-or-contraindication, PLUS severe-osteoporosis-or-denosumab/ibandronate-injection/zoledronic-acid-failure); the exact numeric thresholds defining 'severe osteoporosis' and 'very high risk for fracture' were seen in the source but not fully transcribed digit-for-digit in this pass, so the stepTherapy/preferredRestriction text summarizes the structure rather than quoting every criterion verbatim -- flagged as the one inferred/summarized element. Deliberately excluded 'Palopegteriparatide (Yorvipath)' from this record even though it appears PA-Required on the same MHDL table: Yorvipath is FDA-indicated for chronic hypoparathyroidism, not osteoporosis, so its presence on this general 'Bone Metabolism Agents' table appears to be a table-scope artifact (the table covers bone metabolism broadly, not only osteoporosis) rather than a genuine osteoporosis anabolic-agent option -- flagging this rather than silently including a non-osteoporosis drug as an 'alternative,' per this project's standing instinct to sanity-check extracted entries against real drug-indication facts. Choosing teriparatide/Forteo as the single 'preferred' pick (all three require identical-structure PA, none is open) follows this project's existing NY-medicaid-anabolic convention.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Not Otherwise Classified' subsection: 'Raloxifene (Evista) - No PA', carrying an 'M90' drug-note designation (mandatory 90-day supply after initial fill), with no '#' brand-requires-PA symbol against this line -- unlike alendronate, MassHealth's own table does not require PA for brand Evista here, so both generic and brand are captured as no-PA. All three oral bisphosphonate generics -- alendronate sodium (tablet 10/35/70mg and oral solution 70mg/75mL), risedronate sodium (tablet 5/30/35/150mg, including delayed-release 35mg), and ibandronate sodium (tablet 150mg) -- are listed in lowercase (this document's convention for generic-preferred entries) at Tier 1 with no PA or ST notation; only the oral solution and the 30mg risedronate tablet carry a quantity limit. No brand-name oral bisphosphonate (Fosamax, Actonel, Atelvia, Boniva) appears anywhere in the document, confirming generic-only coverage with no BOGL. Document cover states 'Effective Jan. 1, 2026' and 'Created: October 1, 2025'; each page footer additionally shows 'Last Update: 07/15/26 / Next Update: 08/15/26', indicating this specific PDF snapshot was regenerated within the last week (fetched 2026-07-21), so it reflects current, live formulary content, not a stale archive. Alendronate 70mg once-weekly was selected as the single preferred representative (over risedronate/ibandronate) because it is the most commonly prescribed first-line oral bisphosphonate in clinical practice; the sig is standard bisphosphonate-class dosing instruction, not itself read verbatim from this formulary document (the PDF lists drug/tier/limitations only, not prescribing directions), so the sig fields are marked as inferred from standard clinical dosing convention rather than sourced from the PDF.

This is the expected pattern for an IV bisphosphonate: zoledronic acid/Reclast is typically physician-administered as an office/infusion-center infusion and billed under the medical benefit (buy-and-bill), not adjudicated through the retail/mail pharmacy benefit this PDL governs, so its absence from a pharmacy drug list is a structural gap rather than a document error. The preferred-agent identity (zoledronic acid/Reclast as the sole IV bisphosphonate on the market) is standard clinical/drug-class knowledge, not something inferred from this document; the tier, PA-required, and alternatives fields are left null/empty because this pharmacy source states nothing about medical-benefit coverage terms for this class. records.verificationNote: Read directly from the cited Harvard Pilgrim/OptumRx 2026 Select 3-Tier Prescription Drug List, 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents - Drugs for Osteoporosis' section (document page 135-136). Confirmed verbatim: 'teriparatide solution pen-injector 560 mcg/2.24ml subcutaneous' and the identically-worded all-caps entry 'TERIPARATIDE SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR 560 MCG/2.24ML SUBCUTANEOUS' are both Tier 3, PA required, QL max 1 pen per 28 days, must use Optum Specialty, not available through home delivery; 'BONSITY SUBCUTANEOUS SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR 560 MCG/2.24ML' (teriparatide biosimilar) carries identical Tier 3/PA/QL 1-per-28-days terms and is treated as functionally interchangeable with the generic teriparatide entry rather than a separate alternative. 'TYMLOS SUBCUTANEOUS SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR 3120 MCG/1.56ML' (abaloparatide) is also Tier 3 with PA and QL 1 pen per 30 days -- since it carries its own PA barrier it is listed under paRequired rather than alternatives per the schema's rule that PA-gated drugs never belong in alternatives. Romosozumab (Evenity) does not appear anywhere in the document (checked the full osteoporosis section and searched the entire document text); its absence is flagged as nonformulary rather than inferred coverage. Teriparatide was selected as the preferred agent because it is the only agent in this class with a true generic/biosimilar option (Bonsity), even though PA still applies class-wide; this PA-even-for-preferred fact is captured in preferredRestriction per the task instructions. Document cover page states 'Created: October 1, 2025' with page footers showing 'Last Update: 07/15/26'.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the cited Harvard Pilgrim/OptumRx 2026 Select 3-Tier Prescription Drug List, 'Hormonal Agents - Selective Estrogen Receptor Modifying Agents' section (document page 116). Confirmed verbatim: 'raloxifene hcl oral tablet 60 mg' is Tier 1 with no PA/ST/QL restriction (HSA-preventive and ACA flags only, which do not gate coverage). 'OSPHENA ORAL TABLET 60 MG' (ospemifene) appears in the same formulary section at Tier 3 with no explicit PA noted, but ospemifene is FDA-indicated for postmenopausal dyspareunia/vulvovaginal atrophy, not osteoporosis, so it was excluded as an osteoporosis-class alternative rather than misrepresented as a covered substitute for raloxifene's bone indication. No other SERM (e.g., bazedoxifene, tamoxifen for osteoporosis) appears in this section. Raloxifene is the only oral SERM FDA-approved and marketed specifically for osteoporosis prevention/treatment, so it is both the sole and preferred agent in this class with clean, unrestricted Tier 1 coverage. Document cover page states 'Created: October 1, 2025' with page footers showing 'Last Update: 07/15/26'.

'Prolia Subcutaneous Solution Prefilled Syringe' -> Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 SYR per 6 Months) -- confirmed verbatim. Xgeva (denosumab at the oncology 120mg/28-day dose) appears separately at Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 VIAL per 28 days) and is a different indication/dose, correctly excluded from this osteoporosis class. Four denosumab biosimilar brand names appear in the document: Conexxence (Tier 6, PA; SP; QL 1 SYR/6 months -- matches Prolia's osteoporosis schedule, listed as an alternative), Bomyntra (Tier 6, PA; SP; QL 1 VIAL or 1 SYR per 28 days -- this dosing interval matches the Xgeva/oncology schedule, not osteoporosis, so it was excluded here), Jubbonti (Tier 5, PA; SP; QL 1 SYR per 28 days -- flagged as an anomaly: Jubbonti is FDA-approved as the Prolia-reference biosimilar and should dose q6months like Prolia/Conexxence, but this PDF's QL note reads 28 days, which matches the Xgeva schedule instead; included as an alternative but the QL text should not be trusted without a follow-up read against the live formulary), and Wyost (Tier 5, PA; SP; QL 1 VIAL per 28 days -- consistent with the Xgeva/oncology reference product, excluded here). Preferred agent, tier, and PA/SP/QL for Prolia itself are verified verbatim; the biosimilar QL inconsistency (Jubbonti) is flagged rather than silently resolved, per this project's convention for PDF table-extraction anomalies.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off Mass General Brigham Health Plan's 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary (FBO 192 / 20266T.pdf), 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF page ~111 (teriparatide) and ~117 (Tymlos). Two teriparatide lines both reference brand 'Bonsity': 'Teriparatide Solution Pen-Injector 560 MCG/2.24ML Subcutaneous' and 'Teriparatide Subcutaneous Solution Pen-Injector 560 MCG/2.24ML' -- both Tier 6, PA; SP; ALVOGEN; QL (1 PEN per 28 days), confirmed verbatim (the 'ALVOGEN' note appears to flag a required-manufacturer restriction, i.e., only the Alvogen-manufactured product is covered at this line). 'Bonsity' also appears as its own standalone drug-name entry (PDF page ~16, alphabetical B section) with identical Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 PEN per 28 Days). No separate 'Forteo' (the original teriparatide brand) line exists anywhere in the document -- confirmed via full-text search -- meaning Forteo itself is not separately listed/covered; Bonsity (the FDA-approved substitutable teriparatide product) is the only teriparatide formulation on this formulary. 'Tymlos' (abaloparatide) appears once at Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 PEN per 30 days), confirmed verbatim, no generic exists for abaloparatide. A full-text search of the entire 135-page document for 'Evenity' and 'romosozumab' (and partial-string variants) returned zero matches -- confirmed via a flattened whitespace-normalized regex search, not just a plain grep, to rule out a mid-word PDF line break. This is treated as a genuine coverage gap (nonformulary) rather than an extraction failure, consistent with the document's exhaustive alphabetical drug-list format where every other osteoporosis anabolic agent (teriparatide, Tymlos) was found without difficulty. Preferred-vs-alternative ordering between teriparatide and Tymlos is inferred (teriparatide chosen as preferred because it has an available generic-equivalent product, Bonsity, while Tymlos has none) since both sit at the identical Tier 6/PA/SP combination with no explicit step-therapy ordering stated in the source.

POLICY states verbatim: 'Prolia (denosumab), Conexxence (denosumab-bnht), Jubbonti (denosumab-bbdz), Ospomyv (denosumab-dssb), Stoboclo (denosumab-bmwo), Bildyos (denosumab-nxxp) and Bosaya (denosumab-kyqq) will require prior authorization for medical benefit coverage to ensure appropriate use.' Postmenopausal osteoporosis...' with criteria beginning 'Patient has one of the following: I. ...history of fragility fracture...' (page cut off before full criteria list was read). Bildyos is named in the policy's overall product list but was NOT included in the specific postmenopausal-osteoporosis-approval sentence read from page 1 -- rather than guess why, it is omitted from this record's alternatives list entirely (may apply to a different denosumab indication, e.g. cancer-related bone disease, addressed elsewhere in the 5-page policy that was not fully read). This document does not apply specifically to Priority Partners by name in its 'Participating Organizations' list (it names 'Johns Hopkins Medical Services Corporation (USFHP)'), but it is a Johns Hopkins Health Plans-wide 'Pharmacy Public Medical Management Drug Policy' and the same policy number (PHARM 20 process, Policy MMDP055) governs PA processing across all Johns Hopkins Health Plans lines of business including Priority Partners, consistent with Priority Partners' own published Prolia PA form (pp_prolia_form.pdf) which routes to the identical fax/phone contacts as this policy.

records.verificationNote: Fetched https://www.uhcprovider.com/content/dam/provider/docs/public/resources/pharmacy/commercial-pdl-jan-2026.pdf directly (WebFetch's own summarizer returned only a 'corrupted/encoded' message per this repo's known uhcprovider.com quirk, but the tool result named the local path where it had already saved the raw PDF bytes; opening that path with PyMuPDF/fitz yielded full readable text across all 80 pages). Page 1 (0-indexed) confirms the document is UnitedHealthcare's '2026 Prescription Drug List,' Effective January 1, 2026, publisher 'UnitedHealthcare & affiliated companies.' A full-document keyword search for 'maryland' returned zero hits anywhere in the PDF, confirming this national PDL applies to Maryland as a default-rules state with no state-specific carve-out language (unlike CT/NJ/NY, which get an explicit exception). The 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents - Drugs for Osteoporosis' section (page footer '44', 0-indexed page 43) lists verbatim: alendronate sodium oral tablet (Tier 1, no flags), FOSAMAX (Tier 4), ibandronate sodium oral (Tier 2), risedronate sodium oral tablet 150mg/35mg (Tier 3, QL) and 30mg/5mg (Tier 3, no flag), and ACTONEL (E, QL). Page 4 (0-indexed) supplies the symbol legend: 'E: May be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York' -- since Maryland is not named in that carve-out, a bare 'E' flag (no separate PA flag) means straightforward exclusion from coverage in Maryland. Alendronate tablet is the only oral bisphosphonate at Tier 1 with zero flags, so it was selected as the class-level preferred agent -- the PDL itself does not use an explicit 'preferred agent' label, so this single-agent selection is a judgment call from the tier/flag pattern, though the underlying tier/flag data for every named agent is verbatim-verified. The representative 70mg once-weekly strength and directions are standard FDA-label prescribing convention, not stated in the PDL (which lists drug names/tiers/flags only, no dosing guidance).

records.preferredRestriction: Not listed anywhere on the outpatient pharmacy PDL -- this agent is an in-office/infusion-center administered drug, and the PDL explicitly states its scope 'does not include ... medications obtained or administered in a physician's office.' Zoledronic acid for osteoporosis is billed under UHC's medical benefit (HCPCS J3489), not the OptumRx pharmacy benefit, so no PDL tier or pharmacy PA-notification document applies to it the way one does for self-administered/specialty-pharmacy drugs in this class list.
records.verificationNote: The cited 2026 Prescription Drug List (effective 2026-01-01, publisher UnitedHealthcare & affiliated companies) is the correct, current pharmacy formulary for this payer/state -- a full-document keyword search across all 80 pages for 'zoledronic', 'reclast', and the HCPCS code 'J3489' returned zero hits, confirming zoledronic acid/Reclast is genuinely absent from the pharmacy PDL rather than missed, consistent with the PDL's own stated scope exclusion for physician-office-administered drugs (page 3, 0-indexed). A full-document search for 'maryland' also returned zero hits, confirming no MD-specific carve-out exists in this national document. A follow-up WebSearch located UnitedHealthcare's 'Maximum Dosage and Frequency - Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy' (effective 2026-01-01), which lists Reclast's maximum dose (5 mg per administration, HCPCS J3489) but is a dosage-ceiling policy, not a dedicated prior-authorization policy for the drug -- it does not state whether PA is required. No dedicated UHC commercial medical-benefit PA policy specifically for zoledronic acid/Reclast (mirroring the ones confirmed for Denosumab and Evenity in the sibling rankl-inhibitor and anabolic classes) could be located via WebSearch. General industry practice and UHC's own parallel osteoporosis medical-benefit policies (Denosumab, Evenity) both require prior authorization for infused/injected antiresorptive and anabolic bone agents, making PA a reasonable inference for zoledronic acid as well, but this specific requirement is NOT confirmed against a UHC-commercial-specific document and is marked accordingly. The representative dosing (5mg/100mL, annual/biennial IV infusion) is standard FDA-label information, not sourced from a UHC document.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization is required for every denosumab product, including the preferred Prolia and Stoboclo, per UHC's Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy for Denosumab (Policy 2026D0068T, effective 7/1/2026 -- this is a national commercial policy with no Maryland-specific carve-out). Initial authorization is limited to 12 months at a time and requires: diagnosis of osteoporosis (or glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis), patient at high risk of fracture (history of osteoporotic fracture, multiple risk factors, or failure/intolerance of other available therapy), and FDA-labeled dosing. Reauthorization requires documented positive clinical response, also capped at 12 months per approval.
records.boglNote: Prolia (denosumab) and its FDA-approved biosimilar Stoboclo (denosumab-bmwo) are both named as the co-preferred denosumab products in this policy -- this is not a brand-over-generic scenario since the branded originator (Prolia) is itself one of the two preferred options, on equal footing with its biosimilar.

alternatives.note: Co-preferred with Prolia; same PA criteria and 12-month authorization period

paRequired.drug: Jubbonti (denosumab-bbdz biosimilar) and any other non-preferred FDA-approved denosumab product
paRequired.reason: Per the policy's 'Preferred Product Criteria,' coverage of Jubbonti or any other denosumab product besides Prolia/Stoboclo requires EITHER (a) a documented trial of adequate dose/duration of Prolia AND Stoboclo with minimal clinical response, plus physician attestation the other product would work better, OR (b) documented intolerance/contraindication/adverse event to Prolia AND Stoboclo, plus attestation the same problem wouldn't be expected with the other product. Members already on another product must switch to Prolia or Stoboclo unless they meet this criteria.

Per WebSearch, located and fetched UHC's actual 'Denosumab' UnitedHealthcare Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy PDF at https://www.uhcprovider.com/content/dam/provider/docs/public/policies/comm-medical-drug/denosumab-prolia-xgeva.pdf (again via WebFetch's saved raw bytes, opened directly with PyMuPDF). Page 1 confirms verbatim: 'UnitedHealthcare Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy, Denosumab, Policy Number: 2026D0068T, Effective Date: July 1, 2026.' Page 2 reads verbatim: 'Prolia and Stoboclo are the preferred denosumab products' and 'members already on Jubbonti or other non-preferred denosumab products will be required to change therapy to Prolia or Stoboclo unless they meet the criteria in this section,' followed by the exact non-preferred-product criteria quoted above (both eligibility paths read verbatim from the Preferred Product Criteria section). The Diagnosis-Specific Criteria section confirms verbatim the 12-month initial-authorization and reauthorization limits and the high-fracture-risk definition. A full-document search of this policy for 'maryland' also returned zero hits -- this is a national commercial policy (title says 'Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy' with no state qualifier), so it applies to Maryland as written. The representative 60mg/6-month dosing is standard FDA-label information for Prolia, not stated in the policy document itself.

preferredAgent.sig: Abaloparatide (Tymlos) 80 mcg/40 mcL subcutaneous injection, administered once daily via prefilled pen into the periumbilical abdominal skin
preferredAgent.plainSig: Inject 80 mcg under the skin of the abdomen once a day using the prefilled pen; patients can be trained to do this themselves at home.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization plus specialty pharmacy dispensing required even for TYMLOS, despite its Tier 3 placement on the PDL (flags 'PA, SP'). Per UHC's dedicated Tymlos Prior Authorization/Notification program (2025 P 1222-11, effective 12/1/2025 -- a national commercial pharmacy program with no Maryland-specific carve-out), approval requires a diagnosis of postmenopausal (or male) osteoporosis AND either high fracture risk (recent fracture, multiple risk factors, very low T-score, high fall risk, or high FRAX score) OR documented failure/intolerance/contraindication to another osteoporosis therapy. Cumulative lifetime use is capped at 2 years (24 months) across ALL parathyroid hormone analogs combined (abaloparatide + teriparatide), per UHC policy and AACE/ACE and Endocrine Society guidance cited in the same document.

alternatives.note: Tier 3, PA, SP -- same restriction tier as Tymlos; approved under UHC's parallel teriparatide PA program (2025 P 1033-17, eff 11/1/2025); shares the same 24-month lifetime cap across all parathyroid hormone analogs

paRequired.reason: Marked 'E, PA, SP' on the PDL -- the explicit PA flag applies nationally (it is not part of the CT/NJ/NY-only carve-out that governs bare 'E' listings), so Forteo remains PA-eligible rather than flatly excluded in Maryland. Approvable under the same teriparatide PA program that covers Bonsity (2025 P 1033-17, eff 11/1/2025), but priced at the higher-cost 'E' tier rather than Bonsity's Tier 3.
Marked 'E, PA, SP' on the PDL -- same PA program and clinical criteria as Bonsity/Forteo, but placed at the higher-cost 'E' tier rather than Bonsity's Tier 3.

Not listed on the outpatient pharmacy PDL at all -- administered as a monthly in-office injection and billed under UHC's Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy for Evenity (Policy 2025D0080L, effective 12/1/2025, a national commercial policy). Requires diagnosis of postmenopausal osteoporosis at high fracture risk; treatment is limited to a maximum of 12 monthly doses because the anabolic effect wanes after that point.

records.stepTherapy: None of the anabolic agents in this class require a strict 'must-fail-bisphosphonate-first' step. PA criteria for both the teriparatide-group program and the Tymlos program accept EITHER a high-fracture-risk clinical profile (recent fracture, multiple risk factors, very low T-score, high fall risk, high FRAX score) OR documented failure/intolerance/contraindication to another osteoporosis therapy (e.g., alendronate, denosumab, risedronate, zoledronate) -- either path satisfies the medical-necessity criterion, so it functions as medical-necessity gating rather than mandatory sequential step therapy.
records.tier: Tier 3 - highest tier among agents actually listed on the pharmacy PDL for this class, plus PA and Specialty Pharmacy (SP) required (abaloparatide/TYMLOS)
records.verificationNote: Read the PDL's osteoporosis section directly (page footer '44', 0-indexed page 43; fetched at the assigned URL via WebFetch's saved raw PDF bytes, opened with PyMuPDF since the tool's own extraction returned only a 'corrupted/encoded' summary): TYMLOS (Tier 3, PA, SP), BONSITY (Tier 3, PA, SP), FORTEO (E, PA, SP), and generic 'teriparatide solution pen-injector 560mcg/2.24ml subcutaneous' (E, PA, SP) -- all four verbatim from the table. A full-document search for 'maryland' in the PDL returned zero hits, confirming no MD carve-out exists; because the FORTEO and generic-teriparatide lines carry an explicit 'PA' flag (not a bare 'E'), the national PA requirement applies in Maryland the same as everywhere else -- unlike the bare-'E' items in the oral-bisphosphonate and SERM classes, these are PA-eligible rather than non-formulary. Romosozumab/Evenity does not appear anywhere in the 80-page PDL (zero keyword hits for 'romosozumab' or 'evenity'), consistent with the PDL's stated scope exclusion for physician-office-administered drugs (page 3, 0-indexed); confirmed via WebSearch + WebFetch (again reading saved raw bytes with PyMuPDF) that Evenity is instead governed by a dedicated 'UnitedHealthcare Commercial Medical Benefit Drug Policy, Evenity (Romosozumab-Aqqg), Policy Number: 2025D0080L, Effective Date: December 1, 2025' -- a national commercial policy with zero hits for 'maryland' in its own text. Also fetched and read UHC's dedicated Tymlos PA/Notification document ('2025 P 1222-11', Effective 12/1/2025, at https://www.uhcprovider.com/content/dam/provider/docs/public/prior-auth/drugs-pharmacy/commercial/r-z/PA-Notification-Tymlos.pdf) and the combined Bonsity/Forteo/Teriparatide-Injection PA/Notification document ('2025 P 1033-17', Effective 11/1/2025, at https://www.uhcprovider.com/content/dam/provider/docs/public/prior-auth/drugs-pharmacy/commercial/a-g/PA-Notification-Forteo-Bonsity.pdf) directly -- both confirm verbatim the coverage-criteria structure (high fracture risk OR prior therapy failure, 2-year/24-month cumulative lifetime cap across all parathyroid hormone analogs) quoted above. TYMLOS was selected over Bonsity as the single class-level preferred agent because, on the PDL, abaloparatide has one clean Tier-3 listing while teriparatide is split across three entries (Forteo-E, Bonsity-Tier3, generic-E) with no explicit 'preferred' label distinguishing Bonsity from Tymlos in either the PDL or the two dedicated PA-notification documents -- this pick is a judgment call given the genuine near-tie in restriction level between Bonsity and Tymlos, disclosed here rather than presented as an unambiguous single preferred agent. The representative 80mcg/daily Tymlos dosing is standard FDA-label information, not stated in the UHC documents themselves.

records.preferredRestriction: Not listed anywhere in this Part D pharmacy formulary -- zero hits for 'zoledronic,' 'reclast,' or 'zometa' across a full-text search of all 130 pages, including the alphabetical drug index. Zoledronic acid for osteoporosis is a physician-administered IV infusion, and this drug class is typically billed under Medicare Part B (medical/physician-administered drug benefit) rather than the Part D pharmacy benefit, consistent with its total absence here.
records.verificationNote: The cited document (Complete Drug List (Formulary) 2026 -- AARP Medicare Rx Preferred from UHC (PDP), Formulary ID 00026000, footer-dated 'Last updated October 1, 2025' on every page) is confirmed as the correct, current Part D formulary for this exact plan -- the same document already verified for this payer's SSRI checkpoint this session. A full-text keyword search programmatically run across all 130 extracted pages for 'zoledronic,' 'reclast,' and 'zometa' returned zero matches anywhere, including the back-of-book alphabetical drug index -- this absence is directly and verifiably confirmed, not a guess. However, unlike UHC's commercial PDL (which states in its own scope section that it 'does not include ... medications obtained or administered in a physician's office'), this Medicare Part D document contains no equivalent explicit scope-exclusion sentence (a full-text search for 'physician,' "doctor's office," 'infusion,' and 'does not include' also returned zero hits) -- so the specific REASON for zoledronic acid's absence (Part B medical-benefit billing rather than Part D exclusion for some other cause) is inferred from general Medicare coverage knowledge, not read verbatim from this document. That inferred reasoning is why this checkpoint is marked 'partial' rather than 'verified' -- the fact of non-coverage on the Part D formulary is verified; the stated cause is not.

Table rows: 'Teriparatide (Subcutaneous Solution Pen-Injector) | Brand | Tier 5 | PA; DL; QL', 'Bonsity (Subcutaneous Solution Pen-Injector) | Brand | Tier 5 | PA; DL; QL' (this row actually appears on printed page 87, the same subheading), and 'Tymlos (Subcutaneous Solution Pen-Injector) | Brand | Tier 5 | PA; DL; QL' -- all three read directly with identical restriction codes. The 'Teriparatide' row is coded 'Brand' in the source table despite using the generic INN as its display name; this most likely reflects the FDA-approved authorized-generic/brand-equivalent teriparatide pen product rather than a true multisource generic, but the document does not clarify further, so genericAvailable is set to true on the strength of a distinct, lower-cost teriparatide pen product existing in this table separate from Bonsity and Forteo. Teriparatide was picked as the class-level preferred agent (over Bonsity or Tymlos) because it is the plainest/most standard INN-named entry and matches the convention used for this same class by the majority of other Maryland payers already checkpointed this session (mdmedicaid, cigna, kpmidatlantic, and priority-partners all also chose teriparatide as the anabolic-class preferred agent). A full-document keyword search for 'romosozumab' and 'evenity' returned zero hits anywhere, including the alphabetical index, confirming its absence; the specific reason given (Part B billing) is inferred from general Medicare coverage knowledge and the parallel pattern already confirmed for zoledronic acid in the iv-bisphosphonate class for this same payer/document, not stated verbatim in this document.

The table row reads: 'Raloxifene HCl (Oral Tablet) | Generic | Tier 3 | QL' -- confirmed directly, with only a quantity limit (not a PA/ST barrier) per the document's own abbreviation legend on pages 6-7. The same subheading also lists 'Osphena (Oral Tablet) | Brand | Tier 3 | PA; QL' (ospemifene) immediately above raloxifene in the same table, but Osphena is FDA-approved for dyspareunia due to menopause, not osteoporosis, so it is excluded from alternatives/paRequired here as clinically out of scope for this drug class rather than a competing osteoporosis SERM. A full-document search for 'bazedoxifene' returned zero hits, but a separate search for 'duavee' returned 2 hits -- one in the back-of-book alphabetical index (page 16) and one real table row on printed page 78 (0-indexed page 77), under a different subheading ('Estrogens,' part of the same broader contraceptives/hormone-therapy category rather than the 'Selective Estrogen Receptor Modifying Agents' subheading raloxifene appears under): 'Duavee (Oral Tablet) | Brand | Tier 4 | DL' -- confirmed directly, no PA. Duavee (bazedoxifene/conjugated estrogens) is added to alternatives since it is a real, clean-covered SERM-class product for osteoporosis prevention, even though the source document files it under a different table heading than raloxifene.

paRequired.drug: risedronate (generic and all brands, e.g. Actonel, Atelvia)
paRequired.reason: Not listed anywhere in the 153-page PDL (searched full text for 'risedronate,' 'Actonel,' 'Atelvia,' 'Boniva' — zero hits). Per DMAS's own Pharmacy Provider Manual Chapter IV (rev. 3/4/2026, p.10): a new drug in a class the P&T Committee has already reviewed is immediately classified as requiring Service Authorization to dispense. Bisphosphonates are a reviewed PDL class (alendronate/ibandronate appear in it), so an absent oral bisphosphonate defaults to requiring Service Authorization (SA).

The PDL's own 'Reference' column names Fosamax only as the brand cross-reference for the preferred GENERIC alendronate sodium tablet entry; the brand itself carries no separate clean listing. Per Virginia Medicaid's standard generic-first policy (confirmed via UnitedHealthcare Community Plan of Virginia Cardinal Care member materials: 'covers generic drugs first, and if a doctor feels a brand name drug is medically necessary, prior approval will be needed'), the brand would need SA if the generic isn't tried/is not appropriate.

records.tier: Listed as preferred (no PA/ST/QL flag) on the VA Cardinal Care Medicaid-Approved PDL; this PDL does not use numbered cost tiers, only PA/ST/QL/SP/AL/DO/GR footnote flags — an unflagged entry is payable without Service Authorization.
records.verificationNote: Fetched the full 153-page 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026' directly via curl with a browser User-Agent (a plain WebFetch call was not attempted first given this session's prior confirmed pattern of fm.formularynavigator.com blocking WebFetch but not curl; curl returned HTTP 200, 153 pages, verified with pypdf). Page 82, under the header '*ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC AGENTS - MISC.*' > '*BISPHOSPHONATES***', reads verbatim (Drug Name / Reference / Notes columns): 'alendronate sodium oral tablet | Fosamax | [no notes]' and 'ibandronate sodium oral tablet | [no reference] | [no notes]' — both directly confirmed clean-covered (no PA/ST/QL/SP/AL flag) with the document's own Effective May 1, 2026 date on the cover page (page 1), which also supplies the flag legend (PA/AL/DO/GR/OTC/QL/SP/ST). Full-text search across all 153 pages for 'risedronate,' 'Actonel,' 'Atelvia,' and 'Boniva' returned zero hits — risedronate is absent from the Bisphosphonates section and the entire document. The PA-required conclusion for risedronate and for brand Fosamax is inferred: from DMAS's Pharmacy Provider Manual Chapter IV (rev. 3/4/2026, fetched via curl from vamedicaid.dmas.virginia.gov, page 10) describing that absent-from-a-reviewed-PDL-class drugs are non-preferred and require Service Authorization, plus a WebSearch-sourced UHC Cardinal Care member-facing statement on generic-first policy — neither is a risedronate-specific or Fosamax-specific verbatim PA line inside the PDL itself. Alendronate/ibandronate's own clean (no-restriction) status is the directly-verified core fact for this class.

records.verificationNote: Searched the full extracted text of all 160 pages of the 'Sentara Community Plan Prescription Drug Formulary (July-September 2026)' (SHP_MD_MEM_OMSC_230005, effective 07/01/2026) plus its alphabetical index (pages ~126-159) for 'teriparatide', 'forteo', 'abaloparatide', 'tymlos', 'romosozumab', and 'evenity' — zero hits for any of the three anabolic agents. None appear in the OSTEOPOROSIS THERAPY category or elsewhere. This is consistent with anabolic osteoporosis agents commonly being dispensed through a specialty pharmacy channel (the document notes on page 4 that 'Specialty Drugs are only available from a Plan Specialty Pharmacy') rather than itemized individually in the retail PDL, or potentially requiring a medical-necessity exception process not documented here. Teriparatide was selected as the preferred pick because a generic teriparatide (Teva and Ambio, FDA-approved November/December 2023; Amphastar approved December 2025) now exists, making it the lowest-cost option in the class by clinical convention — abaloparatide and romosozumab have no generic/biosimilar equivalent as of this review. No PA/tier/restriction language for any of the three agents could be confirmed from this source; strength/sig reflect standard clinical dosing, not text read from the formulary.

records.verificationNote: The full text of the cited PDL (370 pages, effective 7/1/2026, publisher UnitedHealthcare Community Plan/United HealthCare Services, Inc.) was searched for 'zoledronic', 'Reclast', and every other IV bisphosphonate synonym with zero matches, confirming this drug is not carried on the retail pharmacy formulary (expected, since IV zoledronic acid for osteoporosis is typically administered and billed as a physician-office infusion under the medical benefit). Searched uhcprovider.com via WebSearch for a dedicated 'Community Plan Medical Benefit Drug Policy' for zoledronic acid/Reclast analogous to the ones that exist for denosumab and romosozumab, and found none - only references to 'zoledronic acid' as a comparator/precondition drug inside UHC's own Denosumab (effective 2026-04-01) and Evenity (effective 2026-04-01) Community Plan Medical Benefit Drug Policies, both of which list failure/contraindication/intolerance to IV bisphosphonate therapy 'e.g., pamidronate, zoledronic acid' as an acceptable pathway toward approving those other drugs. This implies UHC treats IV bisphosphonate therapy as an accessible, non-gated first-line option relative to denosumab/romosozumab, but that is an inference from absence of a restrictive policy, not a direct statement of Reclast's own PA/coverage status - hence 'partial' rather than 'verified'. The strength/sig reflect standard FDA-labeled annual osteoporosis dosing for zoledronic acid, not a value read from a UHC-specific document.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required (medical-benefit drug, not on the retail pharmacy PDL). Initial approval requires: diagnosis of osteoporosis with a qualifying BMD T-score <= -2.5 (or a T-score between -1 and -2.5 plus a qualifying FRAX 10-year fracture-risk threshold), or a history of a minimal-trauma fracture (vertebral, hip, distal radius, pelvis, or proximal humerus); AND documented failure, contraindication, or intolerance to oral bisphosphonate therapy, or to IV bisphosphonate therapy (e.g., zoledronic acid). Authorized for no more than 12 months per approval, renewable with provider attestation of a positive clinical response.
records.boglNote: Not a classic small-molecule brand-over-generic case: Jubbonti (denosumab-bbdz) and Bildyos (denosumab-nxxp) are both interchangeable-status biosimilars that UHC has designated 'preferred' over the originator brand Prolia and the other biosimilars (Conexxence, Stoboclo) for the osteoporosis (Prolia-reference) indication group.

alternatives.note: Co-preferred biosimilar alongside Jubbonti; same PA criteria, no step barrier between the two preferred biosimilars.

paRequired.reason: Requires a documented trial/failure of BOTH preferred biosimilars (Bildyos and Jubbonti), or a documented contraindication/intolerance to both, plus physician attestation that Prolia would be expected to produce a superior clinical response, before approval.

records.stepTherapy: For the osteoporosis indication, any denosumab product (preferred biosimilar or non-preferred brand/biosimilar) requires documented failure, contraindication, or intolerance to oral bisphosphonate therapy AND/OR IV bisphosphonate therapy (e.g., zoledronic acid) before initial approval. Separately, non-preferred products (Prolia, Conexxence, Stoboclo) additionally require a documented trial/failure (or contraindication) of both preferred biosimilars Bildyos and Jubbonti before they will be approved.
records.verificationNote: The known pharmacy PDL source (VA Cardinal Care PDL, effective 7/1/2026) was searched in full and contains zero references to denosumab, Prolia, Xgeva, or any biosimilar - confirming this is a medical-benefit-only drug for this payer. Followed up with a WebSearch that surfaced UnitedHealthcare's own 'Denosumab - Community Plan Medical Benefit Drug Policy' (Policy Number CS2026D0068X, effective 04/01/2026), fetched directly from uhcprovider.com. Confirmed by reading pages 1-6 verbatim: the policy's 'Application' section lists states it does NOT apply to (Florida, Indiana, Kansas, North Carolina, Ohio, Pennsylvania - refer to state-specific policy - plus Washington for the Prolia product only); Virginia is not on that exclusion list, so this default/national policy governs UHC Community Plan of Virginia. Confirmed verbatim: 'Preferred Products: Bildyos, Bilprevda, Jubbonti, and Wyost are the preferred denosumab products,' with Bomyntra, Conexxence, Osenvelt, Prolia, Stoboclo, and Xgeva named as non-preferred, gated by a step-through-preferred-products criterion. Confirmed verbatim the osteoporosis diagnosis-specific criteria (BMD T-score/fracture history + oral/IV bisphosphonate failure or intolerance, 12-month authorization). Note: Bilprevda/Bomyntra/Osenvelt/Wyost/Xgeva are the Xgeva-reference biosimilar group used for oncology/SRE indications, not the postmenopausal osteoporosis indication, and are therefore excluded from this osteoporosis-class checkpoint.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from Aetna Better Health of Virginia's own FormularyNavigator machine-readable NDC export (fetched via curl with a browser User-Agent from https://fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/111/Aetna_Better_Health_of_Virginia.json; the file's own Profile_Information confirms public_name 'Aetna_Better_Health_of_Virginia', formulary_type 'State Medicaid', formulary_name 'ABH VA V2', formulary_id 22791, formulary_version 33, formulary_effective_date 07/01/2026). Parsed all 102,529 Drug_Information rows in Python and filtered by drug name for every named agent in this class. Confirmed verbatim per-NDC drug_tier + prior_authorization + step_therapy flags: generic alendronate tablets (10/35/70 mg, every NDC) = 'Preferred', prior_authorization=false, step_therapy=false; generic ibandronate 150 mg tablet and 3 mg/3 mL IV solution (every NDC) = 'Preferred', PA=false (IV has quantity_limit=true); generic risedronate (every strength/NDC including delayed-release) = 'State PDL Non-Preferred', PA=false, step=false; Fosamax, Fosamax Plus D, Actonel, and Atelvia brand NDCs = 'State PDL Non-Preferred', PA=false; alendronate oral solution NDC = 'State PDL Non-Preferred', PA=false. No agent in this class carries a PA or step-therapy flag in this payer's own data, so paRequired is genuinely empty -- risedronate and the brand products are higher cost-share tier only, not access barriers, which is why they are classified as alternatives rather than PA-required per this payer's own boolean flags (not an assumption). The sig/plainSig text is standard alendronate weekly-dosing clinical convention, not itself stated in the payer's export, which carries NDC-level coverage flags only, no dosing text. No class-wide step-therapy criterion is exposed by this data source (boolean flags only, no underlying PA-criteria narrative).

records.verificationNote: Zoledronic acid / Reclast does NOT appear anywhere in Aetna Better Health of Virginia's own FormularyNavigator NDC export (https://fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/111/Aetna_Better_Health_of_Virginia.json, formulary_id 22791, v33, effective 07/01/2026) -- searched exhaustively across all 102,529 rows for 'zoledronic', 'zoledr*', 'reclast', 'zometa', and 'aclasta' with zero matches, both in the parsed Drug_Information array and a raw case-insensitive text grep of the full file. This is a genuine, verified absence, not a search-term miss. To determine the likely coverage terms, cross-referenced the shared Virginia DMAS Cardinal Care Common Core Formulary, which all Cardinal Care MCOs (including Aetna Better Health of Virginia) are contractually required to follow: fetched Anthem HealthKeepers Plus's own FormularyNavigator export of that same statewide PDL (https://fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/VA%20Anthem%20Common%20Core%20Formulary.json, public_name 'VA Anthem Cardinal Care Formulary', formulary_id 16032, v75, effective 07/01/2026), which shows 'Zoledronic Acid Solution 5 MG/100ML Intravenous' and 'Reclast Solution 5 MG/100ML Intravenous' both at drug_tier='Non-Preferred', prior_authorization=true, quantity_limit=true. The preferred agent, tier, and PA/QL restriction in this record are therefore inferred from that statewide-policy cross-reference, not read verbatim from Aetna's own file -- hence 'partial' rather than 'verified'. A plausible explanation for the absence from Aetna's own pharmacy NDC export is that this drug, as a physician-administered IV infusion, may be processed through a medical-benefit or specialty-infusion channel outside the standard retail-pharmacy NDC list this export represents, rather than being genuinely excluded from coverage -- but this is not confirmed by any source read in this gather. Dosing/administration instructions are standard clinical convention for the osteoporosis (Reclast) regimen, not text pulled from either payer's tier export (NDC-level coverage flags only).

Page 51 of 83, under therapeutic class header '*ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC AGENTS - MISC.* / *BISPHOSPHONATES***', lists exactly four oral bisphosphonate line items: 'alendronate sodium oral solution' (Tier 1, QL), 'alendronate sodium oral tablet' (Tier 1, QL), 'FOSAMAX PLUS D ORAL TABLET' (Tier 3, QL), and 'ibandronate sodium oral tablet' (Tier 1, QL); page 52 continues the same section with 'risedronate sodium oral tablet' (Tier 2, QL) before moving to pamidronate (IV) and calcimimetics. The document's own tier-key footer on every page confirms Tier 1 = lowest cost share, Tier 2 = higher than Tier 1, Tier 3 = higher than Tier 2, Tier 4 = highest/specialty, and that the Notes column would show 'PA'/'ST' if either applied — none of the five oral-bisphosphonate line items carry a PA or ST note, confirming no restriction on any of them. Document cover page states 'Effective 07012026' (also printed as a running footer on every content page) and the PDF's own metadata title is '2026 VA Select HIX' (creation timestamp 2026-07-14, i.e. a mid-quarter refresh of the 07/01/2026-effective booklet). Alendronate tablet is designated the single preferred pick here as the guideline-standard, once-weekly first-line oral bisphosphonate; ibandronate sits at the identical Tier 1/no-PA level so is listed as an alternative rather than a second preferred pick only because one representative agent was requested per class.

Per Anthem/CarelonRx Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0019, approvable for osteoporosis treatment/prevention in postmenopausal women, treatment to increase bone mass in men, or glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (on ≥7.5 mg/day prednisone-equivalent for ≥12 months) — no mandatory prior bisphosphonate failure is required for the core osteoporosis indication; quantity limit of 100 mL (5 mg) once per year.

records.verificationNote: The cited pharmacy formulary PDF (2026 VA Select Drug List, Four Tier, effective 07/01/2026) was searched page-by-page for 'zoledronic', 'reclast', and 'pamidronate' and confirmed to contain NO zoledronic acid/Reclast line item anywhere in its 83 pages — the booklet's own FAQ page states plainly 'Yes, this is a complete listing of all the drugs on the drug list,' confirming the omission is real and not an extraction gap (only 'pamidronate disodium intravenous solution', Tier 4/SP, appears in the *BISPHOSPHONATES* section on page 52). This is expected: physician-administered IV zoledronic acid is virtually always billed under the medical benefit rather than the outpatient pharmacy benefit. Confirmed via the current CarelonRx-hosted Medical Drug Clinical Criteria document CC-0019 'Zoledronic Acid (Reclast)' (fetched directly; WebFetch's AI summarizer again returned only a binary-format non-answer, so the locally-saved raw PDF bytes were read directly with PyMuPDF — same known workaround), Publish Date 12/22/2025, Last Review Date 11/14/2025 (Status: Reviewed, no substantive change from an earlier 2022 vintage of the same document also found via Anthem's provider-news CDN). The document explicitly states criteria are evaluated 'When a drug is being reviewed for coverage under a member's medical benefit plan' and lists HCPCS J3489 (a medical/buy-and-bill billing code, not an NDC/pharmacy claim), confirming medical-benefit administration. Approval criteria for Reclast specifically include osteoporosis treatment/prevention in postmenopausal women and treatment to increase bone mass in men with no explicit prior-bisphosphonate-failure gate (contrast the harder step-therapy gate on romosozumab/Evenity in the anabolic-class file). The quantity limit (100 mL/5 mg once per year) is read verbatim from the document's own Quantity Limits table. records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required under the medical benefit (this drug is physician-administered and billed under medical, not pharmacy, so it does not appear on the pharmacy drug list/tier structure at all). Per Anthem/CarelonRx Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0027, initial approval requires a diagnosis of osteoporosis (BMD T-score ≤ -2.5, or history of a fragility fracture) plus at least one of: a prior osteoporotic fracture, 2+ fracture risk factors, or failure/intolerance/contraindication to other osteoporosis therapies (e.g. bisphosphonates) — the bisphosphonate-failure route is one of several qualifying paths, not a mandatory first-line step. Quantity limit: one 60 mg prefilled syringe every 6 months. A plan-level step-therapy addendum in the same document also allows a 'preferred' vs. 'non-preferred' denosumab-biosimilar list, with non-preferred agents requiring an inactive-ingredient allergy/intolerance to switch from the preferred one.

paRequired.reason: Not listed anywhere on the 2026 VA Select four-tier pharmacy drug list, which the source itself states is a complete listing of all covered drugs — meaning it is not a pharmacy-benefit-covered option under this plan. (An Anthem multi-market Tymlos PA-criteria template exists but its stated market applicability — GA/KY/MD/NJ/NY/WA — does not include Virginia, consistent with non-formulary status here.)

Covered only under the medical benefit (HCPCS J3111), not the pharmacy drug list. Per Anthem/CarelonRx Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0139, approval requires the individual be refractory to, intolerant of, or contraindicated to a bisphosphonate trial AND refractory to, intolerant of, or contraindicated to denosumab/teriparatide/abaloparatide before Evenity is approved; it cannot be used in combination with any other osteoporosis therapy; and is capped at 12 months of lifetime use. This is a genuine step-therapy gate positioning Evenity as a late-line 'rescue' option behind both bisphosphonates and the other anabolic/RANKL-inhibitor agents.

records.stepTherapy: Evenity (romosozumab) requires documented failure/intolerance/contraindication to a bisphosphonate AND to denosumab, teriparatide, or abaloparatide before approval (CC-0139); no equivalent step gate applies to teriparatide itself under the pharmacy benefit.
records.verificationNote: Teriparatide: read directly from the cited pharmacy formulary PDF (raw bytes extracted with PyMuPDF after WebFetch's AI summarizer returned only a binary-format non-answer). Page 53, under '*ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC AGENTS - MISC.* / *PARATHYROID HORMONE AND DERIVATIVES***', lists exactly one line item: 'teriparatide subcutaneous solution pen-injector' — Tier 4, Notes 'PA; SP; QL'. Lowercase generic-name formatting (per the document's own key: 'Generic drugs are in lower case, plain type') confirms this is the generic product, i.e. genericAvailable=true is directly sourced, not inferred. No brand Forteo/Bonsity line item appears at all. Abaloparatide (Tymlos) and romosozumab (Evenity) were searched for across the full 83-page document by name and both return zero hits, and the document's FAQ page states it is a complete drug list — confirming both are absent from the pharmacy benefit for this plan. Evenity's PA/step-therapy gate was confirmed by reading the CarelonRx-hosted Medical Drug Clinical Criteria CC-0139 'Evenity (romosozumab-aqqg)' directly (Publish Date 10/01/2025, Last Review Date 08/15/2025, Status: Revised) — HCPCS J3111 confirms medical-benefit billing; the document's Section III/IV criteria explicitly require prior bisphosphonate refractoriness/intolerance AND prior refractoriness/intolerance to Prolia/denosumab-biosimilars, Forteo/Bonsity (teriparatide), or Tymlos (abaloparatide), plus a prohibition on combination use and a 12-month lifetime dosing cap (Section V), all read verbatim. Tymlos's non-formulary status is corroborated (not independently verified for VA specifically) by an Anthem multi-market PA-criteria template (PHARM_ALL_Tymlos.pdf) whose stated market-applicability checklist (GA, KY, MD, NJ, NY, WA) omits Virginia — consistent with, though not conclusive proof of, its absence from the VA commercial pharmacy formulary.

records.verificationNote: Fetched both current Alabama Medicaid Agency PDL documents directly (WebFetch itself hit an SSL cert error on the payer's HTML landing page, so a WebSearch located the two live PDF documents on medicaid.alabama.gov, which were downloaded with curl and read locally via PyMuPDF -- the state-index.json entry for al-medicaid already had the correct formularyUrl for the PDL landing page, confirming payer identity). The 'PREFERRED DRUG LIST BY THERAPEUTIC CATEGORY' document (4.3.7_PDL_Thera_3-16-26.pdf, page 1) states 'Effective 04/01/2026' and lists the Antidepressants category verbatim as: 'All covered generics (generic desvenlafaxine ER [Khedezla] and sertraline capsules require a PA)' -- this is read directly and verbatim, confirming that all covered generic SSRI forms (fluoxetine, sertraline tablets/solution, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine, fluvoxamine) are preferred without PA, with the single named exception of sertraline capsules (the desvenlafaxine carve-out is an SNRI, not an SSRI, so it is not included in this record). The companion alphabetical PDL (4.3.7_PDL_Alpha_1-1-26.pdf, single page, 'Effective 01/01/2026') lists every brand-preferred product statewide and contains zero SSRI brand names, confirming no brand SSRI is PDL-preferred. Choosing sertraline as THE single 'preferred agent' for this app's schema (vs. the other five equally-preferred molecules) is an editorial choice matching this repo's established convention for the ssri-oral class in every other state gathered so far (NY, MA, MD, VA, IL), not something the AL PDL itself singles out. The brand-requires-PA conclusion for each named brand (Zoloft, Prozac, Celexa, Lexapro, Paxil, Luvox) is inferred from the document's own stated general rule ('Brand preferred drugs, generics... are available without prior approval. If a non-preferred drug is ordered, the practitioner will need to obtain prior authorization') combined with the alphabetical PDL's confirmation that no SSRI brand appears on the brand-preferred list -- not an itemized per-brand PA table in the source, hence 'verified' for the preferred agent and its one named restriction, with the brand list treated as a standard, well-documented convention rather than a fabricated guess.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the exact cited PDF (myprime.com 2026_AL_4T_Standardized_Source+Rx_1.0.pdf); WebFetch's built-in extraction returned only corrupted/binary stream text (this repo's known large-PDF extraction gap), so the tool's own saved local copy was re-read directly with PyMuPDF (fitz) instead, per the existing CLAUDE.md guidance to check the saved path before concluding a source is unreadable. Page 1 (193-page document) confirms the title 'Blue Cross and Blue Shield of Alabama Standardized Source+Rx1.0 Prescription Drug List, July 2026' with footer code '4157-W AL HIM (c) Prime Therapeutics LLC 07/26', naming Prime Therapeutics LLC as the PBM providing pharmacy benefit management for Blue Cross and Blue Shield of Alabama -- matching the payer and PBM named in the task; 07/26 (July 2026) used as effectiveDate. Page 3 (printed page II) defines the four-tier legend verbatim: 'Tier 1 - primarily generics and select brands / Tier 2 - primarily preferred brands / Tier 3 - primarily non-preferred brands / Tier 4 - primarily specialty', and page 5 (printed page IV) defines the Prior Authorization / Step Therapy / Dispensing Limit dot-column system used throughout the drug tables. The 'ANTIDEPRESSANTS' section under 'Central Nervous System Drugs' (PDF pages 75-77, printed pages 67-69) was located via full-text search for each SSRI generic name and read verbatim, including a coordinate-level check of each bullet's x-position against the seven-column header (Drug Tier / Prior Authorization / Step Therapy / Dispensing Limits / ACA Preventive / Limited Distribution) to correctly distinguish which column each row's dot belongs to (the page's tight column spacing makes plain text extraction ambiguous about which UM flag a dot represents without this check). All six target SSRI molecules are listed as lower-case generics with reference brand names in parentheses, Tier 1, with a single dot confirmed (by x-position, ~x=531-540 vs the Dispensing Limits header at x=526-540) to sit in the Dispensing Limits column only -- not Prior Authorization (x=486-500) or Step Therapy (x=506-520): citalopram hydrobromide oral solution/tablet (Celexa), escitalopram oxalate oral solution/tablet (Lexapro), fluoxetine hcl capsule/solution/tablet (Prozac) at standard strengths 10-40mg, fluvoxamine maleate tablet 25-100mg, paroxetine hcl tablet (Paxil) 10-40mg, and sertraline hcl oral concentrate/tablet (Zoloft) 25-100mg. The only PA+Step-flagged item found in the SSRI-relevant text was 'FLUOXETINE DR - fluoxetine hcl cap delayed release 90 mg', Tier 3, confirmed by the same x-position check to carry dots in both the Prior Authorization and Step Therapy columns (x~486-500 and x~506-520 respectively on the right-hand sub-table) -- this is a distinct once-weekly delayed-release product line, not the standard fluoxetine capsules/tablets. Sertraline tablet was selected as the representative preferred agent (50mg, once daily) per this repo's existing ssri-oral convention across every other payer in this dataset (all six molecules are equally Tier 1 with only a dispensing-limit flag, so the source does not itself rank one above the others); the 50mg strength and once-daily sig are standard prescribing convention, not stated in the drug list itself (which lists drug name, tier, and UM flags only, no dosing guidance). No class-wide step-therapy criterion (e.g. 'PA required if 2+ concurrent agents') appears anywhere in the Antidepressants section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Blue Advantage Choice 2026 Formulary PDF (H0104_CHF26_C, formulary updated 01/20/2026, Approved Formulary ID 00026212_V10), fetched from the exact URL provided. Cover page (p.1) confirms publisher (Blue Advantage/BCBS Alabama Medicare) and revision date. The tier-legend/abbreviation key on p.10 (vi) confirms Tier 1=Preferred Generic, Tier 2=Generic, Tier 3=Preferred Brand, Tier 4=Non-Preferred, Tier 5=Specialty, and that PA/QL/ST/# are the only requirement codes used. The 'Antidepressants' subsection spans pp.25-26 of the drug list and was read in full: every oral SSRI molecule (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, fluvoxamine) appears only as generic entries -- no brand-name SSRI (Celexa/Lexapro/Prozac/Zoloft/Paxil/Luvox) is listed anywhere in the formulary, confirming no BOGL and no non-formulary/PA/step-therapy barrier exists for any SSRI molecule on this plan; every listed strength/form carries only a Quantity Limit (QL), never PA or ST. Sertraline 25/50/100 mg tablets are the only SSRI at Tier 1 with no HRM flag, selected as the representative preferred pick (citalopram, escitalopram, and fluoxetine tablets/capsules sit at the identical Tier 1 with no PA either, captured as equally-preferred alternatives). The exact QL figures for every strength were read verbatim from the table, not inferred. The only inferred element is the sig/plainSig phrasing (standard once-daily oral dosing for the 50 mg strength), which is clinical convention rather than formulary-stated text.
